# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Define a clipper circuit. With the help of a transfer characteristic, explain the working of an unbiased positive clipper.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **clipper circuit** is a wave-shaping network that limits the amplitude of a signal by removing (clipping) the portion of the waveform that exceeds a predetermined reference level. The remaining part is transmitted without distortion.

For an **unbiased positive series clipper**, the diode is placed in series with the load:
- **When $V_{in} < +V_F$ (≈ 0.7 V):** The diode is reverse-biased (OFF); it acts as an open circuit, so no current flows through $R_L$, and **$V_{out} = 0$ V**.
- **When $V_{in} \geq +V_F$:** The diode becomes forward-biased (ON) and behaves like a 0.7 V battery opposing the input. Therefore, **$V_{out} = V_{in} - 0.7$ V**.

The transfer characteristic has a "knee" at $V_{in} = +0.7\ \text{V}$, below which the output is flat at zero, and above which it follows the input with a 0.7 V offset. The positive half-cycle appears trimmed at its lower portion.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between a clipper and a clamper. Why is the time constant RC required to be much greater than the input period T in clamper circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Clipper | Clamper |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Removes a portion of the waveform | Shifts the entire waveform by a DC level |
| Waveform shape | Distorted (trimmed) | Preserved |
| Element added | Diode + resistor (no capacitor) | Diode + capacitor |
| Key formula | $V_{out} = V_{in} \pm V_F$ (when ON) | $V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t) \pm V_m$ |

**Why $\tau = RC \gg T$:**

The capacitor in a clamper charges to the peak input voltage $V_m$ during one half-cycle and must *retain* this charge during the next half-cycle. If $\tau \gg T$, the capacitor discharges very slowly through $R$, so the voltage droop across one cycle is negligible:

$$\Delta V_C \approx V_C \cdot \frac{T}{2RC} \approx V_C \cdot \frac{T}{2\tau}$$

A small $\Delta V_C$ ensures the clamping level stays stable and the output waveform exhibits minimal **"tilt"**. If $\tau \ll T$, the capacitor would discharge rapidly, the clamp level would collapse, and the output would resemble the original AC-coupled signal. Thus, $RC$ must typically be **at least 10×T** for a faithful clamping action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Drawing the transfer curve on the wrong axis** (V_in on Y instead of X) — leads to zero marks for the plot.
> - **Forgetting the diode forward drop $V_F = 0.7\ \text{V}$** in numerical calculations — costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Confusing "positive clipper" with "positive clamper"** in the question — read carefully.
> - **Not mentioning the steady-state condition $\tau \gg T$** when explaining clippers' RC selection — a 2-mark deduction.
> - **Drawing waveforms without proper axis labels and amplitude/period values** — KTU strictly penalizes unlabeled diagrams.

---

### QUESTION A — `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**Part (a)** — **(7 Marks)**

*Design and analyze the transfer characteristic of a biased positive clipper with reference voltage $V_{REF} = +4\ \text{V}$, silicon diode ($V_F = 0.7\ \text{V}$), input $V_{in} = 10\sin(\omega t)$, and load $R_L = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Sketch the input and output waveforms.*

**Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit operation.** A DC source of $+4\ \text{V}$ is placed in series with the diode, opposing its conduction. The diode will conduct only when the input is positive enough to overcome both the bias and the diode drop:

$$V_{in} \geq V_{REF} + V_F = 4 + 0.7 = 4.7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Piecewise output equation.**

$$
V_{out} = 
\begin{cases}
0\ \text{V}, & V_{in} < 4.7\ \text{V} \\
V_{in} - 4.7\ \text{V}, & V_{in} \geq 4.7\ \text{V}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical samples.**

| $V_{in}$ (V) | Region | $V_{out}$ (V) |
|---|---|---|
| +10 (peak) | ON | 10 − 4.7 = **+5.3** |
| +6 | ON | 6 − 4.7 = **+1.3** |
| +4.7 (knee) | ON/OFF boundary | **0** |
| 0 | OFF | **0** |
| −5 | OFF | **0** |
| −10 (trough) | OFF | **0** |

**Step 4 — Sketch the waveforms.** *Input:* Standard sine of amplitude 10 V. *Output:* Flat at 0 V for all of the negative half-cycle and the lower positive portion. The output rises linearly only when input exceeds 4.7 V, peaking at +5.3 V.

```
   V_in (V)
     +10 ┤      ╭──╮              ╭──╮
         │    ╭╯    ╰╮          ╭╯    ╰╮
       0 ┼──╯────────╰────────╯──────────╰──
         │  ╭                ╭
     -10 ┤  ╰────────────────╯

   V_out (V)
     +5.3┤         ╱╲                ╱╲
         │       ╱    ╲            ╱    ╲
       0 ┼──────╱──────╲──────────╱──────╲──
         │                  (flat at 0)
     -5.3┤
```

**Part (b)** — **(7 Marks)**

*For the same biased positive clipper, derive the slope of the output line in the conduction region and compute the output when $V_{in} = +8\ \text{V}$. Comment on the role of $R_L$ if it were doubled.*

**Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Slope of transfer curve in ON region.**
The transfer equation in the ON region is linear: $V_{out} = V_{in} - 4.7$.

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = 1$$

Hence the transfer curve in the conduction region is a **45° line** parallel to $V_{in}$, shifted down by 4.7 V. The ideal-diode assumption makes this slope exactly unity.

**Step 2 — Output at $V_{in} = +8\ \text{V}$.**

$$V_{out} = 8 - 4.7 = +3.3\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Practical slope (non-ideal diode).**
If the diode has ON resistance $r_d = 25\ \Omega$ and $R_L = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$\text{Slope}_{practical} = \frac{R_L}{R_L + r_d} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 25} \approx 0.976$$

So output becomes: $V_{out} = 0.976 \cdot (V_{in} - 4.7)$.

**Step 4 — Effect of doubling $R_L$ to 2 kΩ.**

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 25} \approx 0.988 \quad (\text{closer to unity})$$

A larger $R_L$ minimizes the loading effect of the diode's internal resistance, giving a more ideal transfer curve. However, an excessively large $R_L$ will reduce the output current capability and may make the circuit more susceptible to noise pick-up.

---

### QUESTION B — `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a)** — **(7 Marks)**

*Explain the working of a positive clamper circuit with a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveform sketch. Derive the expression for the steady-state output voltage if the input is $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.*

**Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit.** A positive clamper consists of a capacitor $C$ in series with the input, followed by a diode $D$ (cathode at output, anode at ground) in parallel with the load resistor $R_L$.

**Step 2 — Working — First negative half-cycle.**
When $V_{in}$ goes negative, the diode becomes forward-biased (anode grounded, cathode at negative input → anode is more positive). The diode conducts heavily, charging the capacitor rapidly (small diode ON resistance, small RC) toward $V_m$ with the **left plate positive** and the **right plate (output side) at 0 V** (clamped).

**Step 3 — Working — Positive half-cycle.**
Now $V_{in}$ rises positively. The diode is reverse-biased and OFF. The capacitor retains charge $V_C = V_m$ (assuming $RC \gg T$). By KVL:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t) + V_C = V_m \sin(\omega t) + V_m$$

**Step 4 — Final steady-state expression.**

$$
\boxed{V_{out}(t) = V_m\bigl(1 + \sin(\omega t)\bigr)}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify clamping.**

- Negative input peak ($\omega t = -\pi/2$): $V_{out} = V_m(-1 + 1) = 0\ \text{V}$ ✓ (clamped)
- Positive input peak ($\omega t = +\pi/2$): $V_{out} = V_m(+1 + 1) = 2V_m$ ✓ (doubled)
- Peak-to-peak: $2V_m$ — *unchanged from input* ✓ (shape preserved)

**Waveforms:**

```
   V_in:                          V_out:
     +Vm┤   ╱╲                       +2Vm┤      ╱╲
        │ ╱    ╲                        │    ╱    ╲
       0┼╱──────╲──                  0 ┼──╱────────╲──
        │╲      ╱                       │╱          ╲
     -Vm┤ ╲____╱                     -Vm┤ (no excursion, clamped)
        (sine)                       (sine shifted up by Vm)
```

**Part (b)** — **(7 Marks)**

*For a positive clamper using $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$, $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, input frequency $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$, input amplitude $V_m = 5\ \text{V}$. Compute (i) time constant, (ii) settling time, (iii) output at $t = T/4$, and (iv) explain what happens if $R_L$ is reduced to 1 kΩ.*

**Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Time constant.**

$$\tau = R_L \cdot C = (10 \times 10^3) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Input period.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

Check clamping condition: $\tau / T = 10 \gg 1$ → ✓ good clamper.

**Step 3 — Settling time.**

$$t_s = 5\tau = 5 \times 10\ \text{ms} = 50\ \text{ms} \quad (\approx 50\ \text{cycles})$$

**Step 4 — Output at $t = T/4 = 0.25\ \text{ms}$ (first quarter of period).**

Using the steady-state expression (after $t > t_s$):

$$V_{out}(T/4) = V_m \bigl(1 + \sin(\omega \cdot T/4)\bigr) = 5\bigl(1 + \sin(\pi/2)\bigr) = 5 \cdot 2 = +10\ \text{V} = 2V_m$$

For the very first cycle (transient), the output is not yet fully shifted; capacitor is still charging. Approximately:

$$V_{out,first\ cycle}(T/4) \approx 5(1 + 1) - 5(1 - e^{-0.25\ \text{ms}/10\ \text{ms}}) \approx +9.88\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Effect of reducing $R_L$ to 1 kΩ.**

New time constant: $\tau' = 1\ \text{k}\Omega \cdot 1\ \mu\text{F} = 1\ \text{ms} = T$.

Now $\tau' / T = 1$, which violates the clamping condition. The capacitor discharges substantially between cycles, producing noticeable **tilt** in the output waveform:

$$\Delta V_C \approx V_m \cdot \frac{T}{2\tau'} = 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 2.5\ \text{V droop per half cycle}$$

The waveform becomes a **decaying staircase** rather than a clean shifted sine. The clamp action fails for fast-changing signals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- **Clipper** = *cuts* the waveform at a threshold; uses **diode + resistor**.
- **Clamper** = *shifts* the waveform by a DC level; uses **diode + capacitor**.
- **Ideal diode:** $V_F = 0$ in textbooks; **practical silicon diode:** $V_F = 0.7\ \text{V}$.
- **Series clipper** with diode pointing **upward** (anode to input) = **positive clipper**; reversed diode = **negative clipper**.
- **Shunt clipper** has the diode across the load; opposite behavior to series.
- **Transfer characteristic** is always plotted as $V_{out}$ on **Y-axis** vs $V_{in}$ on **X-axis**.
- **Biased clipper threshold** = $V_{REF} \pm V_F$, depending on diode orientation and bias polarity.
- **Zener clipper** clips at $\pm(V_Z + V_F)$ — used for symmetrical limiting.
- **Positive clamper:** negative peak of output sits at **0 V**; output = $V_{in} + V_m$.
- **Negative clamper:** positive peak of output sits at **0 V**; output = $V_{in} - V_m$.
- **Clamper requirement:** $RC \geq 10T$ for stable clamping; $\tau = RC$ is the time constant.
- **Settling time** = $5\tau$ (≈ 98% of final value reached).
- **Tilt** in clamper output = $\Delta V \approx V_C \cdot (T / 2RC)$ — visible at CRO if $\tau$ too small.
- **First cycle transient** in a clamper shows charging spike; steady state reached after ~5τ.
- **Diode ON state current** in clipper: $I_D = (V_{in} - V_F) / R_L$.
- **Reverse breakdown** of diode must never be exceeded in clipper/clamp design.
- **Lab tip:** Always measure $V_{out}$ and $V_{in}$ simultaneously on dual-trace CRO; capture at least 5 cycles to confirm steady state.
- **XY mode of CRO** is used to plot the **transfer characteristic** by feeding $V_{in}$ to X-input and $V_{out}$ to Y-input.
- **KTU viva favorites:** *Why use silicon diode instead of germanium?* (lower reverse leakage, higher $V_F$ gives predictable clipping); *Why is a clamper called a DC restorer?* (it restores the DC component lost during AC coupling).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CE Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CE Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier:** A BJT amplifier configuration in which the **emitter terminal is common to both the input (base–emitter) port and the output (collector–emitter) port**. The CE stage is the most widely used small-signal voltage amplifier because it provides **moderate input impedance, moderate output impedance, a high voltage gain (typically 10–500), and a 180° phase inversion** between input and output.

For the lab experiment, the task is twofold:
1. **Design** the bias network, collector resistor, and emitter resistor such that the **mid-band voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$** equals a specified value (commonly 10, 20, 50, or 100).
2. **Plot the frequency response**, i.e., the Bode magnitude plot of $\vert A_v(f) \vert$ (in dB) versus signal frequency $f$ (on a log scale), and identify the **lower cutoff $f_L$**, **upper cutoff $f_H$**, and **bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Megaphone With a Volume Knob"

Imagine a public-address (PA) system in a college auditorium:
- The **microphone** = the base of the transistor (small voice signal enters here).
- The **amplifier box** = the BJT itself, which takes energy from the DC power supply ($V_{CC}$) and "shapes" it using the small input voice.
- The **loudspeaker** = the load $R_C$ in the collector, which produces a much louder version of the same sound.
- The **volume knob** = the value of $R_C$. Turning the knob up (increasing $R_C$) makes the output louder, but only up to a point — if the gain is too high, the speaker distorts (clipping) and the system becomes unstable (oscillation).
- The **frequency response curve** = a graph showing *how loud the speaker is* for *pitches of different frequencies*. Human ears (or in our case, an oscilloscope/Bode plotter) tell us that very low frequencies (bass rumble) and very high frequencies (whistles) are not amplified as well as the mid-band (speech range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The CE amplifier is the *workhorse* of analog electronics. Mastering its design equations and frequency response is essential because the same topology is embedded in op-amp gain stages, RF front-ends, and audio pre-amplifiers. KTU examiners frequently test the mid-band gain formula, the Miller effect, and the cutoff-frequency expressions.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Typical Range | Bold Constant |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Thermal voltage | $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ (at 300 K) | $V_T = kT/q$ |
| Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | 0.1 – 500 mA/V |
| Current gain | $\beta$ (or $h_{fe}$) | 50 – 300 |
| Mid-band voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | 10 – 500 | dimensionless |
| Bandwidth (audio CE amp) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | Hz |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology and Biasing

The **self-bias / voltage-divider bias CE amplifier** is used in the lab because it provides excellent $Q$-point stability against $\beta$ and temperature variations.

**Component roles:**
- $R_1, R_2$ — Base bias divider, sets $V_{BB} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- $R_C$ — Collector load; converts collector current swing to output voltage.
- $R_E$ — Emitter resistor; provides DC negative feedback and stabilizes $I_C$.
- $C_{in}, C_{out}$ — Coupling capacitors; block DC, pass AC.
- $C_E$ — Emitter bypass capacitor; shorts $R_E$ at mid-band to maximize gain.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step DC Analysis (Q-Point Design)

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the base–emitter loop:
$$V_{BB} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$

$$I_E = \frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE}}{R_E}$$

**Step 2:** Collector current:
$$I_C \approx I_E = \frac{V_{BB} - 0.7}{R_E}$$

**Step 3:** Collector–emitter voltage:
$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** For maximum symmetrical swing, choose the $Q$-point at the **middle of the DC load line**, i.e., $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$ and $I_C \approx I_{C(sat)}/2$.

**Step 4:** Verify the **stability condition** (so $\beta$ variations do not shift $Q$):
$$\beta R_E \geq 10 R_2$$

## 2.3 AC (Small-Signal) Analysis — Mid-Band Region

In the mid-band region, all coupling and bypass capacitors act as **AC short circuits**, and the internal transistor capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) are **AC open circuits**. The simplified hybrid-$\pi$ model reduces to:
- $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$
- A controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$
- Collector load = $R_C \parallel R_L$ (call this $R_C'$)

**Voltage gain (mid-band):**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$$

where $r_e = 1/g_m = V_T/I_E \approx 26\ \text{mV}/I_E$ is the intrinsic emitter resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** — a hallmark of the CE stage. This is why the output waveform is flipped vertically relative to the input on the CRO.

## 2.4 Designing for a Specific Voltage Gain

Given a target mid-band gain $\vert A_v \vert$:

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \vert A_v \vert \cdot r_e = \vert A_v \vert \cdot \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E}$$

If the load is unknown or absent, $R_C \approx \vert A_v \vert \cdot r_e$. Always keep $R_C \ll \beta R_E$ to retain bias stability.

## 2.5 Frequency Response Regions

| Region | Frequency Range | Cause of Roll-off | Slope |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|-------|
| **Low-frequency** | $f < f_L$ | Coupling ($C_{in}, C_{out}$) and bypass ($C_E$) capacitors | $-20$ dB/decade per pole |
| **Mid-band** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | All capacitors "ideal"; gain is flat = $\vert A_{v,mid} \vert$ | 0 dB/decade |
| **High-frequency** | $f > f_H$ | Internal BJT capacitances $C_\pi$ ($C_{be}$), $C_\mu$ ($C_{bc}$); **Miller effect** dominates | $-20$ dB/decade (or $-40$ dB/dec if poles coincide) |

## 2.6 Lower Cutoff Frequency

The lower cutoff is set by the **input RC**, **output RC**, and **emitter bypass RC** networks. Each contributes a pole:

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi) C_{in}}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}$$

$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E} \quad \text{(often the dominant pole)}$$

Overall (when poles are well-separated, factor of $\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ applies):

$$f_L \approx 1.1 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LE}^2}$$

## 2.7 Upper Cutoff Frequency — Miller's Theorem

At high frequencies, $C_\mu$ (collector–base capacitance, typically 2–10 pF) bridges input and output. **Miller's Theorem** reflects it to the input side:

$$C_{M} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

Total input capacitance:
$$C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_M$$

**Miller input time constant** (R seen by $C_{in,tot}$):
$$R_{th} = (R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$$

$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} (C_\pi + C_M)}}$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|--------|---------|---------|-------|
| $V_T$ | $kT/q \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ | Thermal voltage | V |
| $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ | Transconductance | A/V or S |
| $r_e$ | $V_T / I_E$ | Intrinsic emitter resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $r_\pi$ | $\beta / g_m = \beta r_e$ | Base–emitter input resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Mid-band voltage gain | — |
| $A_v$ (alt) | $-(R_C \parallel R_L)/r_e$ | Mid-band voltage gain | — |
| $A_v$ (dB) | $20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Gain in decibels | dB |
| $f_L$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | Lower cutoff | Hz |
| $f_H$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}(C_\pi + C_M)}$ | Upper cutoff | Hz |
| $C_M$ | $C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Miller capacitance | F |
| $BW$ | $f_H - f_L$ | Bandwidth | Hz |
| $GBP$ | $\vert A_v \mid \cdot BW$ | Gain–bandwidth product | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Gain–Bandwidth Product $GBP = \vert A_v \mid \cdot BW$ is **approximately constant** for a given transistor. This is the design trade-off in CE amplifiers — *higher voltage gain ⇒ smaller bandwidth* (e.g., $GBP \approx 100$ MHz for BC547, $GBP \approx 300$ MHz for 2N2222).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — CE Amplifier Design for $\vert A_v \vert = 50$

**Specifications given by the KTU lab manual:**

- $V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}$
- $I_C = 2\ \text{mA}$
- Transistor: BC547 ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$)
- Load: $R_L = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ (optional)
- Target mid-band voltage gain: $\vert A_v \vert = 50$

### Step 1 — Compute the intrinsic emitter resistance $r_e$

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 13\ \Omega$$

### Step 2 — Required effective collector load

$$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = \vert A_v \vert \cdot r_e = 50 \times 13 = 650\ \Omega$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $R_C$

$$\frac{1}{R_C} = \frac{1}{R_C'} - \frac{1}{R_L} = \frac{1}{650} - \frac{1}{4700}$$

$$R_C = \frac{650 \times 4700}{4700 - 650} = \frac{3{,}055{,}000}{4050} \approx 754\ \Omega$$

**Choose standard value:** $R_C = \mathbf{750\ \Omega}$ (E12 series, ±5%).

### Step 4 — Choose $R_E$ for bias stability (use rule $R_E \approx 0.2\,V_{CC}/I_C$)

$$R_E = \frac{0.2 \times 12}{2\ \text{mA}} = \frac{2.4}{0.002} = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Choose standard value:** $R_E = \mathbf{1.2\ k\Omega}$.

### Step 5 — Choose $V_{BB}$ and the divider

For maximum swing, $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2 = 6\ \text{V}$. Therefore:

$$V_E = I_E R_E = 2\ \text{mA} \times 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega = 2.4\ \text{V}$$

$$V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 2.4 + 0.7 = 3.1\ \text{V}$$

Assuming the divider current $I_D = 10\,I_B = 10\,I_C/\beta = 10 \times 2/100 = 0.2\ \text{mA}$:

$$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_D} = \frac{12}{0.2\ \text{mA}} = 60\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \frac{V_B}{I_D} = \frac{3.1}{0.2\ \text{mA}} = 15.5\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{15\ k\Omega}$$

$$R_1 = 60 - 15.5 = 44.5\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{47\ k\Omega}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the actual $A_v$ with chosen $R_C = 750\ \Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$

$$R_C' = \frac{750 \times 4700}{750 + 4700} = \frac{3{,}525{,}000}{5450} = 647\ \Omega$$

$$A_v = -\frac{647}{13} \approx -49.7 \;\;(\text{very close to target} = -50)$$

Gain in dB: $20 \log_{10}(49.7) \approx \mathbf{33.9\ dB}$.

### Step 7 — Compute lower cutoff frequencies

Assume $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\ \mu\text{F}$, source resistance $R_S = 600\ \Omega$.

$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 47\text{k} \parallel 15\text{k} \parallel (100 \times 13) = 47\text{k} \parallel 15\text{k} \parallel 1.3\text{k} \approx 1.18\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 1180)(10 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 8.95\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (750 + 4700)(10 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 2.92\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1200 \parallel 13)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 12.86 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 123.7\ \text{Hz}$$

**Dominant lower pole:** $f_L \approx 1.1 \sqrt{8.95^2 + 2.92^2 + 123.7^2} \approx \mathbf{136\ Hz}$.

### Step 8 — Compute upper cutoff frequency (Miller's Theorem)

BC547 typical: $C_\pi \approx 10\ \text{pF}$ at $I_C = 2\ \text{mA}$, $C_\mu \approx 2\ \text{pF}$.

$$C_M = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 2\ \text{pF} \times (1 + 49.7) = 101.4\ \text{pF}$$

$$C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_M = 10 + 101.4 = 111.4\ \text{pF}$$

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_{in} = 600 \parallel 1180 = 397\ \Omega$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 397 \times 111.4 \times 10^{-12}} \approx \mathbf{3.6\ MHz}$$

### Step 9 — Bandwidth and Gain–Bandwidth Product

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx 3.6\ \text{MHz} - 136\ \text{Hz} \approx \mathbf{3.6\ MHz}$$

$$GBP = \vert A_v \vert \times BW = 50 \times 3.6\ \text{MHz} = \mathbf{180\ MHz}$$

## 3.2 Summary Table of Designed Values

| Component | Calculated | Standard Value Used | Purpose |
|-----------|------------|---------------------|---------|
| $R_C$ | 754 $\Omega$ | 750 $\Omega$ | Sets mid-band gain |
| $R_E$ | 1.2 k$\Omega$ | 1.2 k$\Omega$ | Bias stability |
| $R_1$ | 44.5 k$\Omega$ | 47 k$\Omega$ | Upper divider |
| $R_2$ | 15.5 k$\Omega$ | 15 k$\Omega$ | Lower divider |
| $C_{in}$ | — | 10 $\mu$F | Input coupling |
| $C_{out}$ | — | 10 $\mu$F | Output coupling |
| $C_E$ | — | 100 $\mu$F | Emitter bypass |
| $\vert A_v \vert$ | 50 | 49.7 (achieved) | — |
| $f_L$ | 136 Hz | — | Lower cutoff |
| $f_H$ | 3.6 MHz | — | Upper cutoff |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CE Amplifier Circuit — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Vcc = 12 V DC Supply"] --> nodeR1["R1 = 47 kohm"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeN1["Base Node VB"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeR2["R2 = 15 kohm"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeGND["Ground GND"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeCin["Cin = 10 uF Coupling"]
    nodeCin --> nodeVin["AC Input Vi from Function Generator"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeBJT["BJT BC547 Q1 Emitter Common"]
    nodeBJT --> nodeRC["RC = 750 ohm"]
    nodeRC --> nodeVCC2["Vcc Rail"]
    nodeBJT --> nodeCout["Cout = 10 uF Coupling"]
    nodeCout --> nodeRL["RL = 4.7 kohm Load"]
    nodeRL --> nodeVout["AC Output Vo to CRO"]
    nodeBJT --> nodeRE["RE = 1.2 kohm"]
    nodeRE --> nodeCE["CE = 100 uF Bypass"]
    nodeCE --> nodeGND2["Ground GND"]
    nodeRE --> nodeGND2
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response — Bode Magnitude Plot (ASCII Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["20 Hz - fL roll-off -20 dB/dec"] --> B["136 Hz = fL Corner"]
    B --> C["136 Hz to 3.6 MHz FLAT Mid-Band 33.9 dB"]
    C --> D["3.6 MHz = fH Corner"]
    D --> E["Above 3.6 MHz roll-off -20 dB/dec"]
```

**Numerical sketch of the response:**

| Frequency $f$ | $\vert A_v \vert$ | Gain in dB |
|---------------|------------------|------------|
| 20 Hz | 7.0 | 16.9 dB |
| 100 Hz | 32 | 30.1 dB |
| **136 Hz ($f_L$)** | **35.1** | **30.9 dB** |
| 1 kHz | 49.7 | 33.9 dB |
| 100 kHz | 49.6 | 33.9 dB |
| 1 MHz | 47 | 33.4 dB |
| **3.6 MHz ($f_H$)** | **35.1** | **30.9 dB** |
| 10 MHz | 13 | 22.3 dB |

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nV1["Vi Source"] --> nA["Input Coupling Cin and Rin"]
    nA --> nB["BJT Hybrid Pi Model gm Vbe and rpi"]
    nB --> nC["Output Coupling Cout and RC parallel RL"]
    nC --> nV2["Vo to CRO"]
    nB -.-> nD["Miller Capacitance CM = Cmu times 1 + Av"]
    nD -.-> nA
    nB -.-> nE["Emitter Bypass CE and RE"]
    nE -.-> nF["Low Frequency Pole fLE"]
```

## 4.4 CRO Observations (Phase Inversion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When you display both input (CH1) and output (CH2) on a **dual-trace oscilloscope**, the output sinusoid will be **180° out of phase** with the input. The peak of $v_o$ occurs at the **trough** of $v_i$. This phase inversion is a defining characteristic of the CE stage and is a direct consequence of the negative sign in $A_v = -g_m R_C'$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the emitter resistor $R_E$ bypassed by a capacitor $C_E$ in a CE amplifier? What happens to the voltage gain if $C_E$ is removed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) $R_E$ is required for DC negative feedback to stabilize the $Q$-point against $\beta$ and temperature variations. However, the same $R_E$ would also provide AC negative feedback if left unbypassed, which would *reduce* the AC voltage gain.
- (1 mark) $C_E$ acts as an **AC short circuit** at mid-band frequencies, so the AC emitter is effectively grounded, eliminating AC degeneration and allowing maximum gain.
- (1 mark) If $C_E$ is removed, the gain drops to $A_v \approx -R_C'/r_e + R_E \approx -R_C'/(r_e + R_E) \approx -R_C'/R_E$, becoming very small (often $<1$). For example, with $R_C' = 650\ \Omega$ and $R_E = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\vert A_v \vert$ collapses from 50 to $\approx 0.54$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Miller effect. How does it limit the high-frequency response of a CE amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The Miller effect refers to the multiplication of the feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ (collector–base) as seen at the input: $C_M = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.
- (1 mark) This large effective capacitance, in parallel with $C_\pi$, forms a low-pass RC time constant with the source resistance, producing the upper cutoff $f_H = 1/[2\pi R_{th}(C_\pi + C_M)]$.
- (1 mark) Higher mid-band gain $\Rightarrow$ larger $C_M \Rightarrow$ lower $f_H \Rightarrow$ reduced bandwidth. This is the fundamental gain–bandwidth trade-off.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in $A_v = -g_m R_C'$, losing 1 mark on the phase-inversion question.
> - Many confuse the **stability condition** ($\beta R_E \ge 10 R_2$) with the **divider current rule** ($I_D = 10 I_B$) — both are required, but for different reasons.
> - In Miller's theorem, students often write $C_M = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ but then **fail to take the absolute value** when $A_v$ is negative, giving a wrong (smaller) $C_M$.
> - In Bode plots, examiners expect **$-20$ dB/decade** slope to be explicitly stated, not merely sketched.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Design a voltage-divider biased CE amplifier using a silicon BJT ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$) with $V_{CC} = 15\ \text{V}$ and $I_C = 1\ \text{mA}$ to achieve a mid-band voltage gain of $\vert A_v \vert = 40$. Load $R_L = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$. Specify $R_C, R_E, R_1, R_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the designed amplifier, with $C_{in} = C_{out} = 4.7\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 47\ \mu\text{F}$, $C_\pi = 8\ \text{pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\ \text{pF}$, and $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, calculate the lower and upper cutoff frequencies. Sketch the frequency response. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Intrinsic emitter resistance:**
$$r_e = V_T / I_E = 26\ \text{mV} / 1\ \text{mA} = 26\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Required effective collector load:**
$$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = \vert A_v \vert \cdot r_e = 40 \times 26 = 1040\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_C$:**
$$R_C = \frac{R_C' R_L}{R_L - R_C'} = \frac{1040 \times 5000}{5000 - 1040} = \frac{5{,}200{,}000}{3960} \approx 1313\ \Omega$$

Choose $R_C = \mathbf{1.3\ k\Omega}$ (standard E12).

**Step 4 — Choose $R_E \approx 0.2 V_{CC}/I_C$:**
$$R_E = \frac{0.2 \times 15}{1\ \text{mA}} = 3\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_E = \mathbf{3\ k\Omega}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_B, V_E$:**
$$V_E = 1\ \text{mA} \times 3\ \text{k}\Omega = 3\ \text{V}, \quad V_B = 3 + 0.7 = 3.7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Divider design with $I_D = 10 I_B = 10 \times 1/100 = 0.1\ \text{mA}$:**
$$R_1 + R_2 = 15/0.1\text{mA} = 150\ \text{k}\Omega$$
$$R_2 = 3.7/0.1\text{mA} = 37\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{39\ k\Omega}$$
$$R_1 = 150 - 37 = 113\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{120\ k\Omega}$$

**Verification of stability:** $\beta R_E = 100 \times 3000 = 300{,}000$; $10 R_2 = 380{,}000$. Condition **not quite satisfied** — increase $R_E$ to $3.9\ \text{k}\Omega$ or reduce $R_2$ to $27\ \text{k}\Omega$. With $R_2 = 27\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 123\ \text{k}\Omega \approx \mathbf{120\ k}\Omega$. Stability now holds.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Input resistance seen by $C_{in}$:**
$$R_{in} = 120\text{k} \parallel 27\text{k} \parallel (\beta r_e) = 120\text{k} \parallel 27\text{k} \parallel 2600 \approx 2.45\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000 + 2450)(4.7 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 9.8\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1300 + 5000)(4.7 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 5.4\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3000 \parallel 26)(47 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 139\ \text{Hz}$$

**Overall $f_L$:**
$$f_L \approx 1.1 \sqrt{9.8^2 + 5.4^2 + 139^2} \approx 1.1 \times 139.4 \approx \mathbf{153\ Hz}$$

**Upper cutoff using Miller's theorem:**

Achieved $A_v = -(R_C' / r_e) = -(1040/26) = -40$.

$$C_M = 2\ \text{pF} \times (1 + 40) = 82\ \text{pF}$$

$$C_{in,tot} = 8 + 82 = 90\ \text{pF}$$

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_{in} = 1000 \parallel 2450 = 711\ \Omega$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 711 \times 90 \times 10^{-12}} \approx \mathbf{2.49\ MHz}$$

**Sketch:** flat at $20\log_{10}(40) = 32\ \text{dB}$ from $\sim 153\ \text{Hz}$ to $\sim 2.5\ \text{MHz}$; $-20$ dB/decade slopes on both sides.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent circuit how the **Miller effect** reduces the bandwidth of a CE amplifier. Derive the expression for the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CE amplifier has $g_m = 50\ \text{mA/V}$, $r_\pi = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 20\ \text{pF}$, $C_\mu = 3\ \text{pF}$, $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate (i) the mid-band gain in dB, (ii) the Miller capacitance, and (iii) the upper cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Hybrid-$\pi$ model explanation:**
The high-frequency model of a BJT includes $C_\pi$ (base–emitter diffusion + depletion capacitance) and $C_\mu$ (feedback capacitance from collector to base). $C_\mu$ bridges the input and output nodes.

**Miller's Theorem derivation:**
The voltage across $C_\mu$ is $V_\mu = V_\pi - V_o = V_\pi - A_v V_\pi = V_\pi(1 - A_v)$.

The current through $C_\mu$ is $I_\mu = j\omega C_\mu V_\mu = j\omega C_\mu (1 - A_v) V_\pi$.

From the input side, an equivalent capacitance drawing the same current $I_\mu$ at voltage $V_\pi$ is:
$$C_M = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \quad (\text{since } A_v < 0)$$

**Upper cutoff derivation:**

Total input capacitance = $C_\pi + C_M$. Thevenin resistance seen by this capacitance:
$$R_{th} = (R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$$

This forms a low-pass RC filter:
$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} (C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert))}}$$

Higher $\vert A_v \vert$ ⇒ larger $C_M$ ⇒ lower $f_H$ ⇒ smaller bandwidth — the gain–bandwidth trade-off.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**(i) Mid-band gain:**
$$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = 4\text{k} \parallel 4\text{k} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$$
$$A_v = -g_m R_C' = -(50 \times 10^{-3})(2000) = -100$$
$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(100) = \mathbf{40\ dB}$$

**(ii) Miller capacitance:**
$$C_M = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 3\ \text{pF} \times 101 = \mathbf{303\ pF}$$

**(iii) Upper cutoff:**
$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_\pi = 1000 \parallel 2000 = 667\ \Omega$$
$$C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_M = 20 + 303 = 323\ \text{pF}$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 667 \times 323 \times 10^{-12}} \approx \mathbf{740\ kHz}$$

**Gain–bandwidth product check:** $GBP = 100 \times 740\ \text{kHz} = 74\ \text{MHz}$ (reasonable for a small-signal BJT).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Mid-band voltage gain** of a CE amplifier: $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L) = -(R_C \parallel R_L)/r_e$. The **negative sign** ⇒ **180° phase inversion**.
- [ ] **Design for specific gain:** first choose $I_C$ (sets $r_e$), then $R_C \parallel R_L = \vert A_v \vert \cdot r_e$, then $R_E \approx 0.2 V_{CC}/I_C$ for bias stability, then $R_1, R_2$ such that $V_B = V_E + 0.7\ \text{V}$.
- [ ] **Stability condition:** $\beta R_E \ge 10 R_2$ — this is essential for $\beta$-independent biasing.
- [ ] **Lower cutoff $f_L$** is set by coupling and bypass capacitors. The **emitter-bypass network** $R_E \parallel r_e$ with $C_E$ is usually the **dominant lower pole**.
- [ ] **Upper cutoff $f_H$** is set by internal BJT capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$. $C_\mu$ is multiplied by Miller's theorem: $C_M = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.
- [ ] **Gain–Bandwidth Product** $GBP = \vert A_v \vert \cdot BW$ is **approximately constant** for a given transistor. High gain ⇒ low bandwidth.
- [ ] **Frequency response plot** has three regions: low-frequency roll-off ($-20$ dB/dec/pole), mid-band (flat at $20\log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ dB), and high-frequency roll-off ($-20$ dB/dec).
- [ ] **CRO observation:** output sinusoid is **180° phase-shifted** from the input — a defining signature of the CE stage.
- [ ] **Coupling capacitors** ($C_{in}, C_{out}$) block DC and pass AC. **Bypass capacitor** $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ at mid-band, increasing gain.
- [ ] **Higher $I_C$** increases $g_m$ and reduces $r_e$, but **reduces $r_\pi$** and lowers input impedance — yet another design trade-off.
- [ ] **Lab measurement tip:** Use a function generator with low output impedance (e.g., 50 $\Omega$) and a CRO in AC coupling mode. Sweep the frequency from 10 Hz to 10 MHz in decade steps, and tabulate $v_{in,pp}$ and $v_{out,pp}$ to compute $\vert A_v \vert$ in dB.
- [ ] **Bandwidth definition** for the lab record: $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ when $f_H \gg f_L$.
- [ ] **KTU practical marking scheme (typical):** Circuit diagram (3 marks), design calculations (4 marks), waveform/frequency response plot (4 marks), result & inference (3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CS MOSFET Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CS MOSFET Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a small-signal field-effect transistor amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied between the **gate and source** terminals, while the amplified output is taken from the **drain terminal** with respect to the source. The source terminal is therefore *common* to both the input loop and the output loop, hence the name.

In the context of the **PCECL307 – Analog Circuits Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module Experiment 2 specifically requires the student to:

1. **Design** a CS MOSFET amplifier (typically using a depletion-mode or enhancement-mode N-channel MOSFET such as the BSS84 / 2N7000 / BF245 family or the laboratory-standard CD4007 array) for a **specified mid-band voltage gain magnitude** $|A_v|$.
2. **Plot the frequency response** — i.e., the magnitude $|A_v(j\omega)|$ in dB versus frequency $f$ on a log scale — and identify the **lower cutoff frequency** $f_L$ and the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$, thereby determining the **bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> *"Design a Common-Source MOSFET amplifier for a required voltage gain. Analyze its frequency response by plotting the gain (in dB) versus frequency on a semi-log graph and determine the lower and upper 3-dB cutoff frequencies."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water-tap** system:

- The **gate** of the MOSFET is like the **handle of the tap** — a tiny rotation (a small voltage change $v_{gs}$) controls a huge flow of water (the drain current $i_d$).
- The **drain** is like the **outlet pipe** where the high-pressure water emerges — this corresponds to the amplified output voltage $v_{ds}$.
- The **source** is the **common ground point** where both the input pressure-sensor and the output pipe are referenced — the *common* terminal.

> **Key Insight:** A CS amplifier is a **voltage-controlled current source (VCCS)** at its core. The small AC voltage at the gate modulates a larger current through the drain, which when passed through a load resistor $R_D$, produces a large output voltage — this is the essence of *voltage amplification*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | **1 mA/V to 30 mA/V** |
| Threshold voltage | $V_{th}$ | **0.7 V to 4 V** |
| Load resistance | $R_D$ | **1 kΩ to 10 kΩ** |
| Source resistance | $R_S$ | **0 (bypassed) to 1 kΩ** |
| Mid-band voltage gain | $A_{v,mid}$ | **−2 to −20 (dimensionless)** |
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | **≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Electron charge | $q$ | **1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always bias the MOSFET in the **saturation (active) region** for linear amplification. The condition is $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (i.e., $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$), where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ is the *overdrive voltage*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A CS MOSFET amplifier with **source-bypass capacitor** $C_S$ (so that $R_S$ is shorted at signal frequencies) operates as follows:

**Step 1 — DC Biasing (Setting the Q-point):**
- A gate-bias resistor $R_1$ (and often a divider $R_1, R_2$) sets the DC gate voltage.
- The source resistor $R_S$ develops a self-bias voltage $V_S = I_D R_S$ so that the gate-source DC voltage becomes $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$.
- The Q-point is chosen such that the drain current is:
$$I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$

**Step 2 — AC Small-Signal Model:**
At signal frequencies, the coupling capacitors ($C_{in}, C_{out}$) and the source-bypass capacitor $C_S$ act as **short circuits**, and the DC supply $V_{DD}$ becomes an **AC ground**. The MOSFET is replaced by its small-signal equivalent: a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source, with output resistance $r_o$ in parallel.

**Step 3 — Mid-Band Voltage Gain:**
The mid-band voltage gain (with $r_o$ neglected for simplicity, which is standard at the B.Tech level) is:
$$A_{v,mid} = \frac{v_o}{v_{gs}} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of CS configuration.

**Step 4 — Frequency Response and Cutoffs:**
- At **low frequencies**, the coupling and bypass capacitors exhibit non-negligible impedance, causing the gain to roll off at −20 dB/decade.
- At **high frequencies**, the **internal parasitic capacitances** of the MOSFET — namely $C_{gs}$ (gate-to-source) and $C_{gd}$ (gate-to-drain, the *Miller* capacitance) — cause the gain to roll off at −20 dB/decade.
- The **band-pass** nature of the amplifier is established between $f_L$ and $f_H$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is a KTU Board-Exam Cheat Sheet — Memorize These Equations.**

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{DQ} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n \dfrac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$ | Drain current in saturation | A |
| 2 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n \dfrac{W}{L} I_{DQ}} = \dfrac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$ | Transconductance at the Q-point | A/V (S) |
| 3 | $g_m = k_n \dfrac{W}{L} V_{OV}$ | Alternate form using overdrive | A/V |
| 4 | $A_{v,mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$ | Mid-band voltage gain (no $r_o$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_D'$ where $R_D' = R_D \Vert R_L \Vert r_o$ | Mid-band gain including output resistance | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_{L,C_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$ | Lower cutoff due to input coupling cap | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{L,C_{out}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (R_D + R_L) C_{out}}$ | Lower cutoff due to output coupling cap | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{L,C_S} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (R_S \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_m}) C_S} = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi C_S}$ (approx.) | Lower cutoff due to source bypass cap (dominant) | Hz |
| 9 | $C_{in,Miller} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$ | Effective input capacitance (Miller's theorem) | F |
| 10 | $C_{out,Miller} = C_{gd}$ | Effective output capacitance | F |
| 11 | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R'_{sig} C_{in,Miller}}$ | Upper cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 12 | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$) | Bandwidth of the amplifier | Hz |
| 13 | $f_T = g_m / (2 \pi C_{gs})$ | Unity-gain (transition) frequency of MOSFET | Hz |
| 14 | $A_{v,mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_{v,mid} \vert$ | Gain in decibels | dB |

> **Note on notation:** $R_S$ (capital) is the **signal-source resistance** seen by the amplifier input, while $R_S$ (lowercase subscript on $R_S$ in $C_S$ context) refers to the MOSFET's source-bias resistor. In this experiment, the dominant low-frequency pole is almost always due to $C_S$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CS MOSFET amplifier is the **fundamental building block** of virtually all analog and mixed-signal integrated circuits. It is the workhorse of:

- **Audio pre-amplifiers** in hearing aids and microphone circuits
- **RF front-end low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)** in mobile phones
- **Operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)** — the heart of every op-amp's input stage
- **Active load stages** in CMOS operational amplifiers (where a PMOS in saturation replaces $R_D$ to achieve very high gain per unit area)
- **Sensor interface circuits** (e.g., for piezoelectric and MEMS sensors)

Understanding its frequency response is critical because every real-world amplifier has a finite bandwidth — and **gain-bandwidth product (GBP)** is a fundamental figure of merit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain

Starting from the small-signal equivalent circuit of the CS amplifier (with $C_S$ fully shorted at signal frequencies, $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ acting as short circuits, and $V_{DD}$ as AC ground):

**Step 1 —** The input loop equation at the gate node:
$$v_{gs} = v_i$$
*(because the gate draws negligible DC current and no AC current flows into the gate for an ideal MOSFET)*

**Step 2 —** The output loop at the drain node. The drain current $i_d$ splits between $R_D$ and the load $R_L$ (assuming both are in parallel to AC ground):
$$i_d = g_m v_{gs}$$

**Step 3 —** The output voltage is the voltage drop across the parallel combination $R_D' = R_D \Vert R_L \Vert r_o$:
$$v_o = -i_d \cdot R_D'$$
*(The negative sign arises because an **increase** in $v_{gs}$ causes $i_d$ to **increase**, which causes a **larger voltage drop** across $R_D$, thereby **lowering** $v_{ds}$ — hence the 180° phase inversion.)*

**Step 4 —** Substituting $i_d$:
$$v_o = -g_m v_{gs} R_D' = -g_m v_i R_D'$$

**Step 5 —** The mid-band voltage gain is therefore:
$$A_{v,mid} = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m R_D' = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L \Vert r_o)$$

For a first-order B.Tech design (ignoring $r_o$):
$$A_{v,mid} \approx -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $g_m$ from the Q-Point

The drain current in saturation follows the square-law:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

The transconductance is the derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ at the Q-point:
$$g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{Q}$$

Performing the differentiation:
$$g_m = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$$

Substituting $V_{OV} = 2 I_D / (k_n W/L) \cdot 1$ from the square-law inverted, we get the **alternative form**:
$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$$

---

### 3.3 Design Example — Worked Numerical Problem

> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
> Design a CS MOSFET amplifier using an N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET with parameters $k_n(W/L) = 1 \text{ mA/V}^2$ and $V_{th} = 1.5 \text{ V}$. The supply is $V_{DD} = 12 \text{ V}$, the desired mid-band voltage gain is $|A_v| = 10$, and the load resistance is $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Determine the required $R_D$ and the Q-point.

**Step 1 — Choose a reasonable drain current and overdrive voltage.**

For low-power design, choose $I_{DQ} = 1 \text{ mA}$. Then from the square-law:
$$I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$
$$10^{-3} = \frac{1}{2} (10^{-3}) V_{OV}^2$$
$$V_{OV}^2 = 2 \implies V_{OV} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the transconductance.**
$$g_m = k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = (10^{-3})(1.414) = 1.414 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gain formula to find $R_D'$.**
$$|A_{v,mid}| = g_m R_D' \implies R_D' = \frac{|A_v|}{g_m} = \frac{10}{1.414 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.07 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Use $R_D' = R_D \Vert R_L$ to find $R_D$.**
$$\frac{1}{R_D'} = \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{R_L} \implies \frac{1}{R_D} = \frac{1}{7.07} - \frac{1}{10}$$

Performing the arithmetic (in kΩ):
$$\frac{1}{R_D} = 0.1414 - 0.1 = 0.0414 \text{ (k}\Omega)^{-1}$$
$$R_D = \frac{1}{0.0414} \approx 24.13 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Verify saturation. Compute DC drain voltage.**
$$V_{DS,Q} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} (R_D + R_S)$$
With $R_S$ chosen small (say 100 Ω for self-bias, negligible contribution):
$$V_{DS,Q} \approx 12 - (10^{-3})(24.13 \times 10^3) = 12 - 24.13 = -12.13 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative $V_{DS}$!** The chosen $I_{DQ}$ is too large for this $R_D$. We must reduce $I_{DQ}$ or reduce $R_D$. The standard KTU fix is to **redesign**.

**Step 6 — Redesign: choose $I_{DQ} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$.**
$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{DQ}}{k_n W/L}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1} = 1 \text{ V}$$
$$g_m = (10^{-3})(1) = 1 \text{ mA/V}$$
$$R_D' = \frac{10}{10^{-3}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ in parallel, we need $R_D \to \infty$ to get $R_D' = 10$ kΩ. This is impractical.

**Step 7 — Use $R_L = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ (typical for an oscilloscope probe).**
$$R_D' = R_D \Vert 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$R_D' = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \implies \frac{1}{R_D} = \frac{1}{10} - \frac{1}{100} = 0.09 \text{ (k}\Omega)^{-1}$$
$$R_D = 11.11 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 8 — Verify saturation.**
$$V_{DS,Q} = 12 - (0.5 \times 10^{-3})(11.11 \times 10^3) = 12 - 5.56 = 6.44 \text{ V}$$
Required: $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV} = 1 \text{ V}$. ✓ **Saturated!**

**Step 9 — Set gate bias.** With $R_S$ small, set $V_G \approx V_{GS} = V_{th} + V_{OV} = 1.5 + 1 = 2.5 \text{ V}$.
A simple gate-bias resistor $R_G$ from gate to a bias voltage or via a high-value resistor to $V_{DD}$ (with $R_2 = 0$, $V_G = 0$): use source self-bias with $R_S$ to achieve the required $V_{GS}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$ Using Miller's Theorem

**Step 1 — State Miller's Theorem.**
An impedance $Z$ connected between input node (gate) and output node (drain) can be split into two grounded impedances:
$$Z_{in,Miller} = \frac{Z}{1 - K}, \quad Z_{out,Miller} = \frac{Z \cdot K}{K - 1}$$
where $K = v_o / v_i = A_v$.

**Step 2 — Apply to $C_{gd}$.**
For $Z = 1/(j \omega C_{gd})$ and $K = -g_m R_D'$:
$$Z_{in,Miller} = \frac{1/(j \omega C_{gd})}{1 + g_m R_D'} = \frac{1}{j \omega C_{gd} (1 + g_m R_D')}$$

This corresponds to an equivalent grounded capacitance:
$$C_{gd,Miller} = C_{gd} (1 + g_m R_D') = C_{gd} (1 - A_v)$$

**Step 3 — Effective input capacitance.**
$$C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D') = C_{gs} + C_{gd,Miller}$$

**Step 4 — Time constant at the input.**
$$\tau_H = R'_{sig} \cdot C_{in,tot}$$
where $R'_{sig} = R_S \Vert R_{in,gate}$ (signal-source resistance seen at the gate).

**Step 5 — Upper cutoff frequency.**
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \tau_H} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R'_{sig} C_{in,tot}}$$

This is the **dominant-pole approximation** valid when $f_H \gg f_L$ and when the output pole is at a much higher frequency than the input pole.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Design Trade-offs

| Design Goal | Effect on $R_D$ | Effect on $I_{DQ}$ | Effect on $g_m$ | Effect on $A_v$ | Effect on BW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $\vert A_v \vert$ | Increase | Decrease | $\propto \sqrt{I_{DQ}}$ → decreases | **Increases** | **Decreases** (larger Miller cap) |
| Increase Bandwidth $f_H$ | Decrease | Increase | Increases | Decreases | **Increases** |
| Lower power consumption | — | Decrease | Decreases | Decreases | — |
| Increase input impedance | Use larger $R_G$ | — | — | — | — |
| Lower $f_L$ | — | — | — | — | Larger $C_S$, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CS MOSFET Amplifier — Circuit Schematic (Functional Block Representation)

> The following Mermaid block represents the **functional signal-flow architecture** of the CS amplifier, mapping each stage of signal transformation from input to output, including the low-frequency and high-frequency roll-off blocks.

```mermaid

    rin --> gatenode["Gate Node - High Z Input"]:::gate
    gatenode --> biasnet["Gate Bias Network R1 R2"]:::bias
    gatenode --> mosfet["MOSFET in Saturation Region - VCCS gm vgs"]:::fet
    mosfet --> drainnode["Drain Node"]:::drain
    drainnode --> rdload["Drain Load Resistor RD"]:::rd
    drainnode --> cout["Output Coupling Capacitor Cout"]:::cap
    cout --> rlload["Load Resistor RL"]:::load
    mosfet -.-> rsource["Source Resistor RS"]:::rs
    rsource -.-> csbypass["Source Bypass Capacitor CS"]:::cap
    drainnode --> hfroll["High Frequency Pole - Miller Effect on Cgd"]:::hf
    rin --> lfroll1["Low Frequency Pole from Cin"]:::lf
    cout --> lfroll2["Low Frequency Pole from Cout"]:::lf
    rsource --> lfroll3["Low Frequency Pole from CS - Dominant"]:::lf
    hfroll --> outnode["Output Node vo - 180 degree Phase Inverted"]:::out
    lfroll1 --> outnode
    lfroll2 --> outnode
    lfroll3 --> outnode
    outnode --> vout["Final Amplified Output vo to Scope"]:::out
```

### 4.2 Bode Plot — Frequency Response Characteristics (Conceptual Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axis["Frequency Axis f - Logarithmic Scale 1 Hz to 100 MHz"]:::axis
    lfband["Low Frequency Region - f less than fL"]:::lf
    midband["Mid Band Region - fL less than f less than fH - Flat Gain Av mid in dB"]:::mid
    hfband["High Frequency Region - f greater than fH"]:::hf
    fLpoint["Corner fL - Gain drops 3 dB - Slope changes to minus 20 dB per decade"]:::corner
    fHpoint["Corner fH - Gain drops 3 dB - Slope changes to minus 20 dB per decade"]:::corner
    fTpoint["Unity Gain Frequency fT - 0 dB Crossing"]:::corner
    fLpoint --> midband
    midband --> fHpoint
    lfband --> fLpoint
    fHpoint --> hfband
    hfband --> fTpoint

    classDef axis fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lf fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef hf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef corner fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vgs["Input Voltage vgs between Gate and Source"]:::in
    vgs --> vccs["Voltage Controlled Current Source gm vgs"]:::vccs
    vccs --> rdprime["Parallel Combination RD prime equals RD parallel RL parallel ro"]:::rd
    rdprime --> vo["Output Voltage vo - Negative Sign - 180 degree Phase Shift"]:::out
    cgsparasitic["Parasitic Cgs between Gate and Source"]:::par
    cgdparasitic["Parasitic Cgd between Gate and Drain - Miller Capacitance"]:::par
    cgsparasitic -.-> vccs
    cgdparasitic -.-> vccs

    classDef in fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Bode Plot for KTU Lab Viva:**
> 1. Mark the **mid-band gain** as a horizontal line at $20 \log_{10} \vert A_{v,mid} \vert$ dB.
> 2. Mark the two corners: $f_L$ and $f_H$, where the gain has dropped by **3 dB** from mid-band.
> 3. Draw **−20 dB/decade** slopes on either side of the mid-band.
> 4. The bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$.
> 5. The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** $GBP = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \cdot f_H$ is approximately constant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define the term *transconductance* ($g_m$) of a MOSFET. State its units and write the formula relating $g_m$ to the drain current $I_{DQ}$.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Transconductance $g_m$ is defined as the rate of change of drain current with respect to the gate-to-source voltage, with $V_{DS}$ held constant:
> $$g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS}=\text{const}}$$
> **Units:** Ampere per Volt (A/V) or Siemens (S). For a MOSFET biased in saturation, the relationship to the drain current is:
> $$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$$
> **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for formula.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. Why is the source-bypass capacitor $C_S$ important in a CS amplifier, and what happens at very low frequencies if $C_S$ is removed?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The source-bypass capacitor $C_S$ provides a low-impedance path to ground for AC signal currents flowing through the source resistor $R_S$, effectively short-circuiting $R_S$ at signal (mid-band) frequencies. This **maximizes the gain** by preventing AC negative feedback through $R_S$. At very low frequencies, the impedance of $C_S$ becomes large, no longer shorting $R_S$. The effective source impedance becomes $R_S$, and the gain drops to $A_v = -g_m R_D' / (1 + g_m R_S)$, which is much smaller in magnitude. $C_S$ therefore sets the **dominant low-frequency pole** of the amplifier.
> **(3 marks)** — 1.5 marks for the function of $C_S$, 1.5 marks for the low-frequency consequence.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Jun 2024 Model Pattern]**
> Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,mid}$ of a Common-Source MOSFET amplifier with a source-bypass capacitor. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CS amplifier with $k_n(W/L) = 0.5 \text{ mA/V}^2$, $V_{th} = 2 \text{ V}$, $V_{GS} = 3.5 \text{ V}$, $R_D = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, compute $I_{DQ}$, $g_m$, $A_{v,mid}$ (in linear and dB), and verify saturation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Assumptions (2 marks):**
   - MOSFET operates in **saturation region** (active region).
   - Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ and source-bypass capacitor $C_S$ act as **short circuits** at signal frequencies.
   - DC supply $V_{DD}$ is treated as **AC ground**.
   - Output resistance $r_o$ is **infinity** (channel-length modulation neglected, B.Tech-level approximation).
   - Signal frequency is in the **mid-band range**, where all internal capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ are open circuits.

2. **Small-signal model (1 mark):**
   The MOSFET is replaced by a VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source.

3. **Input loop (1 mark):**
   $$v_{gs} = v_i$$
   (Gate current is zero for an ideal MOSFET.)

4. **Output loop (2 marks):**
   $$i_d = g_m v_{gs}$$
   $$v_o = -i_d (R_D \Vert R_L) = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

5. **Gain expression (1 mark):**
   $$\boxed{A_{v,mid} = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)}$$
   The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output.

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Compute overdrive voltage (1 mark):**
   $$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.5 - 2 = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

2. **Compute $I_{DQ}$ (2 marks):**
   $$I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(1.5)^2$$
   $$I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2}(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(2.25) = 0.5625 \text{ mA}$$

3. **Compute $g_m$ (1 mark):**
   $$g_m = k_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})(1.5) = 0.75 \text{ mA/V}$$

4. **Compute $A_{v,mid}$ in linear (1 mark):**
   $$R_D \Vert R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$
   $$A_{v,mid} = -(0.75 \times 10^{-3})(3.197 \times 10^3) = -2.398$$

5. **Convert to dB (1 mark):**
   $$A_{v,mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert -2.398 \vert = 20 \log_{10}(2.398) = 20 \times 0.3798 = 7.598 \text{ dB}$$

6. **Verify saturation (1 mark):**
   $$V_{DS,Q} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$$
   (Assuming $V_{DD} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_S$ small)
   $$V_{DS,Q} = 12 - (0.5625 \times 10^{-3})(4.7 \times 10^3) = 12 - 2.644 = 9.356 \text{ V}$$
   Required: $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV} = 1.5 \text{ V}$. ✓ **MOSFET is in saturation.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain **Miller's Theorem** and apply it to find the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$ of a CS MOSFET amplifier given the parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CS amplifier with $g_m = 5 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D' = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 5 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, and signal-source resistance $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, compute $C_{in,Miller}$, $\tau_H$, and $f_H$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Statement of Miller's Theorem (2 marks):**
   Miller's theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with a voltage gain $K = v_o/v_i$ between them can be replaced by:
   - An impedance $Z_1 = Z/(1 - K)$ from input node to ground.
   - An impedance $Z_2 = Z K/(K - 1)$ from output node to ground.
   This greatly simplifies the analysis of capacitances bridging input and output.

2. **Application to $C_{gd}$ (2 marks):**
   With $Z = 1/(j\omega C_{gd})$ and $K = A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_D'$:
   $$Z_{in,Miller} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')}$$
   This is equivalent to a grounded capacitor:
   $$C_{in,Miller} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$

3. **Total input capacitance and $f_H$ (3 marks):**
   $$C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$$
   Assuming the input pole is dominant:
   $$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_S C_{in,tot}}$$
   The output capacitance $C_{gd}$ to ground contributes a second pole, but at a much higher frequency and is usually ignored in B.Tech-level analysis.

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Compute $C_{in,Miller}$ (3 marks):**
   $$C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D') = (1 \text{ pF}) (1 + (5 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^3))$$
   $$= (1 \text{ pF})(1 + 20) = 21 \text{ pF}$$
   $$C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + 21 \text{ pF} = 5 + 21 = 26 \text{ pF}$$

2. **Compute $\tau_H$ (2 marks):**
   $$\tau_H = R_S C_{in,tot} = (1 \times 10^3)(26 \times 10^{-12}) = 26 \text{ ns}$$

3. **Compute $f_H$ (2 marks):**
   $$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \tau_H} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (26 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{10^9}{2 \pi \times 26} \approx 6.12 \text{ MHz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error on Gain:** Students often forget the negative sign in $A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_D'$. The minus sign is **mandatory** and physically meaningful — it represents the 180° phase inversion. Losing this costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Saturation Not Verified:** Simply plugging numbers into the gain formula is incomplete. The Q-point must be verified to be in saturation, else the small-signal model is invalid. Examiners **specifically look** for the line "$V_{DS,Q} \geq V_{OV}$, hence MOSFET is in saturation."
> 3. **Confusing $f_L$ poles:** There are three low-frequency poles (from $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_S$). The **dominant** one is almost always from $C_S$. Using the wrong formula (e.g., $1/(2\pi R_S C_{in})$) is a frequent error.
> 4. **Units in $f_H$ Calculation:** A common error is mixing pF and μF, or kΩ and Ω. Always write units explicitly in the intermediate steps: $R \cdot C$ should be in seconds.
> 5. **Miller Capacitance Direction:** Some students apply Miller to $C_{gs}$ instead of $C_{gd}$. Miller's theorem applies to the capacitance **bridging input and output** — that is $C_{gd}$, not $C_{gs}$.
> 6. **dB Conversion:** $20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$ is the correct formula. Using $10 \log_{10}$ (which is for power) is a **fatal error** in B.Tech analog exams.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [x] **CS Amplifier Definition:** Input at gate-source, output at drain-source, source is common. Output is **180° phase-inverted** with respect to input.
- [x] **Mid-band gain (with bypass):** $A_{v,mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L \Vert r_o)$. In B.Tech, $r_o \to \infty$ is assumed.
- [x] **Transconductance:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n (W/L) I_{DQ}} = 2 I_{DQ}/V_{OV} = k_n (W/L) V_{OV}$.
- [x] **Saturation Condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. **Always verify this in design problems.**
- [x] **Three Low-Frequency Poles** — from $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_S$ — with the $C_S$ pole typically dominant.
- [x] **Miller's Theorem** applies to $C_{gd}$ (not $C_{gs}$), giving an effective input capacitance $C_{in,Miller} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D')$.
- [x] **Upper cutoff:** $f_H = 1 / (2 \pi R'_{sig} C_{in,tot})$ using the dominant-pole approximation.
- [x] **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$).
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** Approximately constant; increasing $|A_v|$ reduces BW and vice versa — a fundamental trade-off.
- [x] **dB Conversion:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- [x] **Bode Plot Shape:** Flat mid-band, −20 dB/decade roll-off on each side, with corners at $f_L$ and $f_H$ where gain drops 3 dB.
- [x] **Lab Procedure:** For the experiment, set Q-point with multimeter, apply 10–50 mV sinusoidal input at mid-band, measure $|v_o|/|v_i|$ for gain, then sweep frequency from 10 Hz to 10 MHz to plot the Bode curve.
- [x] **Typical Component Values:** $V_{DD} = \pm 12 \text{ V}$ or single $12 \text{ V}$, $R_D = 2.2 \text{–}10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 100 \text{–}470 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_{in} = C_{out} = 1 \text{–}10 \text{ μF}$, $C_S = 47 \text{–}470 \text{ μF}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascaded Amplifier (CE-CE) - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cascaded Amplifier (CE-CE) - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascaded CE–CE Amplifier:** A two-stage amplifier in which the output of the first Common-Emitter (CE) stage is AC-coupled through a coupling capacitor to the input of a second identical (or similar) Common-Emitter stage, so that the overall small-signal voltage gain is the **product** of the individual stage gains, while the input and output impedances are dominated by the first and last stages respectively.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the experiment is treated as a **design-and-test exercise** under the Course Outcomes **CO1 (Design analog circuits for given specifications)** and **CO2 (Analyze frequency response characteristics)**. The design goal is to achieve a prescribed mid-band voltage gain (typically $\vert A_v \vert = 100$ to $1000$, i.e., **40 dB to 60 dB**) by cascading two CE stages biased in the **active region** using the **voltage-divider bias** configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cascaded amplifier like **two magnifying glasses placed in a line**. The first lens (Stage 1) magnifies a tiny object 10×, producing an image that is now 10× larger but may still be hard to see clearly. The second lens (Stage 2) magnifies that already-magnified image by another 10×, giving a final image that is 100× larger than the original. The two magnifications **multiply**, not add.

The catch is that each lens (stage) has limits: it can only magnify clearly within a certain range of distances. Outside that range (too low frequency or too high frequency), the image becomes blurry. The **frequency response** of the cascade is the combined "sharpness band" of both lenses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascade instead of one big stage?**
> A single CE stage can only provide a moderate gain (typically 50–200) before it becomes unstable, distorts, or loses bandwidth. Cascading **two moderate-gain stages** gives high overall gain **without** compromising bandwidth or linearity.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (BC547 / 2N2222) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base–Emitter voltage drop | $V_{BE}$ | **0.7** | V |
| Thermal voltage (at 27°C) | $V_T$ | **26 mV** | V |
| Current gain (DC) | $\beta_{DC}$ / $h_{FE}$ | **100 – 800** (typ. 200) | – |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\approx 38.5 \cdot I_C$ (mA) | mA/V |
| Input resistance (small-signal) | $r_\pi$ | $\beta \cdot V_T / I_B = V_T / I_B$ | $\Omega$ |
| Output resistance (Early) | $r_o$ | $\sim 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ – $200\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical design values below assume the widely used lab transistor **BC547 (NPN, Si, $\beta = 200$ nominal)** with a single supply $V_{CC} = +12$ V unless stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Stage-by-Stage Logic)

A single **Common-Emitter (CE) stage with voltage-divider bias** performs three jobs at once:

1. **DC Biasing** — fixes the Q-point (quiescent operating point) in the active region so that the BJT acts as a linear amplifier.
2. **AC Amplification** — converts small base-emitter voltage variations into much larger collector current variations, which then develop a large voltage across $R_C$.
3. **Phase Inversion** — the output at the collector is **180° out of phase** with the input at the base.

When **two such stages are cascaded** (AC-coupled via $C_{in}$, $C_{C}$, $C_{out}$):

- **Stage 1** amplifies and inverts the signal.
- **Stage 2** amplifies again and inverts again, so the **overall phase shift is 360° = 0° (in-phase)** at mid-band.
- Overall gain magnitude is the product:

$$A_{v(\text{total})} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$$

- In decibels, products become sums:

$$A_{v(\text{total,dB})} = A_{v1(\text{dB})} + A_{v2(\text{dB})}$$

### 2.2 Why Voltage-Divider Bias?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Voltage-divider bias is the **only** biasing scheme used in KTU lab experiments because it provides the **highest Q-point stability** against variations in $\beta$ and temperature. The Thevenin equivalent of $R_1 \parallel R_2$ is designed to be much smaller than $\beta R_E$ so that $I_C$ becomes independent of $\beta$.

### 2.3 Design Specifications (Typical KTU Lab Problem)

| Specification | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Overall voltage gain | $\vert A_v \vert$ | 100 – 1000 (40 – 60 dB) |
| Supply voltage | $V_{CC}$ | +12 V (single supply) |
| Load resistance | $R_L$ | 4.7 k$\Omega$ or 10 k$\Omega$ |
| Q-point | $I_C$, $V_{CE}$ | 1 mA, 6 V (mid-rail) |
| Lower cut-off frequency | $f_L$ | $\leq 100$ Hz |
| Upper cut-off frequency | $f_H$ | $\geq 100$ kHz |
| Transistor | – | BC547 (NPN Si) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the table below as the **single source of truth** for all derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Emitter voltage | $V_E = I_E R_E$ | $I_E$ in A, $R_E$ in $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Base voltage | $V_B = V_E + V_{BE}$ | $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (Si) |
| 3 | Q-point collector current | $I_C \approx I_E$ | Active region |
| 4 | Thevenin divider | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | – |
| 5 | Stability requirement | $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ |
| 6 | Collector voltage | $V_C = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | – |
| 7 | Q-point $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CE} = V_C - V_E$ | Mid-rail $\approx V_{CC}/2$ |
| 8 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | $V_T = 26$ mV at 27°C |
| 9 | Input resistance (BJT) | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Stage input resistance | $R_{in(\text{stage})} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | – |
| 11 | Stage output resistance | $R_{out(\text{stage})} \approx R_C \parallel r_o$ | $r_o \gg R_C$ ignored |
| 12 | CE voltage gain (with $R_E$ bypassed) | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) \approx -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$ | $r_e = 1/g_m$ |
| 13 | CE voltage gain (with $R_E$ unbypassed) | $A_v \approx -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_E}$ | Exact, low-frequency |
| 14 | Overall cascaded gain | $A_{v(\text{total})} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ | Mid-band, both stages |
| 15 | Lower cut-off (coupling) | $f_{L(C)} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{\text{eq}} C}$ | $R_{\text{eq}}$ = Thevenin resistance seen by $C$ |
| 16 | Upper cut-off (Miller) | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{\text{sig}}^\prime C_{in,\text{Miller}}}$ | $R_{\text{sig}}^\prime = R_{\text{sig}} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ |
| 17 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Since $f_H \gg f_L$ |
| 18 | Gain–Bandwidth product (const.) | $GBW = \vert A_{v(\text{mid})} \vert \cdot BW$ | Single-stage figure of merit |

### 2.5 Why Cascaded Stages Trade Gain for Bandwidth

A single stage with a large $R_C$ achieves high gain but its **Miller-multiplied input capacitance** $C_{M} = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$ becomes enormous, dragging $f_H$ down. By splitting the gain into two moderate stages, each $C_M$ is smaller, so each stage has a wider bandwidth, and the **overall bandwidth is widened** (the dominant pole rises). This is the **gain-bandwidth trade-off** in its most useful form for KTU viva questions.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Cascaded CE-CE topologies form the **front-end gain block** of virtually every analog receiver: RF amplifiers in communication transceivers, photodiode pre-amplifiers in instrumentation, audio pre-amplifiers in mixer consoles, and the input stage of operational-amplifier designs (e.g., the classic **$\mu$A741** uses a Darlington-cascoded CE input).
