## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Design Example (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Problem:** Design a two-stage CE-CE cascaded amplifier using BC547 with $V_{CC} = 12$ V, load $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, target overall mid-band voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert = 500$ (≈ 54 dB), and lower cut-off frequency $f_L \leq 50$ Hz. Assume both stages are identical.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Choose the per-stage gain

Because the cascade multiplies gains, and to keep both stages identical for biasing simplicity:

$$A_{v1} = A_{v2} = \sqrt{\vert A_{v,\text{total}} \vert} = \sqrt{500}$$

$$A_{v1} = A_{v2} \approx 22.36 \;\;\text{(dimensionless)}$$

In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(22.36) \approx 27$ dB per stage.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Choose the Q-point

Standard lab convention: $I_C = 1$ mA, $V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2 = 6$ V (mid-rail for maximum symmetrical swing).

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Choose $R_E$ and find $R_C$

Take $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$ (standard value). Then:

$$V_E = I_E R_E \approx (1\text{ mA})(1\text{ k}\Omega) = 1\text{ V}$$

Using the approximate gain formula with $R_E$ bypassed by a large $C_E$:

$$A_{v1} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{1\text{ mA}}{26\text{ mV}} \approx 38.46\text{ mA/V}$$

Let $R_{C,\text{eff}} = R_C \parallel R_L$. We need:

$$\vert A_{v1} \vert = g_m \cdot R_{C,\text{eff}} = 38.46 \cdot R_{C,\text{eff}} \approx 22.36$$

$$R_{C,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{22.36}{38.46} \approx 0.581\text{ k}\Omega = 581\,\Omega$$

Now solve for $R_C$ using $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$:

$$\dfrac{1}{R_{C,\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{1}{R_C} + \dfrac{1}{R_L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{1}{R_C} = \dfrac{1}{581} - \dfrac{1}{4700}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{R_C} = 0.001721 - 0.000213 = 0.001508\;\text{ (per }\Omega)$$

$$R_C = \dfrac{1}{0.001508} \approx 663\,\Omega$$

**Standard nearest value: $R_C = 680\;\Omega$** (E12 series).

**Verification of Q-point:** With $I_C = 1$ mA and $R_C = 680\;\Omega$:

$$V_C = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (1\text{ mA})(680\;\Omega) = 12 - 0.68 = 11.32\text{ V}$$

$$V_{CE} = V_C - V_E = 11.32 - 1 = 10.32\text{ V}$$

The Q-point is shifted to the upper half — this is acceptable because the signal swing is constrained by $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2$ V at the lower end and $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ at the upper end, giving an asymmetric but still linear swing of ~10 V. For a perfectly symmetric swing at this $I_C$, we would need $R_C = (V_{CC} - V_E - V_{CE})/I_C = (12 - 1 - 6)/1\text{ mA} = 5$ k$\Omega$, but that would make the gain $\approx 38.5 \times 4.7\text{k} \parallel 5\text{k} \approx 102$ per stage — too high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off:** Higher $R_C$ → more gain per stage → fewer stages needed. But: larger $V_{RC}$ drop → less headroom for output swing. The KTU exam answer must explicitly mention this trade-off.

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Choose the voltage-divider bias resistors

We have $V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 1 + 0.7 = 1.7$ V.

Choose $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1.7 = 12 \cdot \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{R_1 + 10\,\text{k}}$$

$$R_1 + 10\,\text{k} = \dfrac{12 \times 10\,\text{k}}{1.7} = 70.59\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_1 = 70.59 - 10 = 60.59\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Standard nearest value: $R_1 = 56$ k$\Omega$** (E12) or use a **potentiometer** for fine tuning in lab.

**Stability check:** $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = 60.59\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} = 8.57\text{ k}\Omega$. The condition $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ gives $200 \times 1\text{k} = 200\text{k} \gg 10 \times 8.57\text{k} = 85.7\text{k}$ — **stable** (ratio ≈ 23).

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Choose coupling and bypass capacitors

For $f_L \leq 50$ Hz, compute each capacitor's required size using $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the **input-side Thevenin resistance** seen by each capacitor.

| Capacitor | Position | $R_{\text{eq}}$ | Required $C$ (for $f_L = 50$ Hz) | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{in}$ | Source → Stage 1 base | $R_S + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (50)(R_{\text{eq}})}$ | 10 µF |
| $C_E$ | Bypass across $R_E$ | $R_E \parallel \left(\dfrac{r_\pi + R_{TH}}{\beta + 1}\right) \approx R_E \parallel r_e$ | – | 100 µF |
| $C_{C}$ | Stage 1 collector → Stage 2 base | $(R_{C1} \parallel r_{o1}) + (R_{1,2} \parallel R_{2,2} \parallel r_{\pi,2})$ | – | 10 µF |
| $C_{out}$ | Stage 2 collector → Load | $(R_{C2} \parallel r_{o2}) + R_L$ | – | 10 µF |

Numerical check for $C_{in}$: assume $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$ (signal generator's typical 50 $\Omega$ plus series resistor in lab). With $r_\pi = \beta V_T / I_C = 200 \times 26\text{mV} / 1\text{mA} = 5.2$ k$\Omega$:

$$R_{in1} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 60.6\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 5.2\text{k} \approx 3.27\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{\text{eq},C_{in}} = R_S + R_{in1} \approx 1\text{k} + 3.27\text{k} = 4.27\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$C_{in} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (50)(4270)} \approx 0.75\;\mu\text{F} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 10\;\mu\text{F} \text{ (lab safety factor of ~13)}$$

Numerical check for $C_E$ (bypass): $r_e = V_T/I_E = 26\text{mV}/1\text{mA} = 26\;\Omega$. So $R_{\text{eq},C_E} = R_E \parallel r_e = 1000 \parallel 26 \approx 25.3\;\Omega$:

$$C_E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (50)(25.3)} \approx 126\;\mu\text{F} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 100\;\mu\text{F} \text{ or } 220\;\mu\text{F}$$

#### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute small-signal parameters and overall gain

$$g_m = 38.46\text{ mA/V},\quad r_\pi = 5.2\text{ k}\Omega,\quad r_e = 26\;\Omega$$

$$A_{v1} = -g_m (R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2})$$

Now $R_{in2}$ = input resistance of Stage 2 ≈ 3.27 k$\Omega$ (same as Stage 1):

$$R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2} = 680 \parallel 3270 \approx 562\;\Omega$$

$$A_{v1} = -(38.46\text{ mA/V})(562\;\Omega) \approx -21.6$$

$$A_{v2} = -g_m (R_{C2} \parallel R_L) = -(38.46\text{ mA/V})(680 \parallel 4700) \approx -(38.46)(595) \approx -22.9$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-21.6)(-22.9) = +494.6 \approx 495$$

> [!NOTE]
> Achieved gain is 495, very close to the target of 500. The small discrepancy is due to the rounding of $R_C$ to the nearest standard value (680 $\Omega$). To hit exactly 500, use a **1 k$\Omega$ potentiometer** in place of $R_C$ and trim to the desired voltage on the CRO.

In decibels:

$$A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(495) \approx 53.9\text{ dB}$$

#### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Frequency response prediction

The lower cut-off of the **cascade** is dominated by the highest individual $f_L$ among all four capacitors:

$$f_{L,\text{cascade}} \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2 + f_{L4}^2}$$

In our design, $C_E$ is the dominant low-frequency pole (largest $R \cdot C$ product). At $C_E = 100$ µF and $R_{\text{eq}} = 25.3\;\Omega$:

$$f_{L(C_E)} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (25.3)(100\mu)} \approx 63\text{ Hz}$$

The upper cut-off is set by the Miller effect of $C_{\mu} \approx 5$ pF (BC547):

$$C_{M1} = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_{C,\text{eff}}) = 5\text{ pF}(1 + 38.5 \times 0.562\text{k}) = 5\text{ pF}(1 + 21.6) \approx 113\text{ pF}$$

$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{sig}}^\prime C_{M,\text{total}}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (1\text{k})(113\text{ pF})} \approx 1.4\text{ MHz}$$

Bandwidth: $BW \approx f_H - f_L \approx 1.4$ MHz (mid-band is 50 Hz – 1.4 MHz).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How Each Specification Forces a Design Decision

| Specification You Must Hit | Forces You to Choose | Because |
|---|---|---|
| Overall gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | $A_{v1} = A_{v2} = \sqrt{\vert A_v \vert}$ | $A_v = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ |
| Output swing / headroom | $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$, then $R_C$ | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ |
| Bias stability | $R_{TH} \ll \beta R_E / 10$ | $I_C$ must not drift with $\beta$ |
| Lower cut-off $f_L$ | $C_{in}, C_C, C_{out}, C_E$ | $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C)$ |
| Upper cut-off $f_H$ | $R_S$ and $R_C$ (small) | Miller $C_M = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$ |
| Input impedance | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | Set by bias network + $\beta$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define a cascaded amplifier. Why is a CE-CE cascade preferred over a single high-gain CE stage in most amplifier applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cascaded amplifier** is a multi-stage amplifier in which the output of one stage is fed as the input to the next stage, typically through a **DC-blocking (coupling) capacitor** so that the Q-points of the two stages remain independent.

A single high-gain CE stage suffers from a very large **Miller-multiplied input capacitance** $C_M = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$, which drastically reduces the **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$** and shrinks the **bandwidth**. By splitting the required gain into two moderate-gain CE stages in cascade, each stage's Miller capacitance is smaller, so the **overall bandwidth is widened** while still achieving a large overall voltage gain ($A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$). This is the classic **gain-bandwidth trade-off** managed optimally through cascading. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is voltage-divider biasing used in the design of a CE-CE cascaded amplifier instead of fixed bias?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Voltage-divider bias provides a **Q-point that is largely independent of $\beta$ and temperature**, provided the stability condition $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ is satisfied (where $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$). This is essential in a cascaded amplifier because:

1. **Transistor matching** between the two stages becomes less critical — small $\beta$ variations do not shift the operating point.
2. **Thermal runaway** is prevented: as $I_C$ tends to rise with temperature, $V_E$ rises, which reduces $V_{BE}$, thereby reducing $I_B$ and stabilizing $I_C$.
3. **DC isolation between stages** is naturally achieved because the coupling capacitor $C_C$ blocks the DC bias of Stage 1 from interfering with Stage 2's bias.

Fixed bias, by contrast, has $I_C = \beta I_B$, so the Q-point drifts wildly with $\beta$, making it unsuitable for cascaded designs. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) follows the rule: **sub-part (a) for 7 marks + sub-part (b) for 7 marks**. Each sub-part is independently graded against a 7-mark rubric.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Design a two-stage CE-CE cascaded amplifier using BC547 (NPN, $\beta = 200$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V) for a mid-band voltage gain of 600 with $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, and $f_L \leq 100$ Hz. Both stages are identical. Calculate all resistor and capacitor values and predict the overall voltage gain in dB.**

##### **Part (a) — DC Bias Design & Component Selection (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Per-stage gain:**
$$A_{v1} = A_{v2} = \sqrt{600} \approx 24.49$$

**Step 2 — Q-point choice:** $I_C = 1$ mA, $V_{CE} = 6$ V (mid-rail). Choose $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$ → $V_E = 1$ V.

**Step 3 — $R_C$ calculation:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{1\text{ mA}}{26\text{ mV}} = 38.46\text{ mA/V}$$

$$R_{C,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\vert A_{v1} \vert}{g_m} = \dfrac{24.49}{38.46} = 637\;\Omega$$

With $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$:

$$\dfrac{1}{R_C} = \dfrac{1}{637} - \dfrac{1}{4700} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_C \approx 739\;\Omega$$

**Nearest standard: $R_C = 750\;\Omega$** (E24 series).

**Step 4 — Voltage-divider bias resistors:** $V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 1.7$ V. Choose $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$R_1 = \dfrac{V_{CC} R_2}{V_B} - R_2 = \dfrac{12 \times 10\text{k}}{1.7} - 10\text{k} = 60.6\text{ k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_1 = 56\text{ k}\Omega \text{ (std.)}$$

**Stability check:** $R_{TH} = 56\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} = 8.48$ k$\Omega$; $\beta R_E = 200 \times 1\text{k} = 200$ k$\Omega \gg 10 R_{TH} = 84.8$ k$\Omega$. ✓ **(Stable)**

**Step 5 — Capacitors for $f_L = 100$ Hz:**

| Capacitor | $R_{\text{eq}}$ | Required $C$ | Standard value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{in}$ | $R_S + R_{in1} = 1\text{k} + (56\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 5.2\text{k}) \approx 1\text{k} + 3.3\text{k} = 4.3\text{ k}\Omega$ | $1 / (2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 4300) = 0.37\;\mu\text{F}$ | **1 µF** |
| $C_E$ | $R_E \parallel r_e = 1000 \parallel 26 = 25.3\;\Omega$ | $1 / (2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 25.3) = 63\;\mu\text{F}$ | **100 µF** |
| $C_C$ | $R_{C1} + R_{in2} \approx 750 + 3300 = 4050\;\Omega$ | $0.39\;\mu\text{F}$ | **1 µF** |
| $C_{out}$ | $R_{C2} + R_L = 750 + 4700 = 5450\;\Omega$ | $0.29\;\mu\text{F}$ | **1 µF** |

**Complete component list (per stage):** $R_C = 750\;\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_1 = 56$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1\;\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\;\mu\text{F}$. (Inter-stage: $C_C = 1\;\mu\text{F}$; output: $C_{out} = 1\;\mu\text{F}$.) **(7 marks — full design + stability check + capacitor selection)**

---

##### **Part (b) — Gain Verification & Frequency Response Prediction (7 marks)**

**Verify the overall mid-band gain:**

$$A_{v1} = -g_m(R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2}) = -(38.46)(750 \parallel 3300) = -(38.46)(610) = -23.46$$

$$A_{v2} = -g_m(R_{C2} \parallel R_L) = -(38.46)(750 \parallel 4700) = -(38.46)(676) = -26.0$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-23.46)(-26.0) = +609.9 \approx 610$$

$$A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(610) = 55.7\text{ dB}$$

**Predicted lower cut-off:** Dominant pole from $C_E$ (highest $RC$ product):

$$f_{L,\text{dom}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (25.3)(100\mu)} \approx 63\text{ Hz}$$

**Predicted upper cut-off (Miller effect, $C_\mu = 5$ pF):**

$$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{C1,\text{eff}}) = 5\text{ pF}(1 + 38.5 \times 0.61\text{k}) = 5\text{ pF}(1 + 23.46) = 122\text{ pF}$$

$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_{in1}) C_{M1,\text{tot}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (1\text{k})(122\text{ pF})} \approx 1.3\text{ MHz}$$

**Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx 1.3$ MHz

**Gain–Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = 610 \times 1.3\text{ MHz} \approx 793\text{ MHz}$

**Expected CRO observation:** Input sine wave (e.g., 10 mV peak, 1 kHz) and output sine wave (~6.1 V peak) with **no phase shift** at mid-band. As frequency is swept from 10 Hz to 1 MHz, the output amplitude traces the band-pass shape with −3 dB points at ~63 Hz and ~1.3 MHz. **(7 marks — gain, dB, bandwidth, Bode shape, phase behavior)**

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample Paper]` (Alternative Choice)

**For a CE-CE cascaded amplifier with $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$, $I_C = 0.5$ mA, $R_E = 1.2$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 250$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V:**
**(a)** Design the voltage-divider bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ for both stages, verify Q-point stability, and compute the small-signal parameters $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_e$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the overall mid-band voltage gain with and without emitter-bypass capacitors, and sketch the expected frequency response (Bode magnitude plot) showing the mid-band gain in dB and the −3 dB cut-off frequencies. **(7 marks)**

##### **Part (a) — Bias Design & Small-Signal Parameters (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $I_C = I_E = 0.5$ mA, $R_E = 1.2$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 250$.

**Emitter and base voltages:**

$$V_E = I_E R_E = (0.5\text{ mA})(1.2\text{ k}\Omega) = 0.6\text{ V}$$

$$V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 0.6 + 0.7 = 1.3\text{ V}$$

**Choose $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$ (standard). Then:**

$$R_1 = \dfrac{V_{CC} R_2}{V_B} - R_2 = \dfrac{12 \times 10\text{k}}{1.3} - 10\text{k} = 92.3\text{ k}\Omega - 10\text{ k}\Omega = 82.3\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Standard value: $R_1 = 82$ k$\Omega$** (E12 series).

**Stability check:**

$$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = 82\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} = \dfrac{820}{92} = 8.91\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\beta R_E = 250 \times 1.2\text{k} = 300\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$10 R_{TH} = 89.1\text{ k}\Omega \ll 300\text{ k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{Stability ratio} = 300/89.1 \approx 3.37$$

> [!WARNING]
> The stability ratio is **only 3.4**, which is **below the textbook threshold of 10**. In KTU valuation, this would lose 1 mark unless the student explicitly comments on it and either reduces $R_2$ (e.g., to 4.7 k$\Omega$, which raises the ratio to ~5) or accepts reduced stability in favour of higher input impedance. **Examiner's pitfall: failing to verify the stability condition is a common mark loss.**

**Small-signal parameters:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{0.5\text{ mA}}{26\text{ mV}} = 19.23\text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{250}{19.23\text{ mA/V}} = 13.0\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{26\text{ mV}}{0.5\text{ mA}} = 52\;\Omega$$

**Verification:** $r_\pi = \beta r_e = 250 \times 52\;\Omega = 13.0$ k$\Omega$ ✓ **(7 marks — $R_1$, $R_2$, stability check with caveat, all three small-signal parameters)**

---

##### **Part (b) — Gain Analysis and Bode Plot Description (7 marks)**

**Choose $R_C$ to place Q-point at $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2 = 6$ V:**

$$V_C = V_{CE} + V_E = 6 + 0.6 = 6.6\text{ V}$$

$$R_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_C}{I_C} = \dfrac{12 - 6.6}{0.5\text{ mA}} = 10.8\text{ k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_C = 10\text{ k}\Omega\text{ (std.)}$$

**Stage input resistance (each stage):**

$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 82\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 13\text{k} = \dfrac{1}{\frac{1}{82} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{13}} = \dfrac{1}{0.0122 + 0.1 + 0.0769} = \dfrac{1}{0.1891} = 5.29\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Gain WITH $R_E$ bypassed (typical mid-band operation):**

$$A_{v1} = -g_m (R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2}) = -(19.23)(10\text{k} \parallel 5.29\text{k}) = -(19.23)(3.46\text{k}) = -66.5$$

$$A_{v2} = -g_m (R_{C2} \parallel R_L) = -(19.23)(10\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k}) = -(19.23)(5\text{k}) = -96.15$$

$$A_{v,\text{total,bypassed}} = (-66.5)(-96.15) = +6395 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 20\log_{10}(6395) = 76.1\text{ dB}$$

**Gain WITHOUT $R_E$ bypassed (low-frequency limit):**

$$A_{v1,\text{no bypass}} \approx -\dfrac{R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2}}{R_E} = -\dfrac{3.46\text{k}}{1.2\text{k}} = -2.88$$

$$A_{v2,\text{no bypass}} \approx -\dfrac{R_{C2} \parallel R_L}{R_E} = -\dfrac{5\text{k}}{1.2\text{k}} = -4.17$$

$$A_{v,\text{total,no bypass}} = (-2.88)(-4.17) = +12.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 20\log_{10}(12) = 21.6\text{ dB}$$

**The bypass capacitors therefore provide a mid-band gain boost of** $76.1 - 21.6 = 54.5$ **dB.**

**Bode Plot Description:**

| Frequency Band | Slope | Approx. Gain |
|---|---|---|
| $f < 50$ Hz (very low) | +20 dB/decade rising | 21.6 dB floor (set by unbypassed $R_E$) |
| 50 Hz – 200 Hz (transition) | Rises as $C_E$, $C_C$ start conducting | 21.6 → 76.1 dB |
| 200 Hz – 800 kHz (mid-band plateau) | 0 dB/decade (flat) | **76.1 dB** |
| 800 kHz – 5 MHz (high transition) | −20 dB/decade (Miller effect of $C_\mu$) | Falling |
| $f > 5$ MHz | −40 dB/decade (two stages rolling off) | Rapidly falling |

**−3 dB cut-offs:** $f_L \approx 200$ Hz (low), $f_H \approx 800$ kHz (high). **Bandwidth ≈ 800 kHz**. **(7 marks — both gain cases, dB conversions, Bode description with slopes and cut-offs)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CE-CE Cascade Design Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the stability check** $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ — KTU examiners specifically look for this; missing it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in cascade phase** — students often write $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} - A_{v2}$. Correct: $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ (a product, not a sum). The overall sign is **positive** (two inversions = 360° = 0°).
> 3. **Not converting to dB** — KTU expects $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Ignoring the loading effect** of $R_{in}$ of Stage 2 on $R_{C1}$ of Stage 1 — a single-stage $A_{v1}$ formula without loading overestimates gain by ~30 %.
> 5. **Wrong $f_L$ calculation** — students often use $R_{in1}$ alone for the $C_{in}$ pole; correct is the sum $R_S + R_{in1}$.
> 6. **Miller capacitance omission** in $f_H$ analysis — KTU's CO2 specifically tests this; failure loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to bias Stage 2 independently** — the inter-stage coupling capacitor $C_C$ isolates the DC bias of the two stages; this is the entire reason a cascade is feasible.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cascaded CE-CE amplifier** = two voltage-divider biased CE stages AC-coupled by a capacitor, providing multiplicative voltage gain and in-phase overall output.
- **Overall gain = product** $A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$; for identical stages, each stage gain is $\sqrt{\vert A_{v,\text{total}} \vert}$.
- **Voltage-divider bias** is mandatory for $\beta$-independent Q-point; **stability condition** is $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$.
- **$g_m = I_C / V_T$** (≈ 38.5 mA/V per mA of collector current at room temperature).
- **$A_v$ with $R_E$ bypassed** $= -g_m (R_C \parallel R_{L,\text{next}})$ — always include the **next stage's input resistance** in $R_{C,\text{eff}}$.
- **$A_v$ without bypass** $\approx -(R_C \parallel R_{L,\text{next}})/R_E$ — gain drops dramatically at low frequency.
- **dB conversion:** $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Lower cut-off** is set by the **largest** $R_{\text{eq}} C$ product — typically the bypass capacitor $C_E$ dominates.
- **Upper cut-off** is set by the **Miller-multiplied** $C_\mu$: $C_M = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_{C,\text{eff}})$.
- **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for typical audio/RF amplifiers.
- **Gain–Bandwidth product** of the cascade is approximately **constant** when stages are identical: $GBW_{\text{cascade}} \approx GBW_{\text{single}} \cdot \sqrt{2}$.
- **Phase:** Each CE stage inverts (180°); two stages → 360° = 0° (in-phase) at mid-band.
- **Capacitor sizing rule of thumb:** $C_{in}, C_C, C_{out} \approx 1$–$10\;\mu\text{F}$; $C_E \approx 100\;\mu\text{F}$ for low $f_L$.
- **Standard component values used in lab:** $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 680\;\Omega$ to $10$ k$\Omega$ (depends on gain), $R_1 = 56$–$82$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$, BC547 with $\beta \approx 200$.
- **Lab observation:** Input and output are **in-phase** at mid-band; output amplitude is 500–6000× the input (53–76 dB) depending on design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascode Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cascode Amplifier - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascode Amplifier** — A two-transistor analog building block formed by cascading a **Common-Emitter (CE) stage** directly into a **Common-Base (CB) stage**, such that the collector of the lower transistor (Q1, CE) is tied to the emitter of the upper transistor (Q2, CB). The signal is injected at the base of Q1 and extracted from the collector of Q2.

The word *cascode* is a portmanteau of **"CAscaded CODE"** (CEmitter–COmmon-base). It is widely used in **RF front-ends**, **op-amp input stages**, and **high-gain intermediate frequency (IF) amplifiers** because it combines the **high input impedance of a CE stage** with the **excellent high-frequency response of a CB stage**, while suppressing the **Miller effect** that normally cripples a CE amplifier at high frequencies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "Two People Passing a Bucket"** 🪣
>
> Imagine a person (Q1) catching water in a bucket and *almost* spilling it, but instead of holding the bucket high above his head, he hands it instantly to a second person (Q2) standing on a platform, who then lifts and pours it out steadily. The first person never feels the full weight of the lifted bucket (low collector load → no Miller multiplication), and the second person can pour at his own pace without the bucket wobbling. The combined team delivers far more water, far more cleanly, than either person working alone.

In electronics terms:
- **Q1 (CE)** — provides the **voltage-to-current conversion** ($g_m$ transconductance) and the **high input impedance** ($r_\pi \sim$ tens of kΩ).
- **Q2 (CB)** — provides a **very low input impedance at its emitter** ($r_e = 1/g_m \sim$ tens of Ω), which acts as the *collector load* of Q1, dramatically reducing the Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$.
- **The output** at Q2's collector is taken across $R_C$ and inherits the full transconductance of the pair, giving the same midband gain as a CE stage but with **5×–10× higher bandwidth**.

## 1.3 Why Cascode? — The Engineering Motivation

| Issue in Plain CE Amplifier | How Cascode Solves It |
| :--- | :--- |
| Large Miller multiplication of $C_{\mu}$ | Q1's collector sees only $\sim r_e$ of Q2, so $A_{v1}\approx 1$ → $C_M \approx 0$ |
| Low output impedance | Q2's CB output gives $R_{out}\approx r_{o2}$ (very high) |
| Moderate gain | Cascode retains $-g_m R_C$ of CE, no gain penalty |
| Poor reverse isolation | Q2's CB stage prevents signal feed-back into Q1 |

## 1.4 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Thermal Voltage $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$** at $T = 300$ K (standard lab temperature).
> - **Transconductance of a BJT:** $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$.
> - **Base–emitter ON voltage:** $V_{BE(on)} \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$ (silicon, room temperature).
> - **Typical small-signal $\beta$ (hFE):** **100 to 300** for general-purpose BJTs (BC547, 2N2222, 2N3904).
> - **Lab power supply rails used in KTU labs:** $\pm\mathbf{12\ V}$ or $\mathbf{0\ to\ 12\ V}$ single supply.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Topology Recap and Small-Signal Operation

A **BJT cascode** uses two nominally identical NPN transistors Q1 (CE) and Q2 (CB) sharing the same **DC collector current** $I_C$ (set by the bias network). The AC small-signal path is:

$$
v_{in} \;\xrightarrow{\text{base of Q1}}\; i_b \;\xrightarrow{\cdot\beta}\; i_c = g_m v_{be1} \;\xrightarrow{\text{into Q2's emitter}}\; \text{re-emitted at Q2's collector}
$$

Because Q2's base is **AC-grounded**, its emitter presents a resistance of approximately $r_e = 1/g_m$ looking into it. Therefore, the AC load on Q1's collector is **only $\sim 1/g_m$** (a few ohms to tens of ohms), not the full $R_C$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Walk-Through

1. **Input Resistance** — Looking into the base of Q1 with Q2's CB loading the collector:
   
   $$R_{in} = r_{\pi 1} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$$

2. **Voltage Gain of Q1 (CE section)** — Since Q1's collector load is only $r_{e2} = 1/g_m$ (a CB input is essentially $r_e$ in the small-signal model):
   
   $$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} \cdot (r_{e2} \parallel r_{o1}) \approx -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \approx -1 \quad (\text{since } I_{C1}=I_{C2})$$

3. **Voltage Gain of Q2 (CB section)** — Q2 is a common-base amplifier with collector load $R_C$:
   
   $$A_{v2} = +g_{m2} \cdot (R_C \parallel r_{o2}) \approx g_m R_C$$

4. **Overall Midband Voltage Gain** — Multiply the two cascade gains:
   
   $$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-1) \cdot (g_m R_C) = -g_m R_C$$

5. **Output Resistance** — Excellent, dominated by Q2's $r_{o2}$ in parallel with $R_C$:
   
   $$R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_{o2} \approx R_C$$

## 2.3 Frequency Response — The Cascode's Claim to Fame

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **upper cutoff frequency $f_H$** of a cascode is **not** determined by the Miller-multiplied $C_{\mu}$ (as in a CE), because Q1's voltage gain into Q2's emitter is $\approx -1$, not $-g_m R_C$.

**Effective Miller Capacitance at Q1's input:**
$$C_{M,\text{eff}} = C_{\mu 1}\,(1 - A_{v1}) = C_{\mu 1}\,(1 - (-1)) = 2\,C_{\mu 1}$$

Compare with plain CE:
$$C_{M,\text{CE}} = C_{\mu}(1 - (-g_m R_C)) = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C) \;\;\text{(huge!)}$$

Hence the input pole (and hence $f_H$) of the cascode is **orders of magnitude higher** than a comparable CE.

**Upper Cutoff Frequency (dominant-pole approximation):**
$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \, [\,(R_S \parallel r_{\pi})\, (\,C_{\pi} + 2C_{\mu}\,)\,]}$$

**Lower Cutoff Frequency** — set by coupling/bypass capacitors $C_{in}$, $C_E$, $C_{out}$:
$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2} \quad \text{(using dominant-pole method)}$$

**Bandwidth:**
$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_L \ll f_H)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table for your lab record and exam!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range in KTU Lab |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | **26 mV** |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | 10 – 50 mA/V |
| 3 | Base–emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | 1 – 10 kΩ |
| 4 | Emitter resistance | $r_e = 1 / g_m$ | 20 – 100 Ω |
| 5 | Midband voltage gain | $A_v = -g_m R_C$ | 10 – 200 |
| 6 | Gain in dB | $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | 20 – 46 dB |
| 7 | Input resistance | $R_{in} = r_{\pi 1} \parallel R_B$ | 1 – 5 kΩ |
| 8 | Output resistance | $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_{o2}$ | $\sim R_C$ |
| 9 | Effective Miller cap (cascode) | $C_{M,\text{eff}} = 2C_{\mu}$ | a few pF |
| 10 | Effective Miller cap (CE) | $C_{M,\text{CE}} = C_{\mu}(1+g_m R_C)$ | tens-hundreds of pF |
| 11 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = 1 / (2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq})$ | 0.1 – 10 MHz |
| 12 | Lower cutoff frequency | $f_L \approx 1 / (2\pi R C)$ | 10 – 500 Hz |
| 13 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | ≈ $f_H$ |
| 14 | Gain–Bandwidth Product (const.) | $GBP = \vert A_v \vert \cdot f_H$ | used for comparison |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RF amplifiers** (e.g., 100 MHz front-end in a radio receiver) use the cascode to break the **gain–bandwidth trade-off** of a single CE.
- **Wideband op-amp input stages** (e.g., LM741, NE5532 internal front-end) — cascode gives high DC gain *and* high unity-gain bandwidth.
- **Tape read-head pre-amplifiers**, **oscilloscope front-ends**, and **cable-TV line drivers** all exploit the cascode's low Miller capacitance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Design Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Specification (Typical KTU Lab Problem)

> **Design a BJT cascode amplifier to achieve a midband voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert = 50$ (≈ 34 dB).**
> Given: $V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$. Choose $I_C$ and compute all component values. Then compute $f_L$ and estimate $f_H$.

## 3.2 Design Step 1 — Choose the Quiescent Collector Current $I_C$

A standard KTU lab choice is $I_C = 1\ \text{mA}$ — large enough to give a healthy $g_m$ yet small enough to keep power dissipation low. (This step is a **designer decision**; the rest follows.)

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03846\ \text{S} = 38.46\ \text{mA/V}
$$

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.03846} \approx 2.6\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{0.03846} \approx 26\ \Omega
$$

## 3.3 Design Step 2 — Compute $R_C$ from the Required Gain

The cascode midband gain magnitude is:

$$
\vert A_v \vert = g_m R_C
$$

Solving for $R_C$:

$$
R_C = \frac{\vert A_v \vert}{g_m} = \frac{50}{0.03846} \approx 1.30\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

> [!TIP]
> Use the nearest **standard E12 value**: $R_C = \mathbf{1.2\ k\Omega}$ (slight gain increase) or $\mathbf{1.5\ k\Omega}$ (slight gain decrease). For calculation we will use $R_C = 1.3\ \text{k}\Omega$ to match the design exactly.

Recompute actual gain with $R_C = 1.3\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$
A_v = -g_m R_C = -(0.03846)(1300) = -50.0
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(50) = 33.98 \approx 34\ \text{dB}
$$

## 3.4 Design Step 3 — DC Bias Network for Q1 (Voltage-Divider Bias)

To set $V_B \approx V_{CE,\text{desired}} + V_BE + V_{RE}$, choose $V_{CEQ1} = 2.5\ \text{V}$ (so that Q1 stays in active region even when Q2 also drops ~$V_{CE,sat}+V_BE$ across it).

**Base voltage of Q1:**
$$
V_{B1} = V_{BE} + V_{RE} = 0.7 + V_{RE}
$$

Choose $V_{RE} = 1.5\ \text{V}$ to provide thermal stability. Then:
$$
V_{B1} = 0.7 + 1.5 = 2.2\ \text{V}
$$

**Emitter resistor $R_E$ (shared with Q1's emitter side; here taken as $R_{E1}$):**

$$
R_{E1} = \frac{V_{RE}}{I_E} \approx \frac{V_{RE}}{I_C} = \frac{1.5\ \text{V}}{1\ \text{mA}} = 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Voltage-divider bias resistors** (standard rule: choose divider current $\approx 10 \times I_B$):

$$
I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{1\ \text{mA}}{100} = 10\ \mu\text{A}, \quad I_{\text{div}} = 10 I_B = 100\ \mu\text{A}
$$

$$
R_1 + R_2 = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{\text{div}}} = \frac{12}{100\mu} = 120\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{V_{B1}}{V_{CC}}(R_1+R_2) = \frac{2.2}{12}(120\text{k}) = 22\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_1 = 120\text{k} - 22\text{k} = 98\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Use standard values: $R_1 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 22\ \text{k}\Omega$.

## 3.5 Design Step 4 — DC Bias for Q2 (CB Configuration)

Q2's base is held at a **fixed DC potential** to keep it in the active region. Choose $V_{B2}$ such that:

$$
V_{B2} = V_{CE1} + V_{BE2} = 2.5 + 0.7 = 3.2\ \text{V}
$$

**Resistor divider $R_3$, $R_4$ from $V_{CC}$ to ground** producing $V_{B2} = 3.2\ \text{V}$:

$$
R_4 = \frac{V_{B2}}{V_{CC}}(R_3+R_4) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Choose } R_3 = 75\ \text{k}\Omega,\ R_4 = 27\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Check:
$$
V_{B2} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} = 12 \cdot \frac{27}{75+27} = 12 \cdot \frac{27}{102} \approx 3.18\ \text{V} \;\;(\text{acceptable})
$$

Q2's emitter sits at $V_{B2} - V_{BE} = 3.2 - 0.7 = 2.5\ \text{V}$, which matches the chosen $V_{CE1}$ ✓.

**Q2's collector voltage:**
$$
V_{C2} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (1\text{mA})(1.3\text{k}\Omega) = 10.7\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{CE2} = V_{C2} - V_{E2} = 10.7 - 2.5 = 8.2\ \text{V} \;\;(\text{healthy, in active region} \checkmark)
$$

## 3.6 Design Step 5 — Coupling & Bypass Capacitors (for $f_L \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$)

**Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$** — sets $f_{L1}$ with source resistance $R_S = 50\ \Omega$ (function generator) and $R_{in} = r_{\pi 1} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$:

$$
R_{in,\text{total}} = 2.6\text{k} \parallel 100\text{k} \parallel 22\text{k} \approx 1.97\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{eq,1} = R_S + R_{in,\text{total}} \approx 50 + 1970 = 2020\ \Omega
$$

Choose $f_{L1} = 20\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
C_{in} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{L1} R_{eq,1}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (20)(2020)} \approx 3.94\ \mu\text{F}
$$

Use $C_{in} = 4.7\ \mu\text{F}$ (standard).

**Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** (across $R_{E1}$) — sets $f_{L2}$:

$$
R_{eq,2} = R_{E1} \parallel \left( \frac{r_\pi + R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1} \right) \approx 1.5\text{k} \parallel 20\ \Omega \approx 20\ \Omega
$$

Choose $f_{L2} = 20\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
C_E = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{L2} R_{eq,2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (20)(20)} \approx 398\ \mu\text{F}
$$

Use $C_E = 470\ \mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic, observe polarity).

**Output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$** — sets $f_{L3}$ with $R_{out} = R_C = 1.3\ \text{k}\Omega$ and load $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (oscilloscope input):

$$
R_{eq,3} = R_{out} + R_L = 1.3\text{k} + 10\text{k} = 11.3\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Choose $f_{L3} = 20\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
C_{out} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{L3} R_{eq,3}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (20)(11300)} \approx 0.704\ \mu\text{F}
$$

Use $C_{out} = 1\ \mu\text{F}$.

## 3.7 Design Step 6 — Estimate Upper Cutoff Frequency

Assume typical BC547 small-signal capacitances: $C_\pi = 10\ \text{pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\ \text{pF}$ at the bias point.

$$
C_{M,\text{eff}} = 2C_{\mu 1} = 2 \times 2\ \text{pF} = 4\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{\pi 1} + C_{M,\text{eff}} = 10 + 4 = 14\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
R_{\text{src}}' = (R_S \parallel R_B) \parallel r_{\pi 1} \approx 50\ \Omega \parallel (1.97\ \text{k}\Omega) \approx 48.7\ \Omega
$$

$$
f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{src}}' C_{in,\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (48.7)(14 \times 10^{-12})} \approx 233\ \text{MHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a **practical lab** with breadboard parasitics (5–10 pF stray) and a function generator's $50\ \Omega$ source, the **measured** $f_H$ will be **much lower** (typically **1–10 MHz**). The theoretical 233 MHz is the device-intrinsic limit. For the lab report, document both the theoretical estimate and the measured value.

## 3.8 Final Design Component Summary Table

| Component | Calculated Value | Standard Value Used | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_C$ | 1.30 kΩ | **1.3 kΩ** | Sets gain $A_v$ |
| $R_{E1}$ | 1.5 kΩ | **1.5 kΩ** | Q1 emitter bias stability |
| $R_1$ | 98 kΩ | **100 kΩ** | Upper divider for Q1 base |
| $R_2$ | 22 kΩ | **22 kΩ** | Lower divider for Q1 base |
| $R_3$ | 75 kΩ | **75 kΩ** | Upper divider for Q2 base |
| $R_4$ | 27 kΩ | **27 kΩ** | Lower divider for Q2 base |
| $C_{in}$ | 3.94 µF | **4.7 µF** | Input coupling |
| $C_{out}$ | 0.70 µF | **1 µF** | Output coupling |
| $C_E$ | 398 µF | **470 µF** | Q1 emitter bypass |
| $V_{CC}$ | 12 V | **+12 V** | Supply |
| Transistors | — | **BC547 × 2** (Q1, Q2) | Active devices |

## 3.9 Step-by-Step Frequency-Response Plotting Procedure

1. Set $V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}$, connect all components, verify $V_{CE}$ values with DMM.
2. Apply $V_{in} = 20\ \text{mV}_{pp}$, $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$ at the input. Observe $V_{out}$ on CRO. Compute $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$.
3. Compute $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$.
4. **Sweep frequency** from 10 Hz to 10 MHz in decade steps, recording $V_{out}$ at each frequency.
5. **Normalize** each gain to the midband value: $A_{v,\text{norm}}(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{out,\text{mid}})$.
6. Plot $A_{v,\text{norm}}$ (dB) on the **y-axis** vs $\log_{10}(f)$ on the **x-axis** (semi-log graph).
7. Read off $f_L$ (–3 dB below midband on the left) and $f_H$ (–3 dB below midband on the right).
8. Calculate Bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the plot:** The midband gain plateau is **34 dB** ($\vert A_v \vert = 50$). The lower cutoff $f_L \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$ marks the –3 dB point on the rising left edge. The upper cutoff $f_H \approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{MHz}$ marks the –3 dB point on the falling right edge. The slope of the roll-offs is **±20 dB/decade** for a single-pole response.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define a cascode amplifier. Why is it preferred over a single-stage common-emitter (CE) amplifier at high frequencies? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A cascode amplifier is a two-transistor configuration in which a **common-emitter (CE) stage** drives a **common-base (CB) stage**, with the collector of Q1 tied to the emitter of Q2. The input is applied at the base of Q1 and the output is taken at the collector of Q2.

It is preferred at high frequencies because in a plain CE amplifier, the **Miller effect** multiplies the base–collector capacitance $C_\mu$ by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_C)$, creating a dominant low-pass pole that drastically reduces the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. In a cascode, Q1's collector sees only the very low input impedance of the CB stage ($\approx 1/g_m$), so the effective gain of Q1 is close to **–1**, and the Miller multiplication reduces to just a factor of 2. This pushes $f_H$ up by roughly **5× to 10×** without sacrificing the midband voltage gain $|A_v| = g_m R_C$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the expression for the midband voltage gain of a BJT cascode amplifier and identify the factors that determine its value. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The midband voltage gain of a cascode amplifier is:

$$
A_v = -g_m R_C
$$

The factors that determine its magnitude are:

1. **Transconductance $g_m$** — depends on the quiescent collector current $I_C$ as $g_m = I_C / V_T$. Higher $I_C$ → higher $g_m$ → higher gain.
2. **Collector load $R_C$** — larger $R_C$ → higher gain, but limited by the available $V_{CC}$ headroom and the desired $V_{CEQ2}$.
3. **Output resistance $r_{o2}$** of Q2 (Early effect) — sets the upper bound on the effective load $R_C \parallel r_{o2}$.

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output (same as a CE stage).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **often forget the negative sign** of $A_v$ (cascode is inverting like CE).
> - **Bias resistor values** are commonly wrong because students confuse Q1's base voltage ($V_{BE}+V_{RE}$) with Q2's base voltage ($V_{CE1}+V_{BE2}$).
> - In the **frequency-response plot**, students draw the roll-off slope incorrectly; the slope is **±20 dB/decade** (not 40 dB) for a single dominant pole.
> - Coupling capacitor polarity is frequently **reversed** on electrolytics — make sure the positive terminal faces the side with higher DC potential.
> - Always quote **units** ($g_m$ in mA/V or S, $f$ in Hz, gain in dB).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Design a BJT cascode amplifier using two identical NPN transistors (β = 100, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$) to achieve a midband voltage gain of 50. Use $V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}$ and $I_C = 1\ \text{mA}$. Determine the values of $R_C$, the bias resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, and the emitter resistor $R_E$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the designed cascode, explain the procedure to obtain the **frequency response** in the lab. Sketch the expected Bode plot and mark $f_L$, $f_H$, midband gain, and the slopes of the roll-off regions. State two advantages of cascode over a single CE amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute $g_m$** (1 mark)

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1\ \text{mA}}{26\ \text{mV}} = 38.46\ \text{mA/V}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $R_C$** (1 mark)

$$
R_C = \frac{\vert A_v \vert}{g_m} = \frac{50}{38.46 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1.30\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3: Choose $V_{CE1}$ and $V_{RE}$** (1 mark)

Choose $V_{CE1} = 2.5\ \text{V}$ and $V_{RE} = 1.5\ \text{V}$ for thermal stability.

**Step 4: Emitter resistor $R_E$** (1 mark)

$$
R_E = \frac{V_{RE}}{I_E} \approx \frac{1.5\ \text{V}}{1\ \text{mA}} = 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5: Q1 base voltage and divider $R_1, R_2$** (1.5 marks)

$$
V_{B1} = V_{BE} + V_{RE} = 0.7 + 1.5 = 2.2\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{1\ \text{mA}}{100} = 10\ \mu\text{A}, \quad I_{\text{div}} = 10 I_B = 100\ \mu\text{A}
$$

$$
R_1 + R_2 = \frac{12}{100\ \mu\text{A}} = 120\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{2.2}{12} \times 120\text{k} = 22\ \text{k}\Omega, \quad R_1 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 6: Q2 base divider $R_3, R_4$** (1.5 marks)

$$
V_{B2} = V_{CE1} + V_{BE2} = 2.5 + 0.7 = 3.2\ \text{V}
$$

Choosing divider current 100 µA: $R_3 + R_4 = 120\ \text{k}\Omega$.

$$
R_4 = \frac{3.2}{12} \times 120\text{k} = 32\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;(\text{use } 33\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ standard}), \quad R_3 = 87\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;(\text{use } 82\ \text{k}\Omega)
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Frequency-Response Lab Procedure** (3 marks):

1. Power the circuit with $V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}$ and verify all DC node voltages with a DMM.
2. Apply a small sinusoidal input $V_{in} = 20\ \text{mV}_{pp}$ at $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$ (midband) using a function generator.
3. Measure $V_{out}$ on the CRO. Compute $|A_v| = V_{out}/V_{in}$ and $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}|A_v|$. Confirm the designed value (≈ 34 dB).
4. **Sweep** the input frequency from 10 Hz to 10 MHz in decade steps (10, 30, 100, 300, 1 k, 3 k, 10 k, … Hz). At each frequency, hold $V_{in}$ constant and record $V_{out}$.
5. Convert each reading to gain in dB.
6. Plot **gain (dB) vs $\log_{10}(f)$** on a **semi-log graph sheet**.

**Expected Bode Plot Description** (2 marks):

- Midband plateau at **+34 dB** flat from ≈ 500 Hz to ≈ 1 MHz.
- **Lower roll-off** below 100 Hz at a slope of **+20 dB/decade** (gain rises with frequency).
- **Upper roll-off** above 1 MHz at a slope of **–20 dB/decade** (gain falls with frequency).
- Mark $f_L \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$ and $f_H \approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{MHz}$ at the –3 dB points.

**Two Advantages of Cascode over CE** (2 marks):

1. **Higher upper cutoff frequency $f_H$** — the Miller effect is suppressed (effective Miller capacitance drops from $C_\mu(1+g_m R_C)$ to $2C_\mu$), giving 5×–10× wider bandwidth at the same gain.
2. **Better high-frequency reverse isolation** — the CB stage of Q2 prevents output signal from feeding back to the input through $C_{\mu 1}$, improving stability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the **midband voltage gain** of a BJT cascode amplifier. Show clearly why the gain magnitude equals $g_m R_C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cascode amplifier uses two BC547 transistors with $I_C = 0.5\ \text{mA}$, $\beta = 120$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$, and $R_C = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$. Compute: (i) the transconductance $g_m$, (ii) the midband voltage gain $A_v$ and $A_{v,\text{dB}}$, (iii) the base–emitter resistance $r_\pi$, and (iv) the input resistance $R_{in}$ assuming a base-bias resistor $R_B = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$. Comment on the suitability of this design for audio-frequency applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Small-signal model of cascode** (3 marks):

The cascode is split into two stages with the **hybrid-π model** of each BJT. The output of Q1's current source $g_{m1} v_{be1}$ flows into the emitter of Q2. Since Q2's base is **AC-grounded**, its emitter looks like a resistance $r_{e2} = 1/g_{m2}$ in parallel with Q2's $r_{\pi 2}$. For matched Q1 and Q2 ($g_{m1}=g_{m2}$), the load on Q1's collector is $1/g_{m2}$.

**Voltage gain of Q1 (CE section)** (2 marks):

$$
A_{v1} = \frac{v_{c1}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1} \cdot (1/g_{m2}) = -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -1 \;\;(\text{for } g_{m1}=g_{m2})
$$

**Voltage gain of Q2 (CB section)** (1 mark):

$$
A_{v2} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{c1}} = g_{m2} \cdot (R_C \parallel r_{o2}) \approx g_{m2} R_C
$$

**Overall gain** (1 mark):

$$
A_v = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-1)(g_m R_C) = -g_m R_C
$$

This shows that the cascode's midband gain is **identical in magnitude to a single CE stage**, but with the **Miller effect dramatically reduced**, leading to a much higher $f_H$.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $I_C = 0.5\ \text{mA}$, $\beta = 120$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$, $R_C = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**(i) Transconductance** (1.5 marks):

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 19.23\ \text{mA/V}
$$

**(ii) Midband voltage gain** (1.5 marks):

$$
A_v = -g_m R_C = -(19.23 \times 10^{-3})(4700) = -90.38
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(90.38) = 39.12\ \text{dB}
$$

**(iii) Base–emitter resistance $r_\pi$** (1.5 marks):

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{19.23 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 6.24\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**(iv) Input resistance $R_{in}$** (1.5 marks):

$$
R_{in} = r_\pi \parallel R_B = 6.24\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 50\ \text{k}\Omega = \frac{6.24 \times 50}{6.24 + 50} = \frac{312}{56.24} \approx 5.55\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Comment on suitability for audio applications** (1 mark):

With a midband gain of ≈ 39 dB and an estimated $f_H$ in the multi-MHz range, the cascode is **highly suitable for audio-frequency (20 Hz – 20 kHz) applications**. The gain of 90 (39 dB) is more than adequate for audio pre-amplification stages, and the bandwidth easily covers the entire audio spectrum with a large safety margin. The high input impedance (~5.5 kΩ) is also acceptable for typical audio source coupling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Cascode Amplifier**

- **🔹 Definition:** A cascode = **CE (Q1) + CB (Q2)** stacked; input at Q1 base, output at Q2 collector. **Not a Darlington.**
- **🔹 Midband Gain:** $\vert A_v \vert = g_m R_C = \dfrac{I_C R_C}{V_T}$ — **identical to a plain CE**.
- **🔹 Miller Effect:** In CE, $C_M = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$ (huge); in cascode, $C_M = 2C_\mu$ (tiny). **This is the cascode's main advantage.**
- **🔹 Q1's Voltage Gain:** $\approx -1$ (because Q1's collector load is only $1/g_m$ of Q2's CB input).
- **🔹 Q2's Voltage Gain:** $\approx +g_m R_C$ (CB stage is non-inverting).
- **🔹 Q-Point of Q1:** $V_{CE1} \approx 2$–$3\ \text{V}$ — leave enough headroom for Q1 to stay in active region.
- **🔹 Q-Point of Q2:** $V_{CE2} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{B2} + V_{BE2}$ — should be 3–8 V for healthy operation.
- **🔹 Bias Network:** Q1's base at $V_{BE} + V_{RE}$; Q2's base at $V_{CE1} + V_{BE2}$ (use a separate divider for Q2).
- **🔹 Frequency Response Plot:** $f_L \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$ (set by coupling/bypass caps), $f_H \approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{MHz}$ (set by $C_\pi + 2C_\mu$ on Q1's input).
- **🔹 Slope of Roll-off:** $\pm 20\ \text{dB/decade}$ for a single dominant pole.
- **🔹 Bode Plot Axes:** **Gain in dB (linear y-axis)** vs **Frequency in Hz (log x-axis)** — use **semi-log graph paper**.
- **🔹 Measurement Procedure:** Fix $V_{in}$ amplitude, sweep frequency, record $V_{out}$, compute dB gain, plot.
- **🔹 Common Lab Pitfalls:** Reversed electrolytic capacitor polarity; forgetting bypass across $R_E$; using a single ground reference for both emitter and base dividers (causes oscillation); not terminating the function generator with 50 Ω.
- **🔹 Real-World Use:** RF front-ends, op-amp input stages, wideband oscilloscope pre-amplifiers, cable-TV line drivers.
- **🔹 Key Formula to Memorize:** $\boxed{A_v = -g_m R_C, \quad g_m = I_C / V_T, \quad f_H \propto \dfrac{1}{R_{src} (C_\pi + 2C_\mu)}}$

> **🎓 Good luck with your KTU lab exam! Remember to record both theoretical and measured values, calculate the percentage error, and include a neatly labelled Bode plot in your lab report.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series and Voltage Series) - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series and Voltage Series) - Design for a Specific Voltage Gain and Plot Frequency Response Characteristics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Feedback Amplifier** is a two-port linear network in which a fraction of the output signal is returned (fed back) to the input port through a passive sampling/mixing network, in order to precisely control the amplifier's **gain, input resistance, output resistance, bandwidth, and linearity**. The feedback may be either **positive (regenerative)** or **negative (degenerative/shunt)**; the latter is the universal choice in analog design because it trades raw gain for predictability. The two configurations covered in this experiment are: | Configuration | Samples Output | Mixes at Input | Topology | Effect on Rin / Rout | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Current Series Feedback (CSFA)** | Output **current** | Feedback in **series** with input voltage | Series–Series | **Rin ↑ , Rout ↑** | | **Voltage Series Feedback (VSFA)** | Output **voltage** | Feedback in **series** with input voltage | Shunt–Series | **Rin ↑ , Rout ↓** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus explicitly demands that the student must *design* the amplifier for a *specific voltage gain* (typically **Av = 10**) and then *plot the frequency response* (low-cutoff, high-cutoff, and 3 dB bandwidth) — both **with and without feedback** — to experimentally verify the **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)** invariance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of an Amplifier" Imagine driving a car at **150 km/h** on a winding road (this is a high-gain amplifier — fast, but unstable, dangerous, and uncontrollable). Now, by **lightly gripping the steering wheel** (the feedback network), the car settles to a comfortable, predictable **80 km/h** and reacts calmly to bumps and turns. You *lost raw speed* but **gained control, stability, and comfort**. * **Car speed = voltage gain of the amplifier (Av)** * **Steering wheel grip = feedback factor (β)** * **Loss of speed with steering = gain reduction (1 + Aβ)** * **Better cornering & smoother ride = increased bandwidth, reduced distortion, stabilised Rin/Rout** The **steeper** you grip (more feedback), the **slower but smoother** the ride — yet the *distance covered per unit time* (Gain × Bandwidth) remains almost **constant**. This is the celebrated **Gain–Bandwidth Product invariance principle**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant / Benchmark:** The dimensionless quantity **(1 + Aβ)** is called the **desensitivity factor (D)**. All closed-loop parameters are scaled by D: $A_f = A/D$, $R_{inf} = R_{in}(1+A\beta)$, $R_{of} = R_{out}/(1+A\beta)$, and $BW_f = BW \times (1+A\beta)$. ## 1.3 Classification of Feedback Topologies (Quick Reference) The four canonical feedback topologies, identified by **what is sampled** and **how it is mixed**, are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sampling}&\Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Voltage (Shunt at output node)} \rightarrow \text{Voltage Feedback} \\ \text{Current (Series in output loop)} \rightarrow \text{Current Feedback} \end{cases} \\ \text{Mixing}&\Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Shunt (parallel) at input node} \rightarrow \text{Parallel Mixing} \\ \text{Series in input loop} \rightarrow \text{Series Mixing} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic to remember the effect on resistance:** *"Shunt sampling ⇒ low Rout; Series mixing ⇒ high Rin."* — The "diagonal rule" of Bode: **Voltage–Voltage** and **Current–Current** feedback preserve resistance polarity, while **cross configurations** swap them. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Feedback Equation Consider a basic amplifier of open-loop gain $A$ with a feedback network of transfer ratio $\beta$. The closed-loop transfer function is: $$ A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} $$ The product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain** and $(1 + A\beta)$ is the **return difference / desensitivity factor**. The feedback is **negative** when $\beta$ is chosen so that $A\beta > 0$ (i.e., the feedback signal *opposes* the input). > [!NOTE] > **The Four Universal Effects of Negative Feedback:** > 1. **Gain desensitisation:** $A_f$ depends mainly on $\beta$ (a passive, stable network), not on transistor parameters. > 2. **Bandwidth extension:** $BW_f = BW(1 + A\beta)$. > 3. **Gain–Bandwidth Product is invariant:** $A \cdot BW \approx A_f \cdot BW_f$. > 4. **Nonlinearity / distortion reduction by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$.** ## 2.2 Why "Series Mixing" → Input Resistance Increases When the feedback voltage $V_f$ is injected in **series** (opposition) with the source $V_s$, KVL at the input loop gives: $$ V_s = V_{in} + V_f = I_{in}\,R_{in} + \beta \cdot V_{out} $$ The same input current $I_{in}$ now produces a *larger* $V_s$, which means the **effective input resistance is multiplied**: $$ R_{inf} = R_{in}\,(1 + A\beta) \quad \uparrow \uparrow $$ ## 2.3 Why "Current Sampling" → Output Resistance Increases When the output current $I_{out}$ is sampled, the feedback attempts to **hold $I_{out}$ constant** against load changes. A *stiffer current source* is one with a *higher* output resistance: $$ R_{of} = R_{out}\,(1 + A\beta) \quad \uparrow \uparrow $$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are the de-facto KTU 2024 valuation key for the design part of this experiment.** | # | Parameter | Current Series Feedback (CSFA) | Voltage Series Feedback (VSFA) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Open-loop gain $A$ | $A_v = -\dfrac{\beta_{ac} R_C}{h_{ie} + (1+\beta_{ac})R_E'}$ (unbypassed $R_E'$ only) | $A_v = -\dfrac{\beta_{ac} R_C}{h_{ie}}$ (full bypass) | | 2 | Approx. closed-loop gain $A_{vf}$ | $A_{vf} \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{R_E'}$ (when $\beta_{ac} R_E' \gg h_{ie}$) | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta_f}$ | | 3 | Feedback factor $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_E'}{R_C}$ (dimensionless, current-to-voltage sense) | $\beta_f = \dfrac{R_{F2}}{R_{F1}+R_{F2}}$ (resistive divider) | | 4 | Input resistance $R_{in}$ | $R_{in} = h_{ie} + (1+\beta_{ac})R_E'$ | $R_{inf} = R_{in}(1+A\beta_f)$ | | 5 | Output resistance $R_{out}$ | $R_{out(C)} \approx R_C$ (collector side) | $R_{out(F)} = \dfrac{R_C \,\vert\vert\, R_L}{1+A\beta_f}$ | | 6 | Bandwidth | $BW_f = BW_{ol}\,(1+A\beta_f)$ | $BW_f = BW_{ol}\,(1+A\beta_f)$ | | 7 | Lower cutoff $f_L$ | $f_L' = \dfrac{f_L}{(1+A\beta_f)}$ (decreases ⇒ better LF) | same | | 8 | Upper cutoff $f_H$ | $f_H' = f_H\,(1+A\beta_f)$ (increases) | same | | 9 | Desensitivity $D$ | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary condition for CSFA approximation:** The simplification $A_{vf} \approx -R_C / R_E'$ is valid only when $(1+\beta_{ac})R_E' \geq 10\,h_{ie}$ — i.e., the emitter degeneration resistor dominates. This is the regime you **must** ensure when designing for $A_{vf} = 10$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Current Series Feedback** is the heart of **operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)**, **current mirrors**, and **transimpedance front-ends** in instrumentation, where the load is a *current-driven* device (e.g., a solenoid, an LED string, a deflection coil in a CRT). * **Voltage Series Feedback** is the architecture of every **op-amp voltage follower** (unity-gain buffer), **audio preamplifier** (RIAA stages), and **ECG/EEG front-end** — anywhere a *stable, predictable voltage gain* and *low output impedance* are required. * **KTU Real-life example:** The **LM741 op-amp** uses voltage-series feedback internally in its second stage, while the **LM317 voltage regulator** uses current-series feedback across its pass transistor to maintain constant load current. # 3. Step-by-Step Design Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Design Specification (KTU 2024 Typical Lab Statement) > **Design Statement:** Design a **single-stage BJT CE amplifier with Current-Series Feedback** to deliver a *mid-band voltage gain of $|A_{vf}| = 10$* into a load $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, then design a **two-stage Voltage-Series Feedback amplifier** to deliver the *same* $|A_{vf}| = 10$ and compare frequency responses. Assume the following KTU-standard component/scenario data: | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Supply voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **+12** | V | | Quiescent collector current | $I_C$ | **1** | mA | | Quiescent collector–emitter voltage | $V_{CE}$ | **6** | V | | Transistor DC current gain | $\beta_{dc}$ (or $h_{FE}$) | **200** | — | | Transistor small-signal $h_{FE}$ | $\beta_{ac}$ (or $h_{fe}$) | **220** | — | | Transistor input resistance | $h_{ie}$ | **2.0** | kΩ | | Load resistance | $R_L$ | **4.7** | kΩ | | Source resistance | $R_S$ | **600** | Ω | | Desired closed-loop gain | $\vert A_{vf}\vert$ | **10** | V/V | | Lower cutoff w/o feedback | $f_L$ | **100** | Hz | | Upper cutoff w/o feedback | $f_H$ | **500** | kHz | | Coupling capacitor | $C_{in}, C_{out}$ | **10** | µF | | Emitter bypass capacitor | $C_E$ | **100** | µF | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these numbers?** $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$ is the most common lab rail; $I_C = 1\text{ mA}$ gives $g_m = 38.5\text{ mA/V}$ and $h_{ie} \approx 5.6\text{ k}\Omega$ analytically, but the lab uses a measured $h_{ie} \approx 2\text{ k}\Omega$ (typical of BC547 with $I_C = 1\text{ mA}$ at $V_{CE} = 6\text{ V}$). Always use **measured** $h_{ie}$ in the final design report. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Design of the Current-Series Feedback Amplifier (CSFA) ### Step 1 — Choose the Collector Resistor $R_C$ The output is taken from the collector through a coupling capacitor to $R_L$. The **effective a.c. load** is: $$ R_{ac} = R_C \,\vert\vert\, R_L $$ For maximum symmetrical swing, choose the d.c. drop across $R_C$ such that $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2 = 6\text{ V}$: $$ R_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{I_C} = \frac{12 - 6}{1\text{ mA}} = 6\text{ k}\Omega $$ Effective load: $$ R_{ac} = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L} = \frac{6 \times 4.7}{6 + 4.7} = \frac{28.2}{10.7} \approx 2.64\text{ k}\Omega $$ ### Step 2 — Determine the Unbypassed Emitter Resistor $R_E'$ for the Required Gain The exact closed-loop gain (with the emitter resistor $R_E'$ **not** bypassed) is: $$ A_{vf} = -\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{h_{ie} + (1+\beta_{ac})R_E'} $$ Solve for $R_E'$: $$ R_E' = \frac{1}{1+\beta_{ac}}\left[\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{\vert A_{vf}\vert} - h_{ie}\right] $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ \begin{aligned} R_E' &= \frac{1}{221}\left[\frac{220 \times 2.64\text{ k}\Omega}{10} - 2.0\text{ k}\Omega\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{221}\bigl[58.08 - 2.0\bigr]\text{ k}\Omega \\ &= \frac{56.08}{221}\text{ k}\Omega \\ &\approx 0.254\text{ k}\Omega \\ &\approx 254\text{ }\Omega \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical rounding:** Choose the nearest **E12 standard value** — $R_E' = 270\text{ }\Omega$. Recompute the achieved gain: > $A_{vf} = -\frac{220 \times 2.64}{2.0 + 221 \times 0.27} = -\frac{580.8}{61.67} \approx -9.42\text{ V/V}$ — a 6 % deviation, well within the **±10 % KTU tolerance band**. ### Step 3 — Determine the Total Emitter Resistor for Bias Stability For thermal stability, the d.c. emitter voltage is chosen as $V_E = V_{CC}/10 = 1.2\text{ V}$ (a KTU-standard rule of thumb): $$ R_{E(total)} = \frac{V_E}{I_E} \approx \frac{1.2\text{ V}}{1\text{ mA}} = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega $$ Therefore the **bypassed** portion is: $$ R_E = R_{E(total)} - R_E' = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega - 0.27\text{ k}\Omega = 0.93\text{ k}\Omega $$ Rounded E12 value: $R_E = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega$. Total emitter resistance: $R_{E(total)} = 1.0 + 0.27 = 1.27\text{ k}\Omega$. ### Step 4 — Bias-Divider Resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ Using the **approximate (β-independent) bias rule** with $V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 1.2 + 0.7 = 1.9\text{ V}$ and choosing the divider current as $I_2 = 10 \cdot I_B = 10 \cdot (I_C / \beta_{dc}) = 10 \times (1/200)\text{ mA} = 0.05\text{ mA} = 50\text{ }\mu\text{A}$: $$ R_2 = \frac{V_B}{I_2} = \frac{1.9\text{ V}}{50\text{ }\mu\text{A}} = 38\text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ R_1 = \frac{V_{CC} - V_B}{I_2 + I_B} = \frac{12 - 1.9}{(50 + 5)\text{ }\mu\text{A}} = \frac{10.1}{55\text{ }\mu\text{A}} \approx 184\text{ k}\Omega $$ Round to E12: $R_1 = 180\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 39\text{ k}\Omega$. ### Step 5 — Bypass Capacitor $C_E$ Choose $X_{C_E} \leq R_E'/10$ at the lower cutoff $f_L = 100\text{ Hz}$: $$ C_E \geq \frac{1}{2\pi f_L (R_E'/10)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 100 \times 27} = 58.9\text{ }\mu\text{F} $$ Choose standard **100 µF / 25 V** electrolytic. ### Step 6 — Verify the Feedback Factor and Desensitivity The feedback factor for CSFA is: $$ \beta = \frac{R_E'}{R_{ac}} = \frac{0.27}{2.64} \approx 0.1023 $$ The open-loop gain (with $R_E$ **fully bypassed** by $C_E$) is: $$ A_v = -\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{h_{ie}} = -\frac{220 \times 2.64}{2.0} = -290.4\text{ V/V} $$ The desensitivity factor: $$ D = 1 + A\beta = 1 + 290.4 \times 0.1023 \approx 30.7 $$ Predicted closed-loop gain: $$ A_{vf} = \frac{A}{D} = \frac{-290.4}{30.7} \approx -9.46\text{ V/V} \quad (\text{close to design } -10) $$ ### Step 7 — Predicted Bandwidth $$ \begin{aligned} f_{L(\text{feedback})} &= \frac{f_L}{D} = \frac{100}{30.7} \approx 3.26\text{ Hz} \\ f_{H(\text{feedback})} &= f_H \times D = 500\text{ kHz} \times 30.7 \approx 15.35\text{ MHz} \\ BW_f &= f_{H(\text{fb})} - f_{L(\text{fb})} \approx 15.35\text{ MHz} \end{aligned} $$ The **Gain–Bandwidth Product** is conserved: $$ A \cdot BW \approx 290.4 \times 500\text{ kHz} \approx 145.2\text{ MHz} \approx A_f \cdot BW_f = 9.46 \times 15.35\text{ MHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** A 30× increase in desensitivity produces a **30× increase in bandwidth** but a **30× decrease in gain** — the canonical *Gain–Bandwidth Trade-off*. This single result is worth full marks in the KTU design question. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Design of the Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier (VSFA) ### Step 1 — Two-Stage CE Cascade We use **two identical CE stages** cascaded via a coupling capacitor. Each stage has the same bias as the CSFA. Open-loop gain of each stage: $$ A_{v1} = A_{v2} = -\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{h_{ie}} = -290.4\text{ V/V} $$ Total open-loop gain (assuming the second stage does not load the first appreciably, $R_{in2} \gg R_{C1}$): $$ A = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-290.4)^2 \approx 84{,}330\text{ V/V} \quad \approx 98.5\text{ dB} $$ ### Step 2 — Design the Feedback Divider for $A_{vf} = 10$ We require: $$ A_{vf} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta_f} = 10 $$ Solve for $\beta_f$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + A\beta_f &= \frac{A}{A_{vf}} = \frac{84{,}330}{10} = 8{,}433 \\ \beta_f &= \frac{8{,}432}{84{,}330} \approx 0.100 \end{aligned} $$ A $\beta_f$ of about 0.1 is conveniently realised by a **resistive divider** $R_F$ and $R_{E2}'$ where: $$ \beta_f = \frac{R_{E2}'}{R_F + R_{E2}'} $$ Choose $R_{E2}' = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ (a small unbypassed emitter resistor on the **second** stage). Then: $$ \begin{aligned} R_F + R_{E2}' &= \frac{R_{E2}'}{\beta_f} = \frac{1\text{ k}\Omega}{0.1} = 10\text{ k}\Omega \\ R_F &= 10\text{ k}\Omega - 1\text{ k}\Omega = 9\text{ k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ Round to E12: $R_F = 9.1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{E2}' = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega$. ### Step 3 — Check the Desensitivity & Bandwidth $$ D = 1 + A\beta_f = 8{,}433 $$ $$ f_{L(\text{fb})} = \frac{100}{8{,}433} \approx 0.0119\text{ Hz} \quad (\text{essentially d.c.-coupled behaviour}) $$ $$ f_{H(\text{fb})} = 500\text{ kHz} \times 8{,}433 \approx 4.22\text{ GHz} \quad (\text{limited in practice by parasitic } C) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lab Reality:** The predicted $f_H$ of 4.2 GHz is unattainable with discrete transistors. The *measured* $f_H$ is limited by the **Miller effect** and **parasitic capacitances** of the BC547 (~20 pF), giving a practical $f_H \approx 2$–$5\text{ MHz}$ per stage. This discrepancy is **expected** and is itself a key KTU discussion point. --- ## 3.4 Design Summary — Final Component Values | Component | CSFA (Stage 1) | VSFA (Stage 2 of cascade) | |---|---|---| | $R_C$ | **6.0 kΩ** | **6.0 kΩ** | | $R_E$ (bypassed) | **1.0 kΩ** | **0 Ω (fully bypassed)** | | $R_E'$ (unbypassed) | **270 Ω** | **1.0 kΩ** | | $R_1$ | **180 kΩ** | **180 kΩ** | | $R_2$ | **39 kΩ** | **39 kΩ** | | $R_F$ | — | **9.1 kΩ** | | $C_{in}$ | **10 µF** | **10 µF** | | $C_E$ (bypass) | **100 µF** | **100 µF** | | $C_{out}$ | **10 µF** | **10 µF** | --- ## 3.5 Frequency Response Plotting Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Standard) 1. **Without feedback** — bypass all emitter resistors, sweep the function generator from **10 Hz to 1 MHz** in decade steps, record $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$ (use an oscilloscope or true-RMS multimeter), tabulate the gain $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$. 2. **With feedback** — unbypass $R_E'$ (or connect $R_F$), repeat the sweep. 3. **Convert** each gain to **dB**: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$. 4. **Plot** $A_{dB}$ vs. $\log_{10}(f)$ on **semi-log graph paper** (3 decades: 10 Hz – 1 MHz; 5 decades if available). 5. **Mark** the two asymptotes: low-frequency −20 dB/decade roll-off, high-frequency −20 dB/decade roll-off, and the mid-band plateau. 6. **Read off** $f_L$ and $f_H$ (the 3 dB-down points relative to the mid-band gain) and compute $\text{BW} = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$. > [!NOTE] > **Expected observation (KTU marking key):** The with-feedback curve has a *flatter mid-band*, *lower gain* by $\approx 20 \log_{10}(1+A\beta)\text{ dB}$, *lower $f_L$*, and *higher $f_H$* — exactly the four-fold improvement predicted by theory. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use CSFA vs VSFA | Criterion | Choose CSFA | Choose VSFA | |---|---|---| | Load is current-driven (solenoid, LED, coil) | ✔ | ✗ | | Load is voltage-driven (audio speaker, A/D input) | ✗ | ✔ | | Need to step up source impedance (e.g., crystal mic) | ✔ (boosts $R_{in}$) | ✔ (also boosts $R_{in}$) | | Need to drive a low-impedance cable | ✗ (boosts $R_{out}$) | ✔ (lowers $R_{out}$) | | Single-transistor simplicity | ✔ | ✗ (needs 2+ stages for high $A$) | | Transducer near the source (e.g., antenna) | ✗ | ✔ (high $R_{in}$ + low $R_{out}$) | --- # 4. The trade-off is a loss in raw gain — but for predictable, reproducible engineering design, that trade is almost always worth it. --- ## 4.4 Frequency-Response Sketch (Expected Plot) ```mermaid graph LR A[10 Hz
