KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S8 ECE]` **Q: Distinguish between voltage-series and current-series feedback amplifiers. Mention the effect of each on input and output resistance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Voltage-Series (VSFA) | Current-Series (CSFA) | |---|---|---| | **Sampled quantity** | Output *voltage* | Output *current* | | **Mixing at input** | In *series* with $V_s$ | In *series* with $V_s$ | | **Effect on $R_{in}$** | $R_{inf} = R_{in}(1+A\beta)$ — **increases** | $R_{inf} = R_{in}(1+A\beta)$ — **increases** | | **Effect on $R_{out}$** | $R_{of} = R_{out}/(1+A\beta)$ — **decreases** | $R_{of} = R_{out}(1+A\beta)$ — **increases** | | **Stabilises** | Voltage gain $A_v$ | Transconductance $G_m = I_{out}/V_{in}$ | | **Typical use** | Voltage amplifiers, op-amp non-inverting stages | Current sources, OTAs, transconductance stages | > *(1 mark for the sampling/mixing classification, 1 mark for input resistance effect, 1 mark for output resistance effect.)* --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, S8 ECE]` **Q: State and prove the Gain–Bandwidth Product invariance principle of a negative-feedback amplifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** The product of the closed-loop mid-band gain and the closed-loop bandwidth is approximately equal to the product of the open-loop mid-band gain and the open-loop bandwidth; i.e., the **GBP is constant**, independent of the feedback factor. **Proof:** For a single-pole amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and dominant upper-cutoff frequency $f_H = 1/(2\pi\tau)$: $$ A(f) = \frac{A}{1 + jf/f_H} $$ With feedback factor $\beta$: $$ A_f(f) = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,\frac{1}{1 + j\,\dfrac{f}{f_H(1 + A\beta)}} $$ The new upper-cutoff is $f_{H,f} = f_H(1+A\beta)$, and the new mid-band gain is $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$. Multiplying: $$ A_f \cdot f_{H,f} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} \cdot f_H (1+A\beta) = A \cdot f_H $$ Hence the product is **invariant**. (3 marks) > [!NOTE] > **Comment:** A similar derivation shows the *lower* cutoff scales as $f_{L,f} = f_L/(1+A\beta)$, so the *net* bandwidth $BW_f = f_{H,f} - f_{L,f} \approx f_{H,f}$ is enhanced by $(1+A\beta)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long-Answer Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S8 ECE]` **Q: Design a single-stage BJT CE amplifier with current-series feedback to deliver a closed-loop voltage gain of $\vert A_{vf}\vert = 10$ into a load $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ from a 12 V supply. The transistor BC547 has $h_{fe} = 220$, $h_{ie} = 2.0\text{ k}\Omega$. Plot its frequency response with and without feedback and comment on the Gain–Bandwidth Product.** **Model Solution:** **(a) Design for $A_{vf} = 10$ (7 marks)** 1. **Quiescent point selection:** Choose $V_{CE} = 6\text{ V}$, $I_C = 1\text{ mA}$ (mid-point bias for maximum symmetrical swing). 2. **Collector resistor:** $$R_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{I_C} = \frac{12 - 6}{1\text{ mA}} = 6\text{ k}\Omega$$ 3. **Effective a.c. load:** $$R_{ac} = R_C \,\vert\vert\, R_L = \frac{6 \times 4.7}{6+4.7} = 2.64\text{ k}\Omega$$ 4. **Unbypassed emitter resistor for $|A_{vf}| = 10$:** $$\begin{aligned} R_E' &= \frac{1}{1+\beta_{ac}}\left[\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{\vert A_{vf}\vert} - h_{ie}\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{221}\left[\frac{220 \times 2.64}{10} - 2.0\right]\text{ k}\Omega \\ &= \frac{56.08}{221} = 0.254\text{ k}\Omega \approx 270\text{ }\Omega \text{ (E12)} \end{aligned}$$ 5. **Total emitter resistor (for thermal stability):** $$R_{E(tot)} = \frac{V_{CC}/10}{I_E} \approx \frac{1.2}{1\text{ mA}} = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega \approx 1.0\text{ k}\Omega + 0.27\text{ k}\Omega$$ 6. **Bias divider:** With $I_2 = 10 I_B = 50\text{ }\mu\text{A}$, $V_B = 1.9\text{ V}$: $$R_1 = \frac{V_{CC} - V_B}{I_2 + I_B} = 180\text{ k}\Omega,\quad R_2 = \frac{V_B}{I_2} = 39\text{ k}\Omega$$ 7. **Bypass and coupling capacitors:** $$C_{in} = C_{out} = 10\text{ }\mu\text{F},\quad C_E = 100\text{ }\mu\text{F}$$ **(b) Frequency response and GBP comment (7 marks)** 1. **Open-loop gain** (with $C_E$ in place, $R_E$ fully bypassed): $$A_v = -\frac{\beta_{ac} R_{ac}}{h_{ie}} = -\frac{220 \times 2.64}{2.0} = -290.4\text{ V/V} \approx 49.3\text{ dB}$$ 2. **Feedback factor:** $$\beta = \frac{R_E'}{R_{ac}} = \frac{0.27}{2.64} = 0.1023$$ 3. **Desensitivity:** $$D = 1 + A\beta = 1 + 290.4 \times 0.1023 = 30.7$$ 4. **Predicted cutoffs:** $$f_{L,f} = \frac{100}{30.7} \approx 3.3\text{ Hz},\quad f_{H,f} = 500\text{ kHz} \times 30.7 \approx 15.4\text{ MHz}$$ 5. **GBP invariance:** $$A \cdot BW \approx 290.4 \times 500\text{ kHz} = 145.2\text{ MHz} \approx A_f \cdot BW_f = 9.46 \times 15.4\text{ MHz}$$ 6. **Frequency-response plot:** Two semi-log curves — cream curve (no feedback) with mid-band +49 dB, $f_L = 100\text{ Hz}$, $f_H = 500\text{ kHz}$; blue curve (with feedback) with mid-band +20 dB, $f_L = 3.3\text{ Hz}$, $f_H = 15.4\text{ MHz}$. The two curves intersect at the GBP point. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Using the DC β (hFE = 200) instead of the small-signal β (hfe = 220)** when computing $R_E'$ — the *small-signal* value is required for *a.c. gain* calculations. > 2. **Forgetting to use the a.c. load $R_{ac} = R_C \,\vert\vert\, R_L$**, not just $R_C$, in the gain formula. > 3. **Assuming $A_{vf} = -R_C / R_E'$ exactly** without checking the condition $(1+\beta_{ac})R_E' \gg h_{ie}$ — for $R_E' = 270\text{ }\Omega$, the product is $\approx 60\text{ k}\Omega$, only 30× of $h_{ie}$, so the *exact* formula must be used. > 4. **Mis-identifying $f_L$ as the cutoff where the *gain* falls by 3 dB** — at $f_L$, the gain is $A/\sqrt{2} = 0.707\,A$, *not* $A/2$. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, S8 ECE]` **Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a two-stage voltage-series feedback amplifier designed for a closed-loop gain of 10. Derive expressions for (i) closed-loop gain $A_{vf}$, (ii) input resistance $R_{inf}$, and (iii) bandwidth $BW_f$ in terms of the open-loop parameters.** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block diagram and qualitative operation (7 marks)** 1. **Block diagram:** (Refer to §4.2 above.) A two-stage CE cascade with forward gain $A = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$, a voltage-sampling network at the output ($R_F$ in series with $R_{E2}'$), and the feedback voltage $V_f = \beta_f V_{out}$ injected in *series* with the emitter of Stage 2. 2. **Operation:** A fraction $\beta_f = R_{E2}'/(R_F + R_{E2}')$ of the output voltage is returned to the input side. Because it appears as a *voltage drop* across $R_{E2}'$ in *series* with the base–emitter loop, the net input drive to the second stage is reduced by $V_f$, so the overall gain drops from $A$ to $A_f$. In exchange, the input resistance is *boosted* by $(1+A\beta_f)$, the output resistance is *reduced* by $(1+A\beta_f)$, and the bandwidth is *widened* by the same factor. 3. **Numerical design for $A_{vf} = 10$:** $$A = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = 290.4^2 = 84{,}330\text{ V/V}$$ $$\beta_f = \frac{1}{A/A_{vf} - 1} = \frac{1}{8433 - 1} = 1.186 \times 10^{-4}$$ Choosing $R_{E2}' = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega$ and solving for $R_F$: $$R_F = R_{E2}'\left(\frac{1}{\beta_f} - 1\right) = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega \times 8426 = 8.43\text{ M}\Omega$$ > *(This is impractically large! In a real lab we relax to a lower open-loop A by using lower $R_C$ or a single-stage design. The numerical point: $\beta_f$ must be very small when $A$ is very large.)* **(b) Derivation of the three closed-loop parameters (7 marks)** **Standard negative-feedback model** (refer to §4.3 block diagram): The input signal at the summing node is the *error* signal: $$\varepsilon = V_{in} - V_f = V_{in} - \beta_f V_{out}$$ But $V_{out} = A\varepsilon$, so: $$\varepsilon = V_{in} - \beta_f A \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon(1 + A\beta_f) = V_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + A\beta_f}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;A_{vf} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta_f}\;}$$ **Input resistance:** The input current is $I_{in} = \varepsilon/R_{in}$ (current into the amplifier proper). The voltage across the input terminals is: $$V_{in} = I_{in} R_{in} + V_f = I_{in} R_{in} + \beta_f V_{out} = I_{in} R_{in} (1 + A\beta_f)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;R_{inf} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = R_{in}\,(1 + A\beta_f)\;}$$ **Bandwidth:** A single-pole open-loop response $A(f) = A_0/(1+jf/f_H)$ becomes, with feedback: $$A_f(f) = \frac{A_f}{1 + jf/[f_H(1 + A\beta_f)]}$$ Hence the new upper cutoff is $f_{H,f} = f_H(1+A\beta_f)$, and similarly the lower cutoff scales as $f_{L,f} = f_L/(1+A\beta_f)$. The net bandwidth: $$\boxed{\;BW_f = f_{H,f} - f_{L,f} \approx f_{H,f} = BW\,(1 + A\beta_f)\;}$$ where $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for $f_H \gg f_L$. **Final numerical answer:** - $A_{vf} = 84{,}330 / 8433 = \mathbf{10\text{ V/V}}$ ✓ - $R_{inf} = R_{in} \times 8433 = 2\text{ k}\Omega \times 8433 \approx \mathbf{16.9\text{ M}\Omega}$ - $BW_f = 500\text{ kHz} \times 8433 \approx \mathbf{4.22\text{ GHz}}$ (theoretical; *practical* limit ~ 5–10 MHz) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Voltage-Series Trap:** > 1. **Confusing series and shunt sampling** — at the *output*, voltage-sampling means a node parallel to $R_L$, *not* a loop in series. Always draw the sampling element *across* the output. > 2. **Forgetting that the feedback is to the *emitter*, not the *base*** — mixing at the base would be shunt-mixing, giving voltage-shunt (different topology, different formulas). > 3. **Using a single-stage amplifier** — VSFA *requires* $A\beta_f \gg 1$ to be effective; with a single stage and a passive divider $\beta_f < 1$, the desensitivity is too low. Use *at least* two stages. > 4. **Forgetting the loading effect** of $R_F$ on the second stage — $R_{in2}$ of the second stage appears in parallel with $R_{E2}'$ and must be accounted for in the exact calculation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pin these on your lab-record last page — they are the five most-asked KTU points on this experiment.** * ✔ **CSFA** is realised by a *single unbypassed emitter resistor* $R_E'$ in a CE stage. **VSFA** is realised by *feeding back* a fraction of the output through a *resistive divider* to the *emitter* of the second stage of a cascade. * ✔ The **closed-loop gain** of CSFA: $A_{vf} \approx -R_C/R_E'$ (approx.) or $-\beta_{ac} R_{ac}/[h_{ie} + (1+\beta_{ac})R_E']$ (exact). * ✔ The **closed-loop gain** of VSFA: $A_{vf} = A/(1+A\beta_f)$, where $A = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ and $\beta_f$ is the divider ratio. * ✔ **Four effects of negative feedback:** gain ↓, $R_{in}$ ↑ (series-mixing) or $R_{in}$ ↓ (shunt-mixing), $R_{out}$ ↓ (voltage-sampling) or $R_{out}$ ↑ (current-sampling), bandwidth ↑, distortion ↓ — *all by the same factor* $(1+A\beta_f)$. * ✔ **Gain–Bandwidth Product is conserved:** $A \cdot BW \approx A_f \cdot BW_f$. Use this as a *sanity check* after every design. * ✔ **Frequency-response plot** must be drawn on **semi-log** paper; mark $f_L$, $f_H$, the mid-band plateau (in dB), and the −20 dB/decade asymptotes. * ✔ Always use **measured** $h_{ie}$ and $h_{fe}$ in the design report; the *catalog* values are only a starting point. * ✔ **Practical pitfall:** in the VSFA design, the *required* $\beta_f$ is very small (≈ 1/8000) when $A$ is very large — this means the feedback resistors can be impractically large. In a lab, use a *moderate* $A$ (e.g., 1000) and a *moderate* $\beta_f$ (e.g., 0.1) to keep $R_F$ in the kΩ range. * ✔ **KTU lab viva favourites:** (1) Why does feedback *reduce* distortion? (2) What is the *desensitivity factor*? (3) Why does the *input resistance* increase in series-mixing? (4) Prove GBP invariance.

topics.title: RC Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - ** Topic:** RC Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an RC Oscillator?

An **RC Oscillator** is a sinusoidal oscillator circuit that uses a combination of **Resistors (R)** and **Capacitors (C)** as the frequency-selective feedback network to generate sustained periodic waveforms in the **audio frequency range (typically 20 Hz to 200 kHz)**. Unlike LC oscillators which use inductors, RC oscillators are compact, inexpensive, and ideal for low-to-medium frequency signal generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An RC oscillator is a feedback amplifier that satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** — namely, the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$ with a total phase shift around the loop equal to $0°$ (or an integer multiple of $360°$) — where the frequency-determining element is a pure RC network rather than an LC tank circuit.

### 1.2 Two Principal RC Oscillator Topologies

In Module Experiment 2 of PCECL307, students typically study and implement **one** of the following:

| # | Topology | Frequency Range | Distinctive Feature |
|---|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | Uses 3 cascaded RC sections producing 180° phase shift |
| 2 | **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | 1 Hz – 1 MHz | Uses a lead-lag RC network producing 0° phase shift at $f_0$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Push-and-Release Pendulum"

Imagine pushing a child on a swing. To keep the swing going indefinitely, you must:
1. **Push at exactly the right moment** (correct **phase**).
2. **Push hard enough** to compensate for air drag and friction (correct **gain**).

An RC oscillator works identically: the amplifier provides the "push" (gain), while the RC network ensures the feedback signal arrives back at the input at the **correct moment** (correct phase) and at the **right strength**. If the push arrives too early or too late, or if it is too weak, the oscillation dies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (MUST remember for KTU exams):**
> 1. $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (Magnitude condition)
> 2. $\angle A + \angle \beta = 0°$ or $360°$ (Phase condition)
> Violation of either condition results in **no oscillation** or **distorted (clipped) oscillation**.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in RC Oscillator Design

- **π** ≈ **3.14159** (geometric constant for frequency calculations)
- **Unity gain bandwidth** of the op-amp must be ≥ **3 × oscillation frequency** (operational rule of thumb, **bolded for KTU emphasis**)
- **Standard op-amp supplies**: ±**15 V DC** (typical for μA741, TL081, LM358-based lab rigs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operational Logic

**Architecture:** Common-Emitter (BJT) or Inverting (Op-Amp) amplifier cascaded with a **three-stage RC ladder network** in the feedback path.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-step):**
- The single inverting amplifier (BJT CE stage or op-amp inverting configuration) inherently introduces a phase shift of **180°** between input and output.
- To achieve the remaining **180°** required for positive feedback (total 360°), the RC ladder network must produce exactly **180°** of phase shift.
- Since a single RC section can provide a maximum of 90° phase shift (asymptotically), we cascade **three identical RC sections** to collectively produce 180° at the desired frequency.
- The amplifier must provide a minimum voltage gain of **29** (BJT version) or **29** (op-amp non-inverting) to compensate for the attenuation introduced by the RC network.

### 2.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Operational Logic

**Architecture:** Non-inverting op-amp amplifier with a **Wien Bridge network** (a series RC arm in parallel with a parallel RC arm) in the positive feedback path, and a **resistive voltage divider** in the negative feedback path.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-step):**
- The Wien Bridge network is a **frequency-selective lead-lag circuit**.
- At the **resonant frequency $f_0$**, the network introduces **zero net phase shift** and exhibits a **maximum transmission ratio of 1/3**.
- A non-inverting op-amp provides **0° phase shift** with a closed-loop gain $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.
- The Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ demands $A = 3$, hence $R_f = 2R_1$.
- Frequency of oscillation is governed purely by the RC time constants: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION RULE:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` (not the raw pipe character `|`) to preserve the integrity of markdown table rendering.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | Hz (when R in Ω, C in F) | Identical R and C in all 3 sections |
| 2 | **Minimum Gain (BJT CE version)** | $A_{min} = 29$ | Dimensionless | To overcome network attenuation |
| 3 | **Minimum Gain (Op-Amp version)** | $A_{min} = 29$ | Dimensionless | Same requirement for inverting amp |
| 4 | **RC Network Phase Shift** | $\phi = 180°$ | Each section ≈ 60° | At $f = f_0$ |
| 5 | **Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Series and parallel arms matched |
| 6 | **Feedback Fraction at $f_0$** | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Dimensionless | Maximum transmission |
| 7 | **Required Closed-Loop Gain** | $A = 3$ | Dimensionless | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| 8 | **Gain Resistor Relation** | $R_f = 2R_1$ | Ohms | $R_1$ is grounded resistor |
| 9 | **Wien Network Phase at $f_0$** | $\phi = 0°$ | — | Lead = Lag |
| 10 | **Design Rule of Thumb** | $f_{unity} \geq 3 f_0$ | Hz | Op-amp must support 3× oscillating freq |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Function Generators:** Wien bridge topology is the classic heart of bench-top audio sine wave generators.
- **Musical Instrument Synthesizers:** RC phase shift circuits create variable pitch tone generators.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** Low-frequency oscillators (sub-audio, 0.1–20 Hz) for nerve stimulation and EEG calibration use RC designs.
- **Communication Test Equipment:** Carrier generation in radio transmitter local oscillators (though LC dominates at RF).
- **Industrial Control Systems:** Variable-frequency signal sources for sensor calibration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency

**Given:** A three-stage RC ladder network where each section has identical components $R$ and $C$. The network connects from the output of an inverting amplifier back to its input.

**Step 1: Write the transfer function of a single RC high-pass section.**

A single RC high-pass section has the transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 2: Cascade three such sections.**

Assuming ideal isolation (no loading), the overall transfer function is:

$$
\beta(s) = \left(\frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}\right)^3
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $s = j\omega$ to evaluate at sinusoidal steady-state.**

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \left(\frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}\right)^3
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Barkhausen phase condition.** The total phase of the loop must be $360°$ (or $0°$ modulo $360°$). The amplifier contributes $180°$ (inverting), so the RC network must contribute an additional $180°$, meaning each section contributes $60°$.

**Step 5: Express the phase of a single section.**

For one section: $\phi_1 = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$.

Setting $\phi_1 = 60°$ gives:

$$
90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = 60°
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the angle.**

$$
\tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = 30°
$$

$$
\omega_0 RC = \tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 7: Solve for the angular frequency $\omega_0$.**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 8: Solve for the cyclic frequency $f_0$ by dividing by $2\pi$.**

$$
f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{3}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction step — the actual canonical formula is $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$** because in a **loaded** RC ladder (where each section loads the previous one), the effective analysis yields the factor $\sqrt{6}$ in the denominator instead of $\sqrt{3}$. The full loaded analysis includes the loading effect of the next stage.

**Step 9 (Corrected, loaded analysis):**

Considering loading, the standard textbook result is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

**Step 10: Compute the minimum amplifier gain.**

The magnitude of $\beta$ at $f_0$ evaluates to $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ for the loaded network. Thus, by the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$
A_{min} = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency

**Given:** A Wien Bridge network consisting of a series arm $(R_1, C_1)$ and a parallel arm $(R_2, C_2)$.

**Step 1: Write the impedance of the series arm.**

$$
Z_s = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}
$$

**Step 2: Write the impedance of the parallel arm.**

$$
Z_p = \frac{R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}} = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}
$$

**Step 3: Form the voltage divider to obtain $\beta(j\omega)$.**

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute (assume $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ for the matched case).**

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega RC} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}
$$

**Step 5: Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega RC)$.**

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{R}{R(1 + j\omega RC) + \dfrac{(1 + j\omega RC)}{j\omega C} + R}
$$

**Step 6: Simplify the denominator.**

After algebraic simplification, the canonical form becomes:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}
$$

**Step 7: Apply the Barkhausen phase condition.** The imaginary part of the denominator must vanish (for $\angle\beta = 0°$):

$$
\omega_0 RC - \frac{1}{\omega_0 RC} = 0
$$

**Step 8: Solve for $\omega_0$.**

$$
\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

**Step 9: Solve for $f_0$.**

$$
f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Step 10: Compute the feedback fraction at $f_0$.**

Substituting $\omega = \omega_0$ into the expression from Step 6:

$$
\beta(\omega_0) = \frac{1}{3 + j(0)} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 11: Determine the required amplifier gain.**

From the Barkhausen magnitude condition $A \cdot \vert \beta \vert = 1$:

$$
A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 3
$$

**Step 12: For a non-inverting op-amp:** $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 3$, hence:

$$
\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Frequency Formula | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Phase shift of network | $180°$ | $0°$ |
| Required amplifier type | Inverting (BJT CE / Op-Amp inv.) | Non-inverting (Op-Amp) |
| Required gain magnitude | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | $A = 3$ |
| Output purity (THD) | Moderate (≈ 1–2%) | Excellent (≈ 0.01% with AGC) |
| Amplitude stability | Poor (no AGC in basic design) | Excellent (with lamp / diode AGC) |
| Ease of frequency tuning | Difficult (must vary 3 sections) | Easy (ganged R or C) |
| Common application | Fixed-frequency tone source | Lab audio sine wave generator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC Supply plus or minus 15V] --> OP[Op-Amp TL081]
    OP --> AMP_OUT[Amplifier Output Sine Wave Vout]
    OP -- Negative Feedback Path --> DIV[Resistive Divider R1 and Rf]
    DIV -- Returns to Inverting Input --> OP
    OP -- Positive Feedback Path --> WIEN[Wien Bridge Network R and C]
    WIEN -- Returns to Non-Inverting Input --> OP
    OSC[Frequency Set by RC] -.controls.-> WIEN
    AMP_OUT --> LOAD[Load Speaker or CRO]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC Supply VCC and VEE] --> AMP[Inverting Amplifier BJT CE or Op-Amp]
    AMP --> VOUT[Oscillator Output Vout]
    AMP -- Output fed to RC Ladder --> RC1[RC Section 1 - 60 deg shift]
    RC1 --> RC2[RC Section 2 - 60 deg shift]
    RC2 --> RC3[RC Section 3 - 60 deg shift]
    RC3 -- Total 180 deg shift --> FEED[Feedback to Amplifier Input]
    FEED --> AMP
    VOUT --> CRO[Display on CRO]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Oscillation Build-Up and Steady State

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power ON Transient Noise at Amplifier Input] --> B[Amplifier Amplifies Noise A times]
    B --> C[RC Network Filters and Phase Shifts]
    C --> D{Feedback Phase equals 0 or 360 deg?}
    D -- NO --> E[Signal Decays Oscillation Stops]
    D -- YES --> F{Magnitude of A beta equals or greater than 1?}
    F -- NO --> G[Signal Decays Slowly]
    F -- YES --> H[Signal Grows Exponentially]
    H --> I[Amplifier Enters Saturation Region]
    I --> J[Nonlinearity in AGC Limits Amplitude]
    J --> K[Steady State Sinusoidal Oscillation at f0]
    K --> L[Output Available at Vout Terminal]
```

### 4.4 Component Interconnection Schematic — Wien Bridge Oscillator (Lab Implementation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1A[Resistor R - Series Arm]:::series
    C1A[Capacitor C - Series Arm]:::series
    R1B[Resistor R - Parallel Arm]:::parallel
    C1B[Capacitor C - Parallel Arm]:::parallel
    R1C[Resistor R1 - Non-inverting input]:::fb
    R1D[Resistor Rf - Feedback resistor]:::fb
    OP1[Op-Amp uA741 Pin 6 Output]:::amp
    OP2[Op-Amp Pin 3 Non-inverting Input]:::amp
    OP3[Op-Amp Pin 2 Inverting Input]:::amp
    R1A --- C1A
    C1A --- OP2
    OP2 --- R1B
    R1B --- C1B
    C1B --- GNDA[Ground Reference]
    OP1 --- R1D
    R1D --- OP3
    OP3 --- R1C
    R1C --- GNDB[Ground Reference]
    OP1 --- VOUT[Output Sine Wave Terminal]

    classDef series fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef parallel fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** When drawing the schematic in your record, label the op-amp pins clearly (Pin 2 — Inverting, Pin 3 — Non-inverting, Pin 6 — Output, Pin 7 — +V, Pin 4 — -V). Examiners reward pin-level clarity.
