<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.*

**Model Answer:**
The Barkhausen criterion states two necessary conditions for a feedback oscillator to produce sustained sinusoidal oscillations:
1. The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (or zero): $\angle A + \angle \beta = n \times 360°$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, the oscillation decays; if $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, the oscillation grows until limited by amplifier non-linearity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the frequency of oscillation of a Wien bridge oscillator if $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$?*

**Model Answer:**

Given $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^4 \text{ }\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

Using the Wien bridge frequency formula:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}}
$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Wien Bridge Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wien bridge oscillator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Wien bridge oscillator uses an op-amp in a non-inverting configuration with a Wien bridge network in the positive feedback loop and a resistive divider in the negative feedback loop.

**Working:** The op-amp provides a closed-loop gain of $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$. The Wien network acts as a frequency-selective feedback element that returns a fraction $\beta(j\omega)$ of the output to the non-inverting input.

At the resonant frequency $f_0$, the Wien network provides zero phase shift and a feedback fraction of $\beta = 1/3$. To satisfy the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \times \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1
$$

**Frequency Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.2 of these notes, the frequency of oscillation is derived by setting the imaginary part of the Wien network's transfer function denominator to zero:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

where $R$ and $C$ are the matched component values in the series and parallel arms.

The oscillator produces a clean, low-distortion sine wave at $f_0$ because the Wien network's sharp bandpass characteristic combined with the op-amp's high gain rejects all harmonics.

**(b)** Design a Wien bridge oscillator to generate a **2 kHz** sine wave using a 741 op-amp. Choose $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$ and determine all resistor values. Calculate the percentage change in frequency if one of the resistors drifts by **+5%**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose C and calculate R.**

Given $f_0 = 2000 \text{ Hz}$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{4\pi} \approx 7957.75 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Choose standard value: $R \approx 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 2: Determine feedback resistors.**

For sustained oscillation: $R_f = 2R_1$. Choosing $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, we get $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 3: Effect of +5% drift in R.**

If R increases by 5% (i.e., $R' = 1.05 R$):

$$
f_0' = \frac{1}{2\pi (1.05R) C} = \frac{f_0}{1.05}
$$

$$
f_0' = \frac{2000}{1.05} \approx 1904.76 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate percentage change in frequency.**

$$
\Delta f \% = \frac{f_0 - f_0'}{f_0} \times 100 = \frac{2000 - 1904.76}{2000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta f \% \approx 4.76\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A 5% drift in R produces approximately a 4.76% drift in frequency. This inverse-proportional relationship is the reason Wien bridge oscillators require **precision components** for accurate frequency generation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — RC Phase Shift Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT in common-emitter configuration. Explain its working and derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A BJT-based RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A common-emitter (CE) amplifier using a BJT (e.g., BC107, 2N2222) with a collector resistor $R_C$ and emitter bias network.
- A three-stage RC ladder network connecting the collector (output) back to the base (input).
- Each RC section has identical components $R$ and $C$.

**Working:** The CE amplifier provides 180° phase inversion. Each RC high-pass section provides approximately 60° of phase shift at $f_0$. The three sections together contribute 180°, completing the 360° loop required by the Barkhausen phase condition.

**Frequency Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.1 of these notes, the frequency of oscillation is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

**Minimum Gain Derivation:** The attenuation of the loaded RC ladder at $f_0$ is $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$. By the Barkhausen criterion $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
A_{min} = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29
$$

Therefore, the CE amplifier must be designed (by choosing appropriate $R_C$ and the bias network) to provide a voltage gain of at least **29** to sustain oscillations.

**(b)** For an RC phase shift oscillator using $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$, calculate (i) the frequency of oscillation, and (ii) the minimum required gain of the inverting amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^4 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$.

**(i) Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{0.01539}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 64.97 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Required Gain:**

The feedback factor magnitude of the loaded RC ladder network at $f_0$ is:

$$
\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}
$$

By the Barkhausen magnitude condition:

$$
A_{min} = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{min} = 29}
$$

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in RC Oscillator Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor** in the RC phase shift frequency formula. Students often write $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, which is the **Wien bridge** formula — using it in an RC phase shift problem will cost you **3 marks**.
> 2. **Omitting the loading effect** in RC phase shift analysis. The unloaded formula gives $\sqrt{3}$ in the denominator, but the practical formula (used in lab and exams) has $\sqrt{6}$. Always state explicitly whether your analysis is loaded or unloaded.
> 3. **Confusing the gain requirements:** RC phase shift needs $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ (inverting), while Wien bridge needs $A = 3$ (non-inverting). Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the phase condition** in addition to the magnitude condition when asked about the Barkhausen criterion. Both are required.
> 5. **In schematic diagrams, failing to label the op-amp power supply pins** (Pin 7 = +V, Pin 4 = -V). Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for missing supply connections.
> 6. **Writing units in formulas:** Always include Hz for frequency, Ω for resistance, and F for capacitance in your final answer boxes.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Oscillators**

- [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion** = Loop gain magnitude **= 1** + Loop phase shift **= $n \times 360°$**
- [ ] **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ (with $\sqrt{6}$ due to loading)
- [ ] **Wien Bridge Oscillator** frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (no loading factor)
- [ ] **RC Phase Shift** needs inverting amplifier with gain $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ and 3 RC sections each giving 60° shift
- [ ] **Wien Bridge** needs non-inverting op-amp with gain $A = 3$, hence $R_f = 2R_1$
- [ ] **Op-amp unity gain bandwidth** must be at least $3 \times f_0$ to avoid slew-rate distortion
- [ ] **Amplitude stabilization** in Wien bridge: use a **thermistor** or **diode pair** in feedback for automatic gain control (AGC)
- [ ] **Wien bridge is preferred** for low-distortion audio applications; **RC phase shift is preferred** for fixed, simple, low-cost tone generation
- [ ] **Pin diagram of μA741** must be memorized: Pin 2 (Inv), Pin 3 (Non-Inv), Pin 6 (Out), Pin 7 (+V), Pin 4 (-V), Pins 1, 5, 8 (Offset null)
- [ ] **Lab instruments expected**: Function generator (for comparison), CRO (to view waveform), DC power supply, multimeter, breadboard, and the op-amp IC
- [ ] **Expected CRO observation**: A clean sine wave of amplitude ≈ **±13 V** (slightly less than supply rail) at the calculated $f_0$
- [ ] **Practical check**: Vary R or C slightly and observe frequency change on CRO — it should track the formula inversely
- [ ] **Safety note**: Always switch off the power supply before changing any component on the breadboard
- [ ] **KTU viva question**: "Why can't we use only TWO RC sections instead of three?" → Answer: Two sections can provide at most ~120° of phase shift, which is insufficient to compensate for the 180° inversion of the amplifier
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers (Transformerless) - Class B and Class AB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers (Transformerless) - Class B and Class AB # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier designed to deliver appreciable power to a low-impedance load (typically a loudspeaker, 4Ω–8Ω) with high **power conversion efficiency (η)**. Unlike small-signal voltage amplifiers, the transistor in a power amplifier operates over the full extent of its characteristic curves, swinging between cutoff and saturation. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of Analog Circuits Lab, the focus is on **Transformerless Power Amplifiers**, which eliminate the bulky, costly, and frequency-limiting output transformer by directly coupling the amplifier output to the load through complementary transistor pairs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Class B Power Amplifier**: A push-pull amplifier in which each transistor conducts for exactly **half (180°)** of the input cycle (conduction angle θ = 180°). The quiescent (DC) collector current $I_{CQ} = 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Class AB Power Amplifier**: A modified push-pull amplifier in which each transistor is biased with a small forward bias such that it conducts for slightly more than half a cycle (**θ lies between 180° and 360°**). This bias eliminates the dead zone responsible for crossover distortion. > [!NOTE] > **Transformerless Operation** is achieved via the **Complementary Symmetry** configuration, which uses a matched pair of NPN and PNP transistors (or N-channel and P-channel MOSFETs) to deliver current to the load during both halves of the signal without requiring a center-tapped transformer. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **two-person rowing boat** crossing a river: - **Class A** = One person rows the entire time, even when the boat is already moving in the right direction. Lots of wasted effort, but very smooth motion. - **Class B** = Two people take turns — Person A rows only on the forward stroke, Person B only on the return. The boat moves perfectly, but there is a tiny moment of "hesitation" when the oars switch hands, causing a small jerk. - **Class AB** = Two people row, but each starts pulling **slightly before** the other stops, eliminating the hesitation. The boat glides smoothly with much better energy economy. > [!TIP] > Think of the **load (loudspeaker)** as the boat. The two **transistors** are the rowers. In Class B, the "switch-over jerk" is the famous **Crossover Distortion** that Class AB is specifically designed to eliminate. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | ±12 to ±24 | V | | Peak Output Voltage | $V_m$ | $(V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)})$ | V | | Load Resistance | $R_L$ | 4, 8, 16 | Ω | | Maximum Theoretical Efficiency (Class B) | $\eta_{max}$ | **78.5 %** (π/4) | — | | Maximum Theoretical Efficiency (Class AB) | $\eta_{max}$ | ≈ 78.5 % (approaches Class B) | — | | Power Transistor Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{JA}$ | 50–200 | °C/W | > [!NOTE] > The constant **π/4 ≈ 0.785 = 78.5 %** is the absolute maximum conversion efficiency of any Class B push-pull amplifier — this is one of the most frequently tested values in KTU board examinations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier The Class B complementary symmetry push-pull amplifier uses **two transistors**: - **Q1 (NPN)** handles the **positive half-cycle** of the input signal. - **Q2 (PNP)** handles the **negative half-cycle** of the input signal. ### Working Steps: 1. **Zero Input Condition ($v_i = 0$)**: Both transistors are at cutoff ($V_{BE} = 0 < V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$). The quiescent output voltage is $V_o = 0\text{ V}$, and the quiescent current drawn from $V_{CC}$ is **zero**, making the no-signal power dissipation $P_{DQ} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 0$. 2. **Positive Half-Cycle ($v_i > 0$)**: Q1 turns ON, Q2 remains OFF. Current flows from $V_{CC}$ through Q1 into the load $R_L$ toward ground, producing the positive half of the output. 3. **Negative Half-Cycle ($v_i < 0$)**: Q2 turns ON, Q1 remains OFF. Current flows from ground through the load $R_L$ into Q2 toward $-V_{EE}$, producing the negative half of the output. 4. **Full Cycle**: The two halves combine at the load to reproduce the complete waveform. > [!IMPORTANT] > The 'Why' behind using complementary pairs: In a **transformer-coupled** push-pull, an input transformer with a center tap was needed to produce two antiphase signals. In **complementary symmetry**, the inherent polarity difference between NPN and PNP transistors means a **single-ended input** naturally produces the antiphase drive — eliminating the input transformer entirely. --- ## 2.2 The Crossover Distortion Problem In a pure Class B amplifier, both transistors are OFF when $\vert v_i \vert < V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$. This creates a **dead zone** of width $2V_{BE} \approx 1.4\text{ V}$ during which neither transistor conducts. - During this dead zone, the output stays at **0 V** while the input is varying. - When plotted, the output waveform shows a **"kink" or "flattening"** at the zero-crossing points. - This non-linearity is called **Crossover Distortion** and is highly objectionable in audio applications (produces harsh harmonics). > [!CAUTION] > **Crossover Distortion is the #1 reason Class AB exists.** A KTU examiner will almost certainly ask you to "explain crossover distortion and how Class AB eliminates it." Memorize this cold. --- ## 2.3 Class AB: The Crossover-Distortion Killer Class AB solves the dead-zone problem by introducing a small **forward bias** ($V_{BB}$) to the base of each transistor using: - **Diodes** (simple, thermally dependent) - **Diode-connected transistors** (better thermal tracking — most common) - **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier circuit** (adjustable, most precise) - **Resistor divider** (basic, least accurate) This bias sets the **quiescent point (Q-point)** slightly above cutoff ($I_{CQ}$ is small, typically 1–5 % of peak signal current). Now: - When the input swings through zero, **at least one transistor is already conducting** — no dead zone. - The crossover region becomes smooth and linear. - A small **quiescent current** $I_{CQ}$ flows even with no signal, hence the name "Class AB" (between A and B). > [!TIP] > The trade-off: Class AB sacrifices a tiny amount of efficiency (since $I_{CQ} > 0$ wastes a small amount of DC power as heat) in exchange for vastly superior linearity. This is the universally accepted compromise in real-world audio amplifiers. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are KTU board-exam verified.** Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or `\vert`) in written answers instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid markdown breakage. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Peak Output Voltage | $V_m = V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$ | V | Ideal: $V_m = V_{CC}$ | | 2 | RMS Output Voltage | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \, V_m$ | V | For sinusoidal signal | | 3 | DC Input Current (Avg) | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_m}{\pi} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi \, R_L}$ | A | Half-wave avg over full cycle | | 4 | DC Power Supplied | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 \, V_{CC} \, I_m}{\pi}$ | W | Total from both rails | | 5 | AC Output Power (Load) | $P_{ac} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 \, R_L} = \dfrac{I_m^2 \, R_L}{2}$ | W | Useful power delivered | | 6 | Max AC Output Power | $P_{ac(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 \, R_L}$ | W | At full swing ($V_m = V_{CC}$) | | 7 | Power Dissipated by Both Transistors | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac}$ | W | Heat to be removed by heat sink | | 8 | Power Dissipated per Transistor | $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = \dfrac{P_D}{2}$ | W | Equal in symmetric push-pull | | 9 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$ | — (×100 for %) | Increases with $V_m$ | | 10 | Maximum Efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\,\%$ | — | At $V_m = V_{CC}$ | | 11 | Power Dissipation at Max Efficiency | $P_{D(max \, \eta)} = \dfrac{2 \, V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 \, R_L}$ | W | $P_{ac} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} P_{DC}$ here | | 12 | Transistor Power at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$ | $P_{D(peak)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 \, R_L}$ | W | **Absolute maximum** dissipation | | 13 | Heat Sink Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ | °C/W | $T_{J(max)} \approx 150\,°C$ for Si | | 14 | Quiescent Power (Class AB) | $P_{DQ} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | W | Wasted when no signal | | 15 | Crossover Distortion Zone | $\Delta V = 2 \, V_{BE(on)} \approx 1.4\text{ V}$ | V | Width of dead zone (Class B only) | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Class B/AB in Audio**: Every home theater, smartphone speaker driver, and car audio amplifier uses Class AB output stages (or its modern descendant, Class D). - **Class B in RF**: High-frequency transmitters use Class B/C for high-efficiency power delivery to antennas. - **Transformerless Design**: Critical for **PCB integration**, **portable devices**, and **wideband applications** (no transformer core saturation or frequency roll-off). - **Heat Sink Design**: Power amplifier labs teach thermal management — a critical engineering skill since **55 % of electronic failures are heat-related** (industry standard metric). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Conversion Efficiency of Class B Amplifier ### Starting Point We have a complementary-symmetry push-pull amplifier with supply rails $\pm V_{CC}$, peak output voltage $V_m$, and load $R_L$. Assume sinusoidal input with $V_m \leq V_{CC}$ (ideal transistor). --- ### Step 1: Express the Output Current For a sinusoidal output, the instantaneous current through the load is: $$i_o(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ where the **peak load current** is: $$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L}$$ > **Logic:** The load is purely resistive (loudspeaker approximated as $R_L$ at audio frequencies), so Ohm's law gives the sinusoidal current directly. --- ### Step 2: Compute the DC Supply Current Each transistor conducts for **only one half-cycle**. The DC current drawn from the supply is the **average value of a half-wave rectified sine wave** over a full cycle: $$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta$$ Evaluating the integral: $$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left( -\cos\pi + \cos 0 \right) = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} (1 + 1)$$ $$\boxed{I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi \, R_L}}$$ > **Logic:** The integral of $\sin\theta$ over $0$ to $\pi$ is 2, divided by $2\pi$ (full period) yields $1/\pi$. This is a standard result for half-wave rectification. --- ### Step 3: Compute the DC Power Supplied by the Source The **total** DC power comes from **both** the $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ supplies (each delivers current for half the cycle): $$P_{DC} = 2 \, V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = 2 \, V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \, V_{CC} \, I_m}{\pi}$$ Substituting $I_m = V_m / R_L$: $$\boxed{P_{DC} = \frac{2 \, V_{CC} \, V_m}{\pi \, R_L}}$$ > **Logic:** The factor of 2 accounts for the symmetric dual supply. This is the **total power drawn from the mains/battery** — most of it ends up as either useful output or heat in the transistors. --- ### Step 4: Compute the AC (Useful) Output Power The RMS value of a sinusoid with peak $V_m$ is $V_m/\sqrt{2}$. Therefore, the power delivered to the load is: $$P_{ac} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R_L} = \frac{(V_m / \sqrt{2})^2}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 \, R_L}$$ $$\boxed{P_{ac} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 \, R_L}}$$ > **Logic:** This is the only "useful" power — what actually drives the loudspeaker cone. Note that $P_{ac}$ depends on $V_m^2$, while $P_{DC}$ depends linearly on $V_m$. --- ### Step 5: Define Conversion Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_m^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)}$$ Simplifying: $$\eta = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC} V_m} = \frac{\pi \, V_m}{4 \, V_{CC}}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}\right)}$$ > **Logic:** The efficiency is **directly proportional to the output swing** $V_m$. The bigger the signal, the higher the efficiency. The maximum possible value occurs when $V_m = V_{CC}$ (full swing). --- ### Step 6: Maximum Efficiency (Maximum Output Swing) Setting $V_m = V_{CC}$: $$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\,\%}$$ > **Logic:** This is the **theoretical upper limit** of any Class B push-pull amplifier. Real amplifiers reach 70–75 % due to $V_{CE(sat)}$ losses, finite transistor resistance, and bias network currents. --- ### Step 7: Power Dissipated in Transistors By conservation of energy: $$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$ $$\boxed{P_D = \frac{1}{R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi} - \frac{V_m^2}{2} \right)}$$ Each transistor dissipates half: $$P_{D1} = P_{D2} = \frac{P_D}{2}$$ > **Logic:** This is the heat that must be removed by the heat sink. A common lab task is to plot $P_D$ vs $V_m$ and verify that the maximum dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, not at maximum output! --- ### Step 8: Find the Peak Power Dissipation Differentiate $P_D$ with respect to $V_m$ and set to zero: $$\frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = \frac{1}{R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - V_m \right) = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$ Substituting back: $$P_{D(peak)} = \frac{1}{R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC} \cdot 2V_{CC}/\pi}{\pi} - \frac{(2V_{CC}/\pi)^2}{2} \right)$$ $$= \frac{1}{R_L} \left( \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2} \right) = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$ $$\boxed{P_{D(peak)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx 0.203 \, \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L}}$$ > **Logic (Exam Gold!)**: The **maximum transistor dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636 \, V_{CC}$**, NOT at maximum output. This is a classic KTU trick question. At this point, efficiency is only $\pi/4 \cdot 2/\pi = 1/2 = 50\%$. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Calculation Style) **Given:** $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$, $V_m = 10\text{ V}$ (sinusoidal). **Find:** $P_{DC}$, $P_{ac}$, $P_D$ (per transistor), and $\eta$. | Step | Calculation | Result | |---|---|---| | Peak Current | $I_m = V_m / R_L = 10 / 8$ | **1.25 A** | | DC Supply Current | $I_{DC} = I_m / \pi = 1.25 / \pi$ | **0.398 A** | | DC Power | $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{DC} = 2 \times 12 \times 0.398$ | **9.55 W** | | AC Output Power | $P_{ac} = V_m^2 / (2 R_L) = 100 / 16$ | **6.25 W** | | Power Dissipated (both) | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac}$ | **3.30 W** | | Per Transistor | $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = P_D / 2$ | **1.65 W** | | Efficiency | $\eta = P_{ac} / P_{DC} = 6.25 / 9.55$ | **65.4 %** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class B Complementary Symmetry Push-Pull Amplifier (Circuit Topology) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INPUT["Input Signal Source"] VS["Vi sine wave"] end subgraph BIAS["Quiescent Bias (Class AB Only)"] D1["Diode D1"] D2["Diode D2"] D3["Bias Resistor R"] end subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE["Complementary Push-Pull Output Stage"] Q1["Q1 NPN Transistor"] Q2["Q2 PNP Transistor"] end subgraph LOAD["Load Network"] RL["Speaker RL 8 ohm"] VCC["VCC Positive Rail"] VEE["VEE Negative Rail"] end VS --> Q1 VS --> Q2 D1 --- Q1 D2 --- Q2 D3 --- D1 D3 --- D2 VCC --> Q1 VEE --> Q2 Q1 --> RL Q2 --> RL RL --> GND1["Ground Node"] classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef supply fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class Q1,Q2 active class VCC,VEE supply class RL,VS load class GND1 ground ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Q1 (NPN)** is the upper transistor (sources current into load from $+V_{CC}$). - **Q2 (PNP)** is the lower transistor (sinks current from load into $-V_{EE}$). - The two **diodes D1, D2** (with series resistor R) provide the small forward bias needed for **Class AB** operation. - In pure **Class B**, omit the diodes → bases connect directly together. --- ## 4.2 Signal Flow & Conduction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input Vi Sine Wave"] --> B{"Comparator
Vi versus 0"} B -- Vi greater than 0 --> C["Q1 NPN Conducts
Positive Half Cycle"] B -- Vi less than 0 --> D["Q2 PNP Conducts
Negative Half Cycle"] C --> E["Current flows from VCC through Q1
through RL to Ground"] D --> F["Current flows from Ground
through RL through Q2 to VEE"] E --> G["Combined Output Vo
across RL"] F --> G G --> H["Speaker produces sound
at audio frequency"] classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A input class B decision class C,D active class E,F,G,H output ``` --- ## 4.3 Crossover Distortion Waveform Visualization (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph IDEAL["Ideal Sinusoidal Input Signal"] I1["Vi smooth sine wave
zero crossing at t equals 0 and t equals T/2"] end subgraph CLASSB["Class B Output at Load"] O1["Vo with crossover notch
dead zone of width 2 VBE"] end subgraph CLASSAB["Class AB Output at Load"] O2["Vo smooth sine wave
no dead zone"] end subgraph CAUSE["Root Cause"] C1["Both transistors OFF when
absolute Vi less than VBE on"] C2["Small forward bias
VBB greater than VBE on"] end subgraph FIX["Class AB Solution"] F1["Bias network using diodes
or VBE multiplier"] end I1 --> C1 C1 --> O1 I1 --> C2 C2 --> F1 F1 --> O2 classDef ideal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef good fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cause fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fix fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class I1 ideal class O1 bad class O2 good class C1,C2 cause class F1 fix ``` **Reading the architecture:** The left branch shows the **Class B problem** — without bias, both transistors are OFF for a small region around zero-crossing, producing a visible "kink" in the output. The right branch shows the **Class AB fix** — a small bias voltage $V_{BB}$ keeps each transistor on the edge of conduction, eliminating the dead zone and producing a smooth output. --- ## 4.4 Power Flow Distribution Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB PWR["P_DC from VCC and VEE supply
2 VCC Im divided by pi"] PWR --> SPLIT{"Power Distribution
at any output level Vm"} SPLIT --> USE["P_AC useful output power
Vm squared divided by 2 RL"] SPLIT --> WASTE["P_D heat in transistors
2 VCC Vm divided by pi RL minus Vm squared divided by 2 RL"] USE --> SPK["Delivered to loudspeaker RL
converted to sound"] WASTE --> HEAT["Removed by heat sink
via conduction and convection"] USE --> EFF["Efficiency eta
pi Vm divided by 4 VCC"] WASTE --> EFF EFF --> NOTE["Maximum eta equals 78.5 percent
at Vm equals VCC"] classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef use fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef waste fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef metric fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class PWR source class SPLIT decision class USE,SPK use class WASTE,HEAT waste class EFF,NOTE metric ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define crossover distortion in a Class B push-pull amplifier. How is it eliminated in Class AB?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Crossover distortion is the non-linear distortion that occurs in a Class B push-pull amplifier near the zero-crossing region of the input signal. It arises because both transistors require a forward base-emitter voltage $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ to conduct. When the instantaneous input $\vert v_i \vert < V_{BE(on)}$, both transistors are OFF, creating a **dead zone of width $\approx 1.4\text{ V}$** in the output waveform. This produces a visible "kink" or flattening at the crossover point, generating unwanted harmonics (especially the 3rd and 5th). **Elimination (1.5 marks):** Class AB eliminates crossover distortion by applying a small **forward bias** $V_{BB}$ (typically $1.2$ to $1.4\text{ V}$) between the bases of the two complementary transistors using a **diode bias network** or a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier**. This pre-bias places the Q-point just above cutoff, so when the input signal swings through zero, at least one transistor is already conducting. The smooth handover between Q1 and Q2 produces a linear output with no dead zone. The trade-off is a small quiescent current $I_{CQ} \neq 0$, which slightly reduces maximum efficiency from 78.5 % to ≈ 70–75 % in practice. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Why are transformerless power amplifiers preferred over transformer-coupled power amplifiers? State any two advantages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **transformerless (complementary symmetry) power amplifier** eliminates the bulky output transformer and uses matched NPN/PNP transistor pairs to drive the load directly. Two key advantages: 1. **No frequency-dependent losses (1.5 marks):** Transformers have a limited frequency response — they roll off at low frequencies (core saturation) and at high frequencies (leakage inductance, winding capacitance). Direct-coupled Class B/AB designs provide flat response from **DC to hundreds of kHz**, which is why they dominate audio and broadband applications. 2. **Smaller, cheaper, and PCB-mountable (1.5 marks):** Transformers are heavy, expensive, and cannot be integrated on a PCB. Transformerless designs use only transistors, resistors, and small capacitors — enabling miniaturization for portable devices, IC integration, and mass production. Additionally, there are no transformer core losses (hysteresis, eddy currents), improving overall efficiency by 5–10 %. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students commonly lose marks by: (1) forgetting to multiply by 2 in the $P_{DC}$ formula for dual supply, (2) using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_m$ in efficiency derivation, (3) writing $V_{rms} = V_m$ instead of $V_m/\sqrt{2}$, and (4) skipping the conversion efficiency step entirely. **Show every formula substitution.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model] **Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **complementary symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its **conversion efficiency** and find the value of **maximum efficiency**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Operation (3 marks):** A complementary symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier uses a matched NPN transistor Q1 and a PNP transistor Q2 connected in series across a split supply $\pm V_{CC}$. The load $R_L$ is connected between the common emitter point (output node) and ground. Both bases are tied together to form the single-ended input $v_i$. - When $v_i > +0.7\text{ V}$: Q1 (NPN) conducts, Q2 (PNP) is OFF. Current flows from $+V_{CC} \rightarrow Q1 \rightarrow R_L \rightarrow$ ground, producing the **positive half-cycle** at the output. - When $v_i < -0.7\text{ V}$: Q2 (PNP) conducts, Q1 is OFF. Current flows from ground $\rightarrow R_L \rightarrow Q2 \rightarrow -V_{EE}$, producing the **negative half-cycle** at the output. - When $\vert v_i \vert < 0.7\text{ V}$: both transistors are OFF → dead zone → **crossover distortion**. The two halves combine across $R_L$ to reconstruct the full sinusoidal output. **Efficiency Derivation (4 marks):** For a sinusoidal output with peak voltage $V_m$ and peak current $I_m = V_m / R_L$: **DC supply current** (average of half-wave rectified sine, from each rail): $$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{\pi}$$ **Total DC power** (from both $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$): $$P_{DC} = 2 \, V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \frac{2 \, V_{CC} \, I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \, V_{CC} \, V_m}{\pi \, R_L}$$ **AC output power** (RMS power in $R_L$): $$P_{ac} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$ **Conversion efficiency**: $$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_m^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{\pi \, V_m}{4 \, V_{CC}}$$ **Maximum efficiency** at $V_m = V_{CC}$: $$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.785 = 78.5\,\%}$$ --- **(b)** For a Class B push-pull amplifier with $V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$, and peak output swing $V_m = 12\text{ V}$, calculate: **(i)** DC input power $P_{DC}$, **(ii)** AC output power $P_{ac}$, **(iii)** Power dissipated per transistor $P_{D1}$, and **(iv)** Conversion efficiency $\eta$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$, $V_m = 12\text{ V}$. **(i) DC input power (2 marks):** $$P_{DC} = \frac{2 \, V_{CC} \, V_m}{\pi \, R_L} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 12}{\pi \times 8} = \frac{360}{25.133}$$ $$\boxed{P_{DC} \approx 14.32\text{ W}}$$ **(ii) AC output power (2 marks):** $$P_{ac} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{144}{16}$$ $$\boxed{P_{ac} = 9.0\text{ W}}$$ **(iii) Power dissipated per transistor (2 marks):** Total transistor dissipation: $$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = 14.32 - 9.0 = 5.32\text{ W}$$ Per transistor (symmetric push-pull): $$P_{D1} = \frac{P_D}{2} = \frac{5.32}{2}$$ $$\boxed{P_{D1} = 2.66\text{ W}}$$ **(iv) Conversion efficiency (1 mark):** $$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{9.0}{14.32} = 0.6283$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 62.83\,\%}$$ **Cross-check using formula:** $\eta = \frac{\pi \, V_m}{4 \, V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi \times 12}{4 \times 15} = \frac{\pi}{5} = 0.6283$ ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] — *Internal Choice Alternative* **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **crossover distortion** in a Class B amplifier with suitable waveforms. Describe how a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier bias network** converts it into a Class AB amplifier, and state **two advantages** of using the $V_{BE}$ multiplier over a simple diode bias. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Crossover Distortion Explanation (3 marks):** In a complementary symmetry Class B amplifier, both Q1 (NPN) and Q2 (PNP) require a forward base-emitter voltage $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ before they can conduct. When the input signal $v_i$ lies in the range $-0.7\text{ V} < v_i < +0.7\text{ V}$, neither transistor conducts, and the output remains at 0 V. As a result, the output waveform exhibits a **flattened region** (dead zone) of width $\approx 1.4\text{ V}$ around each zero crossing, even though the input is a smooth sinusoid. This non-linearity is called **crossover distortion** and is highly objectionable in audio because it introduces odd-order harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th…) that the human ear perceives as harsh and buzzy. **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier Operation (2.5 marks):** A $V_{BE}$ multiplier is a transistor-based bias network that provides a stable, adjustable reference voltage between the bases of Q1 and Q2. It consists of a transistor Q3 with a resistor divider $R_A$ (from collector to base) and $R_B$ (from base to emitter). The output voltage is: $$V_{BB} = V_{BE3} \left( 1 + \frac{R_A}{R_B} \right)$$ By adjusting the ratio $R_A / R_B$, the bias voltage $V_{BB}$ is set to approximately $1.2$ to $1.4\text{ V}$ — just enough to keep both Q1 and Q2 on the verge of conduction. This pre-biases the output stage into the **Class AB** region, eliminating the dead zone and producing a smooth output. **Two Advantages over Diode Bias (1.5 marks):** 1. **Adjustability:** $V_{BB}$ can be precisely tuned via the $R_A/R_B$ ratio to match the specific $V_{BE}$ requirements of the output transistors, whereas a diode chain provides fixed, non-tunable voltage drops. 2. **Thermal Tracking:** The $V_{BE}$ multiplier uses a single transistor Q3 on the same heat sink as the output devices, so its $V_{BE3}$ decreases with temperature at the same rate (≈ -2 mV/°C) as the output transistors — automatically compensating for thermal drift. A diode chain often uses a separate (untracked) diode, leading to thermal runaway risk. --- **(b)** A **Class AB power amplifier** uses a $V_{BE}$ multiplier with $R_A = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{BE3} = 0.65\text{ V}$. Calculate: **(i)** the bias voltage $V_{BB}$, **(ii)** the quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ if the emitter resistor of Q3 is $R_E = 100\text{ }\Omega$, and **(iii)** the percentage increase in quiescent power dissipation when the supply is changed from $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$ to $V_{CC} = 18\text{ V}$ (assuming $I_{CQ}$ scales proportionally with the bias network). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Bias voltage $V_{BB}$ (2 marks):** $$V_{BB} = V_{BE3} \left( 1 + \frac{R_A}{R_B} \right) = 0.65 \times \left( 1 + \frac{1.2}{4.7} \right)$$ $$= 0.65 \times (1 + 0.2553) = 0.65 \times 1.2553$$ $$\boxed{V_{BB} \approx 0.816\text{ V}}$$ **(ii) Quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ (2.5 marks):** The voltage across $R_E$ is $V_{BB} - V_{BE3} = 0.816 - 0.65 = 0.166\text{ V}$. $$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE3}}{R_E} = \frac{0.166}{100}$$ $$\boxed{I_{CQ} = 1.66\text{ mA}}$$ **(iii) Percentage increase in $P_{DQ}$ (2.5 marks):** Quiescent power $P_{DQ} = 2 \, V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ (using both rails): At $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$: $$P_{DQ1} = 2 \times 12 \times 1.66\text{ mA} = 39.84\text{ mW}$$ At $V_{CC} = 18\text{ V}$ (assuming $I_{CQ}$ scales proportionally → $I_{CQ}' = 1.66 \times 18/12 = 2.49\text{ mA}$): $$P_{DQ2} = 2 \times 18 \times 2.49\text{ mA} = 89.64\text{ mW}$$ Percentage increase: $$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{P_{DQ2} - P_{DQ1}}{P_{DQ1}} \times 100 = \frac{89.64 - 39.84}{39.84} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ increase} = 125\,\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall #1:** Many students write $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$ (single supply) and **forget the factor of 2** for the dual supply. The correct formula is $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{DC}$. This mistake costs 2–3 marks per question. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall #2:** When asked for "power dissipated per transistor," students often give the **total** $P_D$. Remember to divide by 2: $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = P_D/2$. Examiners check this carefully. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall #3:** The maximum dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$ — **not at maximum output**. If a question asks "at what output level is transistor dissipation maximum?", do not answer $V_m = V_{CC}$. This is one of the most common board-exam traps. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam!** - **Class B** = each transistor conducts for **180°**; quiescent current $I_{CQ} = 0$. - **Class AB** = each transistor conducts for **180°–360°**; small $I_{CQ}$ to eliminate crossover distortion. - **Crossover distortion** = dead zone of width $2 V_{BE} \approx 1.4\text{ V}$ at zero crossings in Class B. - **Class AB fix** = diode bias / $V_{BE}$ multiplier provides $V_{BB} \approx 1.2$–$1.4\text{ V}$ between bases. - **$V_{BE}$ multiplier formula:** $V_{BB} = V_{BE3} (1 + R_A/R_B)$ — adjustable, thermally stable. - **Max efficiency** of Class B / Class AB = **π/4 = 78.5 %** (theoretical, at $V_m = V_{CC}$). - **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = (\pi/4) \cdot (V_m / V_{CC})$ — proportional to output swing. - **DC input power:** $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$ — note the factor of 2 for dual supply. - **AC output power:** $P_{ac} = V_m^2 / (2 R_L)$. - **Per-transistor dissipation:** $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = (P_{DC} - P_{ac})/2$. - **Peak dissipation point:** $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, giving $P_{D(peak)} = 2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L)$. - **Complementary symmetry** uses NPN + PNP pair — eliminates input/output transformers. - **Transformerless advantages:** wider bandwidth, lighter, cheaper, PCB-mountable, no core losses. - **Class AB trade-off:** tiny $I_{CQ}$ wastes small quiescent power → max efficiency drops to ~70–75 % in practice. - **Heat sink formula:** $\theta_{SA} = (T_{J(max)} - T_A)/P_D - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ — always design for worst-case $P_{D(peak)}$. - **Lab observation tip:** When testing on a breadboard, observe the output on a CRO — the "notch" at zero crossing is your visual confirmation of crossover distortion. Adding bias diodes should make the waveform smooth. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Day Strategy:** For any Class B/AB numerical problem, the four-step process is: (1) identify $V_{CC}$, $V_m$, $R_L$, (2) compute $I_m = V_m/R_L$, (3) use the **cheat-sheet formulas** in Section 2.4, (4) cross-verify $\eta$ using both $P_{ac}/P_{DC}$ and the $\pi V_m/(4 V_{CC})$ formula. If both match, your answer is correct.

topics.title: Transistor Series Voltage Regulator - Design for a Specific Output Voltage with and without Short Circuit Protection (Plot Load and Line Regulation Characteristics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL307 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transistor Series Voltage Regulator - Design for a Specific Output Voltage with and without Short Circuit Protection (Plot Load and Line Regulation Characteristics)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Transistor Series Voltage Regulator** is a linear, closed-loop analog regulator topology in which a power transistor (called the *pass transistor* or *series control element*) is placed in **series** between the unregulated DC input supply and the load, so that the regulator maintains an essentially constant output voltage despite variations in load current (load regulation) and input supply voltage (line regulation).

In the KTU PCECL307 Analog Circuits Lab, this experiment involves the **design, hardware implementation, and characterisation** of a series regulator that delivers a *specific nominal output voltage* (typically $+9\,\text{V}$, $+12\,\text{V}$, or $+15\,\text{V}$), with an **optional short-circuit protection (SCP) network** that prevents catastrophic failure of the pass transistor when the output is accidentally shorted to ground. Students are further required to plot the **Load Regulation** and **Line Regulation** characteristics from measured data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The experiment is assessed on (i) the correct design using the governing equations, (ii) the hardware wiring on a breadboard/PCB, (iii) tabulation of $V_o$ vs $I_L$ and $V_o$ vs $V_{in}$ data, and (iv) interpretation of regulation graphs. Marks are awarded for a working circuit, not just the maths.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap connected to a high-pressure water pipe** that supplies variable pressure (representing the unregulated DC input). The series regulator's pass transistor acts like a **smart, automatic valve** placed *in series* with the pipe. A **pressure gauge** (the Zener reference + error amplifier) constantly measures the output pressure and signals the valve to open or close:

- If the *input pressure rises* (line variation), the valve closes a bit to maintain the same downstream pressure.
- If *someone opens a downstream tap and draws more water* (load increase), the valve opens wider to keep the pressure steady.
- A **safety pressure-relief valve** (the short-circuit protection transistor + sense resistor) slams the main valve shut if the flow suddenly becomes dangerously high (a short circuit).

The output of this "smart valve" is therefore a **rock-steady, regulated water pressure** ($V_o$) — exactly what every electronic load downstream of a power supply needs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities and constants are relevant to this experiment and are highlighted in **bold** because they appear verbatim in KTU valuation keys:

- **Silicon BJT base-emitter ON voltage, $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$** (at $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$, $T = 25^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Zener breakdown voltage, $V_Z$** — selected from the **1N47xx series** (e.g., **1N4732 for $5.1\,\text{V}$**, **1N4735 for $6.2\,\text{V}$**, **1N4742 for $12\,\text{V}$**).
- **Thermal voltage, $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$** at $300\,\text{K}$.
- **Load Regulation** (in % or mV) $= \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{\,V_{in}=\text{const}}$ and is reported as the slope or as a percentage of nominal $V_o$.
- **Line Regulation** (in % or mV) $= \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta V_{in}}\Big\vert_{\,I_L=\text{const}}$ and is reported as a slope or percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Regulation in Brief**
> A *perfect* DC regulator would have a flat horizontal characteristic on both the load and line regulation plots. Any deviation from the horizontal is a *regulation error* and represents the residual output variation that the regulator could not suppress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Functional Decomposition of a Series Regulator

A series-pass regulator is fundamentally a **negative-feedback closed-loop system** with three mandatory functional blocks:

1. **Reference sub-system** — A Zener diode (reverse-biased in breakdown) provides a temperature-dependent but stable reference voltage $V_Z$ that is independent of the unregulated input (provided $V_{in} \ge V_Z + V_{dropout}$ and the Zener current $I_Z$ stays within its minimum and maximum limits).
2. **Error-detection / Sampling sub-system** — A resistive divider ($R_1$, $R_2$) senses the output voltage and produces a scaled feedback voltage $V_{fb} = V_o \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$.
3. **Comparison and Pass sub-system** — A control element (a BJT such as a **2N3055** in the basic version, or an op-amp + power BJT in the improved version) compares $V_{fb}$ with $V_Z$ and modulates the base drive of the pass transistor so that equilibrium is reached when $V_{fb} \approx V_Z$.

### 2.1.1 Why is the Pass Transistor in *Series* and not in *Shunt*?

- In a **shunt regulator**, the control element is placed in *parallel* with the load and *throws away* excess current to ground. This works for low-current, low-power applications.
- In a **series regulator**, the pass transistor *modulates its own collector–emitter voltage* like a variable resistor. The current that flows *into* the load is the *same* current that flows *through* the pass transistor, so virtually all the unregulated input current is delivered to the load. This makes the series topology **inherently efficient** for high-current, medium-voltage applications (e.g., $5\,\text{V}/1\,\text{A}$ logic supplies, op-amp rails, instrumentation).
- The trade-off: the pass transistor must dissipate $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ as heat, so a **heatsink** is mandatory.

### 2.1.2 Why is Feedback Necessary?

Without feedback, the output voltage would track the input minus a fixed $V_{CE(sat)}$ of the pass transistor — a *very poor* regulator. Negative feedback forces the system to obey the relation $V_{fb} = V_Z$ at steady state, making $V_o$ depend only on $V_Z$ and the resistor ratio, *not* on $V_{in}$, $I_L$, temperature, or transistor beta.

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Topologies for This Experiment

### Topology A — Emitter-Follower Series Regulator (Simplest)

This is the **minimum-component** version. The pass transistor (Q1) is wired as an **emitter follower**, and its base is held at a fixed potential $V_Z$ by a Zener diode. The emitter (which is $V_o$) therefore tracks the base minus $V_{BE(\text{on})}$.

- $V_o = V_Z - V_{BE(\text{on})}$ for an NPN emitter follower. *(Note the sign: emitter is $V_B - V_{BE}$.)*
- If you need a *positive* $V_o$ and use an NPN transistor, you actually have $V_o = V_Z - V_{BE}$ — so the Zener must be chosen slightly *above* the desired $V_o$.
- A *practical* NPN version is often drawn with the Zener at the base referenced to ground and the load at the emitter, giving $V_o = V_Z - 0.7$.

### Topology B — Feedback (Op-Amp / Transistor Error Amplifier) Series Regulator

This is the version most often used in KTU experiments because the output voltage is *settable* by a resistor ratio.

- Feedback network: $V_{fb} = V_o \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- At equilibrium: $V_{fb} = V_Z$, hence $V_o = V_Z \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$.
- An op-amp (e.g., **$\mu$A741**) drives the base of the pass transistor with whatever base current is needed to keep the feedback balanced. The op-amp itself can only deliver $\sim 20\,\text{mA}$, so for $I_L > 100\,\text{mA}$ the pass transistor must be a **Darlington pair** (e.g., **TIP122**, a built-in Darlington with $\beta > 1000$).

### Topology C — Series Regulator with Short-Circuit Protection (SCP)

The basic regulator has one fatal flaw: a short at the output forces the pass transistor to dump the *full* unregulated current into ground through itself, destroying it in milliseconds. The SCP network uses:

- A **current-sense resistor, $R_S$**, in series with the load (or with the emitter of the pass transistor).
- A **protection transistor, Q2** (typically a small-signal NPN like **BC547** or **2N2222**), whose base is tied to the *top* of $R_S$ and whose emitter is tied to the *base* of the pass transistor (or to the op-amp output).
- When $I_L \cdot R_S \ge V_{BE(\text{on})}$ of Q2, Q2 turns on and *robs base current* away from the pass transistor, limiting the output current to:

$$I_{L(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} \approx \dfrac{0.7\,\text{V}}{R_S}$$

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this experiment. Memorise every row.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Governing Formula | Typical Numerical Value / Unit | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output voltage (emitter-follower) | $V_o = V_Z - V_{BE(\text{on})}$ | $V_o \approx V_Z - 0.7\,\text{V}$ | Requires $I_Z(\text{min}) < I_Z < I_Z(\text{max})$ |
| 2 | Output voltage (feedback) | $V_o = V_Z\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Volts | Op-amp output must not saturate |
| 3 | Zener current | $I_Z = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_Z}$ | mA | $I_{Z(\text{min})} \le I_Z \le I_{Z(\text{max})}$ |
| 4 | Dropout voltage | $V_{\text{drop}} = V_{in} - V_o$ | $2$ to $3\,\text{V}$ for BJT | Must be $\ge V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ of pass BJT |
| 5 | Load regulation | $\text{LR} = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{V_{in}}$ | mV/A or % | Measured at constant $V_{in}$ |
| 6 | Line regulation | $\text{LR}_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta V_{in}}\Big\vert_{I_L}$ | mV/V or % | Measured at constant $I_L$ |
| 7 | % Load regulation | $\%\text{LR} = \dfrac{V_{o(\text{NL})} - V_{o(\text{FL})}}{V_{o(\text{FL})}} \times 100$ | % | NL = no load, FL = full load |
| 8 | % Line regulation | $\%\text{LR}_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{\Delta V_o / V_o}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100$ | %/V or % | Often expressed per volt |
| 9 | Short-circuit current limit | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}$ | A | Set $R_S \approx 0.7/I_{SC}$ |
| 10 | Pass-transistor power dissipation | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ | W | Heatsink required if $P_D > 1\,\text{W}$ |
| 11 | Feedback fraction | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 12 | Base drive required | $I_B = \dfrac{I_L}{\beta_{\text{pass}}}$ | mA | Op-amp must supply this |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas are "high yield"**: In a typical 14-mark KTU question on this experiment, you will be asked to **design** the circuit (using row 2 or row 1), **size** $R_Z$ (row 3), **size** $R_S$ for SCP (row 9), and **interpret** the regulation characteristics (rows 5–8). Missing any one row costs roughly 3–4 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-board local regulation in PC motherboards, IoT boards, and embedded systems** — almost every microcontroller subsystem has a local series (LDO) regulator right next to the IC.
- **Op-amp and ADC/DAC reference rails** — high-precision data converters demand quiet, low-noise supplies; series regulators with their low output impedance and ripple rejection are the workhorse.
- **Bench laboratory power supplies** — the "raw" front-end of a bench supply is a *series* pass element driven by an error amplifier; even modern switching supplies have a *post-regulator LDO* stage.
- **Automotive and avionics** — the load-dump and cold-crank transients in a $12\,\text{V}$ automotive bus are rejected by series regulators feeding ECUs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Problem Statement (Canonical KTU Question)

> **Design a transistor series voltage regulator to deliver $V_o = +12\,\text{V}$ at $I_L = 100\,\text{mA}$ from an unregulated input of $V_{in} = 18 \pm 2\,\text{V}$. Use an op-amp error amplifier (LM741) with a 2N3055 pass transistor. Design the short-circuit protection such that $I_{SC} = 250\,\text{mA}$. Plot (qualitatively) the load and line regulation curves.**

We will solve this exhaustively, step by step.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Choose the Zener Reference Voltage

A Zener voltage of **$V_Z = 5.1\,\text{V}$ (1N4733)** is selected because:

- It is a stable, low-temperature-coefficient Zener (the **$5.1\,\text{V}$ knee** has near-zero temperature coefficient, since the positive tempco of $V_BE$ cancels the negative tempco of breakdown).
- It is widely available in the KTU lab kit.
- It allows the feedback ratio to be a simple integer.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute the Feedback Resistor Ratio

We want $V_o = +12\,\text{V}$ using the governing relation:

$$V_o = V_Z \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

Rearranging for the ratio:

$$1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_Z} = \dfrac{12}{5.1} = 2.3529$$

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = 2.3529 - 1 = 1.3529$$

Choosing a standard value for $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (1% metal film):

$$R_1 = 1.3529 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 13.529\,\text{k}\Omega$$

The nearest E96 standard value is **$R_1 = 13.3\,\text{k}\Omega$** (or a $13\,\text{k}\Omega$ + $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ trim pot to fine-tune). Substituting back:

$$V_o = 5.1 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{13.3}{10}\right) = 5.1 \times 2.33 = 11.883\,\text{V}$$

which is within **$1\%$** of the design target. *(A trim pot is the standard KTU lab practice.)*

## 3.4 Step 3 — Size the Zener Bias Resistor $R_Z$

The Zener needs a minimum current of $I_{Z(\text{min})} = 5\,\text{mA}$ (for the 1N4733) to stay in regulation, and a maximum of $I_{Z(\text{max})} = 50\,\text{mA}$ (1 W rating). The unregulated input varies between $V_{in(\text{min})} = 16\,\text{V}$ and $V_{in(\text{max})} = 20\,\text{V}$.

At the *worst-case minimum* input, the Zener current must still be $\ge 5\,\text{mA}$:

$$R_Z(\text{max}) = \dfrac{V_{in(\text{min})} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\text{min})}} = \dfrac{16 - 5.1}{5\,\text{mA}} = \dfrac{10.9}{0.005} = 2180\,\Omega$$

At the *worst-case maximum* input, the Zener current must not exceed $50\,\text{mA}$:

$$R_Z(\text{min}) = \dfrac{V_{in(\text{max})} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\text{max})}} = \dfrac{20 - 5.1}{50\,\text{mA}} = \dfrac{14.9}{0.05} = 298\,\Omega$$

Therefore, the design window is $298\,\Omega \le R_Z \le 2180\,\Omega$. Choose the midpoint for safety margin: **$R_Z = 680\,\Omega$**, $0.5\,\text{W}$.

Check Zener current at nominal $V_{in} = 18\,\text{V}$:

$$I_Z = \dfrac{18 - 5.1}{680} = \dfrac{12.9}{680} = 18.97\,\text{mA} \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Verify the Pass-Transistor Power Dissipation and Dropout

The pass transistor (2N3055, TO-3 metal can) has $V_{CE(\text{max})} = 60\,\text{V}$, $I_C(\text{max}) = 15\,\text{A}$, and $P_D(\text{max})} = 115\,\text{W}$ (with infinite heatsink).

Maximum steady-state dissipation (at $V_{in}=20\,\text{V}$, $I_L=100\,\text{mA}$):

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L = (20 - 12) \times 0.1 = 0.8\,\text{W}$$

This is well within the 2N3055's free-air rating ($\sim 1\,\text{W}$), so a small clip-on heatsink or TO-3 mica insulator is sufficient.

Dropout check: $V_{\text{drop}} = V_{in} - V_o = 20 - 12 = 8\,\text{V}$ at the worst case, and $18 - 12 = 6\,\text{V}$ nominal. Both are $\gg V_{CE(\text{sat})}(\approx 1\,\text{V})$, so the pass transistor is in the *active* (linear) region. The regulator will not dropout.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Design the Short-Circuit Protection (SCP) Network

Design target: $I_{SC} = 250\,\text{mA}$ (the current at which the SCP activates).

The sense resistor $R_S$ is placed in series with the load (or in the emitter of the pass transistor). When $V_{R_S} = I_L \cdot R_S$ reaches $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ of the protection transistor Q2, Q2 turns on and pinches off the base drive of the pass transistor.

Solve for $R_S$:

$$R_S = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{SC}} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.25} = 2.8\,\Omega$$

Choose the nearest standard value: **$R_S = 2.7\,\Omega$** (or a $2.2\,\Omega$ + $1\,\Omega$ series combination). The wattage rating must be:

$$P_{R_S} = I_{SC}^2 \cdot R_S = (0.25)^2 \times 2.7 = 0.169\,\text{W}$$

A **$0.5\,\text{W}$ or $1\,\text{W}$ resistor** is comfortable.

Recompute the actual short-circuit current with the chosen $R_S$:

$$I_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{2.7} = 0.259\,\text{A} = 259\,\text{mA} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Foldback vs. Constant-Current Limit**: The design above is a **constant-current limit** — once the output current reaches $259\,\text{mA}$, it is held there irrespective of load resistance (including a hard short). A more advanced *foldback* limit uses two resistors on Q2 to make the limit current *decrease* as $V_o$ collapses, which protects the pass transistor even more aggressively. The KTU syllabus typically requires only the constant-current version.

## 3.7 Step 6 — Final Component List (Bill of Materials)

| Reference | Part | Value / Type | Quantity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Pass transistor | 2N3055 (NPN, TO-3) | 1 | Heatsink mandatory if $P_D > 1\,\text{W}$ |
| Q2 | SCP transistor | BC547 (NPN, TO-92) | 1 | Small-signal |
| D1 | Zener reference | 1N4733, $5.1\,\text{V}$, $1\,\text{W}$ | 1 | |
| $R_1$ | Upper feedback | $13.3\,\text{k}\Omega$, $1\%$, $0.25\,\text{W}$ | 1 | Or $13\,\text{k}\Omega$ + $2\,\text{k}\Omega$ trim |
| $R_2$ | Lower feedback | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $1\%$, $0.25\,\text{W}$ | 1 | |
| $R_Z$ | Zener bias | $680\,\Omega$, $0.5\,\text{W}$ | 1 | |
| $R_S$ | SCP sense | $2.7\,\Omega$, $1\,\text{W}$ | 1 | |
| U1 | Op-amp | $\mu$A741 or LM741 | 1 | Single-supply, $\pm 18\,\text{V}$ rails |
| C_in | Input filter | $1000\,\mu\text{F} / 25\,\text{V}$ electrolytic | 1 | Reduces ripple on $V_{in}$ |
| C_out | Output filter | $100\,\mu\text{F} / 25\,\text{V}$ electrolytic + $0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ ceramic | 1 each | Improves transient response |
| Heatsink | Thermal | TO-3 clip-on | 1 | For 2N3055 |

## 3.8 Step 7 — Plot the Load Regulation Characteristic (Theory)

To plot the **load regulation** experimentally, hold $V_{in} = 18\,\text{V}$ constant and step the load current $I_L$ from $0$ (no-load) to $100\,\text{mA}$ (full-load) in, say, $10\,\text{mA}$ increments. Measure $V_o$ at each step. The expected curve is:

- At $I_L = 0$ (no-load), $V_o$ is *slightly higher* than nominal because the op-amp output sits higher and the pass transistor $V_{CE}$ is at its minimum.
- As $I_L$ increases, $V_o$ *droops* (decreases) almost linearly because the feedback loop has finite open-loop gain (a perfect op-amp with $A_{OL} = \infty$ would give a perfectly flat line).
- The slope of the line is the **load regulation**, typically $-1$ to $-10\,\text{mV}$ per $100\,\text{mA}$ for a discrete 741-based design.

The expected numerical table is:

| $I_L$ (mA) | $V_o$ (V) — without SCP | $V_o$ (V) — with SCP |
|---|---|---|
| 0  | 12.05 | 12.05 |
| 10 | 12.04 | 12.04 |
| 20 | 12.03 | 12.03 |
| 50 | 12.02 | 12.02 |
| 100 | 12.00 | 12.00 |
| 200 | 11.95 | 11.95 |
| 259 | — | 11.50 (limit engages) |
| 500 | (would burn Q1) | $\sim 6$ (Q2 saturates) |

The expected plot is a **gently downward-sloping straight line** that *bends sharply* downward once $I_L$ exceeds the SCP threshold when SCP is enabled.

## 3.9 Step 8 — Plot the Line Regulation Characteristic (Theory)

To plot the **line regulation** experimentally, hold $I_L = 100\,\text{mA}$ constant (using an electronic load or a constant-current sink) and step $V_{in}$ from $16\,\text{V}$ to $20\,\text{V}$ in $1\,\text{V}$ increments. Measure $V_o$ at each step. The expected curve is **almost a horizontal line** with a very small positive slope (on the order of $+0.1\%$ to $+1\%$ per volt):

| $V_{in}$ (V) | $V_o$ (V) at $I_L = 100\,\text{mA}$ |
|---|---|
| 16 | 11.97 |
| 17 | 11.99 |
| 18 | 12.00 |
| 19 | 12.01 |
| 20 | 12.02 |

The slope of this line is the **line regulation**, typically $10$ to $50\,\text{mV/V}$ for a discrete 741-based design. The line regulation with SCP is essentially identical, because the SCP network is *inactive* in the normal load range.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`srcA`, `zenerA`, `opaA`, `passA`, `scpA`, `loadA`, `meterA`, `meterB`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- All node labels are quoted, with **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** only (no bold or italics markers inside labels).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Series Regulator with SCP

The following Mermaid `flowchart` represents the **block-level functional architecture** of the complete circuit, showing how the unregulated input is converted to a regulated, short-circuit-protected output. This is the standard KTU block diagram students are expected to draw in their records.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UNREG["UNREGULATED DC INPUT  VIN EQUALS 16 TO 20 VOLT"]
    FILTIN["INPUT FILTER  C_IN EQUALS 1000 MICROFARAD"]
    REF["ZENER REFERENCE  VZ EQUALS 5.1 VOLT  1N4733"]
    BIASR["BIAS RESISTOR  RZ EQUALS 680 OHM"]
    FBR1["FEEDBACK DIVIDER R1 EQUALS 13.3 KILOOHM"]
    FBR2["FEEDBACK DIVIDER R2 EQUALS 10 KILOOHM"]
    OPA["ERROR AMPLIFIER  LM741  OP-AMP"]
    PASS["PASS TRANSISTOR  Q1 EQUALS 2N3055  NPN"]
    SENSE["SENSE RESISTOR  RS EQUALS 2.7 OHM"]
    SCPN["SCP TRANSISTOR  Q2 EQUALS BC547  NPN"]
    FILTOUT["OUTPUT FILTER  C_OUT EQUALS 100 MICROFARAD"]
    LOAD["ELECTRONIC LOAD  RL VARIABLE  0 TO 1 KILOOHM"]
    METER1["VOLTMETER  VO MONITOR"]
    METER2["AMMETER  IL MONITOR"]
    METER3["VOLTMETER  VIN MONITOR"]

    UNREG --> FILTIN
    FILTIN --> REF
    FILTIN --> BIASR
    BIASR --> REF
    REF --> OPA
    OPA --> PASS
    PASS --> SENSE
    SENSE --> LOAD
    SENSE --> SCPN
    SCPN --> OPA
    FBR1 --> LOAD
    FBR2 --> LOAD
    OPA --> FBR1
    OPA --> FBR2
    PASS --> FILTOUT
    FILTOUT --> LOAD
    LOAD --> METER1
    LOAD --> METER2
    UNREG --> METER3
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- The **unregulated DC input** ($V_{in}$) feeds both the **Zener reference network** (top branch) and the **pass-transistor collector** (right branch).
- The **op-amp** acts as the error amplifier: its *non-inverting input* is held at $V_Z$, and its *inverting input* is fed from the **feedback divider** ($R_1$, $R_2$).
- The op-amp output drives the **base of the pass transistor Q1**.
- The **emitter of Q1** (which is $V_o$) feeds the load and the **sense resistor $R_S$**.
- The voltage across $R_S$ controls the **SCP transistor Q2**: when $V_{R_S} > 0.7\,\text{V}$, Q2 turns on and *diverts* the op-amp's base drive away from Q1, limiting the current.
- The three meters $M_1, M_2, M_3$ are the KTU lab instruments used to record data for the regulation plots.

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram — Closed-Loop Feedback Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SETP["SET POINT  VZ EQUALS 5.1 VOLT"]
    ADDER["SUMMING JUNCTION  AT OP-AMP INVERTING INPUT"]
    AMP["ERROR AMPLIFIER  GAIN A_OL  TYPICALLY 200000"]
    MOD["PASS TRANSISTOR  GAIN APPROX 1  EMITTER FOLLOWER"]
    OUT["OUTPUT  VO EQUALS 12 VOLT"]
    FBK["FEEDBACK NETWORK  BETA EQUALS R2 OVER R1 PLUS R2"]
    LOADVAR["LOAD VARIATION  DELTA IL"]

    SETP --> ADDER
    ADDER --> AMP
    AMP --> MOD
    MOD --> OUT
    OUT --> FBK
    FBK --> ADDER
    LOADVAR -.-> OUT
```

**Reading the signal-flow diagram:**

- The **set-point** ($V_Z$) and the **scaled feedback** ($\beta V_o$) are subtracted at the **summing junction** to produce the **error voltage** $\epsilon = V_Z - \beta V_o$.
- This error is amplified by the op-amp's open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ and applied to the pass transistor (which acts as a unity-gain buffer — the *emitter follower*).
- The closed-loop gain is $V_o / V_Z = 1/\beta$, which is exactly the feedback formula $V_o = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$ derived in Section 3.
- The closed-loop output impedance drops by a factor of $A_{OL} \beta$ compared to the open-loop pass transistor, which is the *fundamental reason* a feedback regulator has such good load regulation.

## 4.4 Topology Comparison Block Diagram (with vs. without SCP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BLOCKA["BASIC SERIES REGULATOR  NO PROTECTION"]
        A1["UNREG VIN"] --> A2["PASS Q1  2N3055"]
        A2 --> A3["LOAD RL"]
        A4["ZENER VZ"] --> A2
        A5["FEEDBACK R1 R2"] --> A2
    end
    subgraph BLOCKB["SERIES REGULATOR  WITH SHORT CIRCUIT PROTECTION"]
        B1["UNREG VIN"] --> B2["PASS Q1  2N3055"]
        B2 --> B3["SENSE RS  2.7 OHM"]
        B3 --> B4["LOAD RL"]
        B5["ZENER VZ"] --> B6["OP-AMP  LM741"]
        B6 --> B2
        B7["FEEDBACK R1 R2"] --> B6
        B8["PROTECT Q2  BC547"] --> B6
        B3 --> B8
    end
    BLOCKA ~~~ BLOCKB
```

The right-hand block (BLOCKB) has the **extra feedback path** through the **protection transistor Q2**, which is absent in BLOCKA. This is the only structural difference between the two versions of the experiment.
