# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term *load regulation* of a DC voltage regulator. Mention its ideal value and the units in which it is expressed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Load regulation is defined as the change in regulated output voltage $V_o$ per unit change in load current $I_L$, with the unregulated input voltage held constant. Mathematically:

$$\text{Load Regulation} = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{V_{in}=\text{const}}$$

It is expressed in **mV/A** or as a **percentage** of the nominal output voltage. The **ideal value is zero** (a perfect regulator maintains constant $V_o$ regardless of $I_L$). For a well-designed discrete 741-based series regulator, typical values are $1$ to $10\,\text{mV/A}$.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for ideal value, 1 mark for units and typical range.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is the function of the pass transistor in a series voltage regulator? Why is it operated in the *active* region and not in *saturation*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pass transistor is the *series* control element that modulates its own collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ to absorb the difference between the unregulated input and the regulated output. By varying its $V_{CE}$ in response to the feedback loop, it acts as a *variable resistor* that keeps $V_o$ constant.

It is operated in the **active (linear) region** because the regulator is a *linear* regulator: the transistor must remain capable of *continuous* variation of $V_{CE}$ in response to small changes in $V_{in}$ or $I_L$. In **saturation**, $V_{CE}$ collapses to $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$ and the device can no longer regulate — it becomes a closed switch, defeating the whole purpose. Operating in saturation also causes the dropout voltage to fall below the minimum required to keep $V_o$ at the design value.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for function, 1 mark for active region justification, 1 mark for saturation disadvantage.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students most commonly lose marks on this experiment for the following reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting to include $V_{BE}$ in the output formula** when using a Darlington pass transistor (it is $2 V_{BE} \approx 1.4\,\text{V}$, not $0.7\,\text{V}$).
> 2. **Incorrectly sizing $R_Z$** — picking a value that violates the $I_{Z(\text{min})}$ constraint at low line, which causes the Zener to drop out of regulation and the output to follow the input.
> 3. **Confusing load and line regulation** in the final graph axes — load regulation has $I_L$ on the x-axis, line regulation has $V_{in}$ on the x-axis. Reversing these costs full marks for the graph.
> 4. **Drawing the SCP transistor backwards** — emitter and collector are interchanged, and the SCP fails to engage in the lab.
> 5. **Omitting the heatsink calculation** for the pass transistor — examiners expect an explicit $P_D = (V_{in}-V_o) I_L$ computation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]

**(a)** Design a transistor series voltage regulator using an op-amp error amplifier (LM741) and a 2N3055 pass transistor to deliver **$V_o = +9\,\text{V}$** at **$I_L = 150\,\text{mA}$** from an unregulated input of **$V_{in} = 15 \pm 2\,\text{V}$**. Use a Zener reference of **$V_Z = 5.1\,\text{V}$** (1N4733). Determine the values of the feedback resistors $R_1, R_2$, the Zener bias resistor $R_Z$, and the pass-transistor power dissipation $P_D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Add a short-circuit protection network to the regulator of part (a) using a BC547 protection transistor and a sense resistor. Design the sense resistor value such that the short-circuit current limit is **$I_{SC} = 300\,\text{mA}$**. Also plot (qualitatively) the **load regulation** and **line regulation** characteristics of the regulator and label the axes, slopes, and the SCP engagement point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Design Without SCP (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Feedback resistor ratio (2 marks):**
Using the governing relation

$$V_o = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

with $V_o = 9\,\text{V}$ and $V_Z = 5.1\,\text{V}$:

$$1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{9}{5.1} = 1.7647$$

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = 0.7647$$

Choose $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (1%, standard). Then:

$$R_1 = 0.7647 \times 10 = 7.647\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Nearest E96 standard: **$R_1 = 7.68\,\text{k}\Omega$** (or $7.5\,\text{k}\Omega$ + small trim).

**Step 2 — Zener bias resistor (2 marks):**
The unregulated input varies between $V_{in(\text{min})} = 13\,\text{V}$ and $V_{in(\text{max})} = 17\,\text{V}$. Take $I_{Z(\text{min})} = 5\,\text{mA}$ and $I_{Z(\text{max})} = 50\,\text{mA}$ for the 1W 1N4733.

$$R_{Z(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{in(\text{min})} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\text{min})}} = \dfrac{13 - 5.1}{0.005} = 1580\,\Omega$$

$$R_{Z(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{in(\text{max})} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\text{max})}} = \dfrac{17 - 5.1}{0.05} = 238\,\Omega$$

Design window: $238\,\Omega \le R_Z \le 1580\,\Omega$. Choose **$R_Z = 470\,\Omega$, $0.5\,\text{W}$**.

Check $I_Z$ at nominal $V_{in} = 15\,\text{V}$:

$$I_Z = \dfrac{15 - 5.1}{470} = \dfrac{9.9}{470} = 21.06\,\text{mA} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Pass-transistor power dissipation (2 marks):**

$$P_D = (V_{in(\text{max})} - V_o) \cdot I_L = (17 - 9) \times 0.15 = 1.2\,\text{W}$$

A small TO-3 heatsink (thermal resistance $\le 15^\circ\text{C/W}$) is required to keep the junction temperature below $100^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 4 — Dropout check (1 mark):**
At the lowest input, $V_{in(\text{min})} - V_o = 13 - 9 = 4\,\text{V}$, which is $\gg V_{CE(\text{sat})}$. The regulator does *not* dropout at the lowest specified line voltage.

##### Part (b) — Design With SCP and Regulation Plots (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sense resistor for SCP (3 marks):**
Design target: $I_{SC} = 300\,\text{mA}$. Use the governing relation:

$$R_S = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{SC}} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.3} = 2.33\,\Omega$$

Nearest standard: **$R_S = 2.2\,\Omega$, $1\,\text{W}$**.

Recompute actual limit: $I_{SC} = 0.7 / 2.2 = 318\,\text{mA}$.

Power in sense resistor at limit: $P_{R_S} = I_{SC}^2 R_S = 0.318^2 \times 2.2 = 0.222\,\text{W}$. A $1\,\text{W}$ resistor has ample margin.

**Step 2 — Load regulation plot description (2 marks):**
A load regulation plot has $I_L$ (mA) on the **x-axis** and $V_o$ (V) on the **y-axis**. The expected curve is a **gently downward-sloping straight line** of slope approximately $-1$ to $-5\,\text{mV}$ per $100\,\text{mA}$. With SCP enabled, the line **bends sharply downward** at $I_L = 318\,\text{mA}$ and *flattens* at a constant $V_o \approx 4$ to $5\,\text{V}$ as the SCP transistor takes over control.

**Step 3 — Line regulation plot description (2 marks):**
A line regulation plot has $V_{in}$ (V) on the **x-axis** and $V_o$ (V) on the **y-axis**, with $I_L$ held constant (say at $100\,\text{mA}$). The expected curve is a **nearly horizontal straight line** of slope approximately $+0.5$ to $+1\,\%\text{/V}$. There is *no visible* SCP engagement because the SCP is dormant at normal load currents.

*(Note: The plots should be drawn by the student; the verbal description above is what an examiner accepts as a model answer. The student must label axes, units, and indicate the SCP threshold on the load regulation plot.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transistor series voltage regulator with short-circuit protection**. Derive the expression for the short-circuit current limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series regulator uses a pass transistor with $\beta = 50$, drives a load current of $I_L = 500\,\text{mA}$, and uses a Zener reference of $V_Z = 6.2\,\text{V}$. Calculate (i) the base current required from the op-amp, (ii) the output voltage (assume emitter-follower configuration with $V_{BE} = 0.7\,\text{V}$), and (iii) the maximum power dissipation in the pass transistor if $V_{in} = 18\,\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Circuit Description and Derivation (7 marks)

**Operation (4 marks):**
The series voltage regulator with short-circuit protection consists of a pass transistor Q1 (series element, typically a power BJT such as 2N3055 or TIP122), a Zener diode D1 providing a stable reference $V_Z$, a resistive feedback divider ($R_1$, $R_2$) sampling the output, an op-amp error amplifier comparing $V_{fb}$ to $V_Z$, and an SCP sub-circuit comprising a sense resistor $R_S$ in series with the load and a small-signal protection transistor Q2 (e.g., BC547). The op-amp continuously adjusts the base drive of Q1 such that $V_{fb} = V_Z$, which sets $V_o = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$.

The SCP sub-circuit operates as follows: under normal load, the voltage across $R_S$ ($= I_L R_S$) is less than the $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ of Q2 ($\approx 0.7\,\text{V}$), so Q2 is *off* and exerts no influence. When the load current rises (either because the user draws too much current or because of a short), $V_{R_S}$ eventually reaches $0.7\,\text{V}$, turning Q2 *on*. Once on, Q2 diverts the op-amp's base-drive current away from Q1, preventing any further rise in $I_L$ and *clamping* the output current to a safe maximum. The output voltage $V_o$ collapses to a small value in this state, and the pass transistor is protected from thermal runaway.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The short-circuit current limit $I_{SC}$ is reached when $V_{R_S} = V_{BE(\text{on})}$ of Q2:

$$I_{SC} \cdot R_S = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$

$$\boxed{I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} \approx \dfrac{0.7\,\text{V}}{R_S}}$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks)

**(i) Base current required (2 marks):**

$$I_B = \dfrac{I_L}{\beta} = \dfrac{500\,\text{mA}}{50} = 10\,\text{mA}$$

The op-amp (e.g., LM741) can source only $\sim 20\,\text{mA}$, so this is acceptable but leaves little margin. For higher $I_L$, a Darlington (with $\beta > 1000$) should be used.

**(ii) Output voltage (2 marks):**
For an emitter-follower configuration with the Zener at the base:

$$V_o = V_Z - V_{BE(\text{on})} = 6.2 - 0.7 = 5.5\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Maximum pass-transistor power dissipation (3 marks):**
At full load, with $V_{in} = 18\,\text{V}$ and $V_o = 5.5\,\text{V}$:

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L = (18 - 5.5) \times 0.5 = 12.5 \times 0.5 = 6.25\,\text{W}$$

A substantial heatsink (thermal resistance $\le 5^\circ\text{C/W}$) is mandatory. The 2N3055 (TO-3, $P_D = 115\,\text{W}$ with infinite heatsink) is comfortably rated, but with no heatsink its $P_D$ rating drops to about $1\,\text{W}$, which is insufficient.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Lab Exam**

- [ ] **Definition of *series regulator***: a regulator whose *pass element* is in **series** with the load (in contrast to a shunt regulator where the pass element is in parallel with the load).
- [ ] **Output voltage formula (feedback version):** $V_o = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$. Memorise it. The op-amp drives the pass transistor base such that $V_{fb} = V_Z$ at equilibrium.
- [ ] **Output voltage formula (emitter-follower version):** $V_o = V_Z - V_{BE(\text{on})}$. Easy to forget the *minus* sign — examiners specifically test this.
- [ ] **Dropout voltage** $V_{\text{drop}} = V_{in} - V_o$ must be $\ge V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 1\,\text{V}$ for the pass transistor to remain in the active region.
- [ ] **Zener bias resistor design** is bounded by $I_{Z(\text{min})}$ and $I_{Z(\text{max})}$. Always compute the *worst-case minimum* and *worst-case maximum* of $V_{in}$ before sizing $R_Z$.
- [ ] **Pass-transistor power dissipation** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$. Heatsink is mandatory when $P_D > 1\,\text{W}$.
- [ ] **Short-circuit protection** uses a sense resistor $R_S$ and a small-signal BJT Q2. The design equation is $I_{SC} = V_{BE(\text{on})} / R_S \approx 0.7/R_S$. Always check the *wattage* of $R_S$.
- [ ] **Load regulation** plot: $V_o$ vs $I_L$ at constant $V_{in}$. Slope is $\Delta V_o / \Delta I_L$, expressed in mV/A or %.
- [ ] **Line regulation** plot: $V_o$ vs $V_{in}$ at constant $I_L$. Slope is $\Delta V_o / \Delta V_{in}$, expressed in mV/V or %.
- [ ] **Distinguish *constant-current limit* from *foldback limit***. The KTU syllabus asks for the constant-current version.
- [ ] **Lab data-taking protocol:** always tabulate five to ten readings, plot the graph *during* the lab (not afterwards), and write the units and the operating point ($V_{in}$, nominal $V_o$, $I_L$) on the graph.
- [ ] **Top five mistakes to avoid** (from the examiner's pitfall callout above): (1) forgetting $V_{BE}$ with Darlington, (2) Zener $R_Z$ out of bounds, (3) swapped axes on regulation plots, (4) reversed SCP transistor, (5) missing heatsink calculation.
